segments.s166-g537: مرا is the dative/experiencer clitic ('to me' / topical 'for me') split here into م + را; هرچ means 'whatever' and introduces a free relative; اندر دل is a locative phrase 'in (the) heart'; اندیشه بود is a nominal clause with the past copula بود 'there was / was', so the whole line says literally 'to me whatever in heart thought was' = 'whatever thought was in my heart'. Word order is typical older Persian (topic/experiencer first, relative word before locative, predicate last).
segments.s166-g538: خرد بود is another small nominal clause 'it was wisdom' (بود = past 'was'); وز 'و + از' here functions as the coordinating preposition 'and/from' introducing the next phrase; هر دری پیشه بود literally 'from every door/quarter there was a craft'—هر modifies دری ('every gate/field'), پیشه 'craft/profession', and the clause ends again with بود. The two clauses are linked by وز to give a paired statement.
segments.s166-g539: همان is a demonstrative/topic 'that very/the same'; آزمایش بد is a noun phrase 'bad/harsh trial' (آزمایش = ordeal/test, بد = bad/ill); از روزگار is a prepositional/genitive phrase 'from fate/the times' where از indicates source. Note that classical Persian often omits an explicit copula, so this phrase functions as a nominal clause describing the event.
segments.s166-g540: ازین = از + این 'from this' (the dataset splits the parts); کینه‌ور is a compound: کینه 'grudge/hatred' + ور (an element meaning 'bearing/possessing' though the provided senses don't list this suffix explicitly) => 'grudge‑bearing' or 'resentful'; تیزدل 'sharp‑hearted' (an adjective/compound) modifies شهریار 'king'. Together the phrase marks the agent/source: 'from this resentful, quick‑hearted king.'
segments.s166-g541: همه 'all/everyone' is the object/topic; پیش تو 'in your presence / before you' places the action; یک به یک is an adverbial phrase 'one by one'; راندم is the past-tense first-person verb 'I drove/put to flight/led' with the subject implied ('I'). Word order places objects/adjuncts before the verb, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s166-g542: چو is a comparative/temporal particle 'as / when / like'; خورشید تابنده is a noun + participial adjective 'the shining sun' (تابنده = 'shining, resplendent'); برخواندم is a prefixed past verb ('بر' + خواندم) meaning here 'I summoned / I called forth' or 'I brought up/placed' — the prefix بر gives a sense of 'up/on/forward' added to خواندم. The clause is a simile: 'I summoned (them) like the shining sun.'
segments.s166-g543: به ایران is a directional/dative phrase 'into/to Iran'; پدر را uses the definite object marker را to mark 'the father' as the direct object; بینداختی is the past second‑person singular verb 'you threw / you cast (away)' (بی‑انداختی = you caused to be cast). The clause thus says 'you cast the father into Iran.'
segments.s166-g544: به توران 'to/in Turan' sets the location; همی is an archaic/progressive particle often used in epic Persian to mark continuous or habitual aspect (here giving a simple past/continuous flavor to the verb that follows); شارستان 'city/urban quarter' is the object; ساختی is the past second‑person singular 'you built / you made'. Together: 'and (in) Turan you built a city.'
segments.s166-g561: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. گفتار و کردار is a coordinated noun phrase 'speech and deed'. بر پیش و پس attaches a locative/temporal framing: بر + پیش و پس = 'with regard to, in (the) before and after' (i.e. 'before or after'); word order (preposition + nouns) reflects Classical Persian style.
segments.s166-g562: ز من = 'from me' (preposition + pronoun). هیچ ... کس forms the negative quantifier 'no one' (lit. 'no ... person'). ناخوب is the adjective 'bad', and نشنید is the past-tense verb 'heard' (3rd person); the verb follows the negated quantifier phrase, so the whole means 'no one heard anything bad from me.'
segments.s166-g563: چو = 'when'. گستاخ شد = 'became bold/impudent' (شد = became). دست با گنج او literally 'his hand with his treasure' — دست = 'hand' (here figurative for power/action), با = 'with', گنج او = 'his treasure'; the clause means 'when his hand grew bold with his treasure' (i.e. emboldened by wealth).
segments.s166-g564: بپیچید is a past-form verb 'twisted' (3rd singular) with the prefix ب- typical in classical usage; همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily/indeed'. تن = 'body', از رنج او = 'from his suffering' (از = 'from/because of', رنج = 'suffering', او = 'his'). The clause states the physical effect: 'his body twisted from his suffering.'
segments.s166-g565: اگرچه = 'although / even if' introducing a concessive clause. بد آید همی بر سرم = 'bad (misfortune) may come upon my head' — آید is present/subjunctive 'come/happen', همی is a classical continuous/emphatic particle, بر سرم is the idiom 'upon me' (lit. 'on my head', meaning 'befall me').
segments.s166-g566: هم از رای و فرمان او نگذرم = 'I will not depart even from his counsel and command.' هم here adds emphasis ('even'). از introduces the domain 'from/regarding' رای و فرمان او = 'his counsel and order/command'. نگذرم is the negative present/subjunctive/first-person verb 'I do not pass over / I will not omit / I will not disregard' (i.e. 'I will not disobey/abandon').
segments.s166-g567: بیابم is a classical subjunctive/jussive form of 'to find' ('let me find' / 'I should find'); here it functions as an intention 'I will find'. برش is the fused form of بر + -ش (the clitic 'him') meaning 'to/at him' — the lemma given in the word list doesn't reflect this enclitic usage, so its dictionary senses don't fit here. هم کنون = 'right now' (هم intensifier + کنون 'now'). بی‌سپاه = 'without an army/defenceless'. Together: 'I will find him now, unescorted.'
segments.s166-g568: ببینم = 'let me see / I will see' (subjunctive/first-person). که introduces the indirect question 'what' / 'why'. از here means 'because of / from' marking the cause. چی ست = 'what is', with ست the copula 'is'. آزار شاه = 'the king's distress/harm'. The clause is an indirect question: 'let me see what is the king's distress (what is afflicting the king).'
segments.s166-g249: چهارم is the ordinal 'fourth' introducing the episode. چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' (not simile); گرسیوز is the proper name subject; آمد is the past verb 'came'. بدر is another proper name/place that functions as the goal ("came to Badr"). The clause is a temporal clause that sets the scene for what follows.
segments.s166-g250: This is a descriptive nominal phrase: کله بر سر 'a head (headpiece) on (his) head' uses بر + noun to mark location ('on'). و links the two coordinated descriptors. تنگ بسته کمر is a reduced adjectival phrase: تنگ 'tight/narrow' modifies بسته (past-participle used adjectivally 'fastened/tied'), and کمر (here 'waist') is the noun being modified. Note Persian permits participial adjectives after the noun and places the locative/possessive structure compactly.
segments.s166-g251: سپهدار ترکان is a compound meaning 'commander of the Turks' (poetic style often omits the ezāfe). ورا is the clitic phrase 'to him' (i.e., 'him' as the patient/goal). پیش خواند is a single verbal complex 'called/summoned forward' (خواند = summoned/called); word order is subject–object–verb, typical for Persian narration.
segments.s166-g252: ز is the poetic preposition 'because of / from'; کار سیاوش is a genitive construction 'Siavash's affair/deeds' where کار means 'deed/affair'; فراوان here intensifies ('very much' / 'many'); براند is a narrative verb meaning 'he drove away' (Ferdowsi often uses such forms in narration — translated here as simple past). The whole clause gives the reason/motive for the action.
segments.s166-g253: بدو is the prepositional clitic 'to him' (introducing the indirect object of گفت). گفت here functions as the verb 'said' (despite the provided senses list emphasizing 'conversation', in context it is the speech verb). کای is the archaic/vocative 'O' addressing the person; یادگار پشنگ is a vocative phrase 'remnant/offspring (or token) of Pashang'—an epithet identifying the addressee.
segments.s166-g254: This is a direct question. چه 'what' is the interrogative; دارم is 'I have' (the first-person present used here for general possession); به گیتی is a locative phrase 'in the world' (به + noun). جز از تو 'except from you' gives the exception; به چنگ is an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'in (my) grasp' or 'within reach' (به used instrumentally/locatively plus چنگ 'grip/claw'). Note the subject 'I' is implicit in the verb دارم.
segments.s166-g255: همه رازها is the subject 'all secrets' (plural). بر تو marks the beneficiary/recipient 'to you.' باید is the modal 'must/ought to,' and گشاد is an adjectival/predicative past-participle 'open/revealed.' The structure is modal + predicate: 'all secrets must be opened to you.'
segments.s166-g256: به ژرفی ببین is an imperative 'look deeply' (ببین = see/look) with به ژرفی 'in/with depth' as an adverbial phrase. تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that'; چه آیدت یاد is a subordinate clause 'what remembrance/memory comes to you' where آید is the verb 'comes' and the enclitic ت on آیدt marks 'to you' (object/experiencer).
segments.s166-g161: بدو (be-do) is the prepositional clitic phrase 'to him' placed before the verb; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (3rd sg.), and گرسیوز‌ای is the name Garsivaz in vocative with the suffix -ای used to address someone directly; نامدار is an adjective 'renowned' placed after the name for poetic emphasis.
segments.s166-g162: This clause literally reads 'of the Turks an army to him is not ally': ز ترکان لشکر = 'an army of the Turks' (ز = 'of/from' marking source), ورا = 'to him/for him', نیست = 'is not' (existential/negative copula), and یار = 'ally/companion' as the predicate. The order is poetic but the meaning is 'no Turkish army is on his side.'
segments.s166-g163: سیاوش is the subject name. بدو 'to him' again precedes گفت 'said' (past). کز is the conjunction 'کز' (poetic variant of که) here meaning 'because of/for that', تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you', and گذشت (past of گذشتن) is used here in the sense 'passed/caused to pass' or 'because you have acted/left' — contextually 'because of you'.
segments.s166-g164: نبرد دلیران is a genitive phrase 'the battle/fighting of the brave ones'. مرا is the object clitic 'me' (م + را), خوار is an adjective 'despised/humbled', and گشت is the past-tense copular verb 'became'. Together 'نبرد دلیران مرا خوار گشت' = 'the warriors' fighting made me despised' or 'I was disgraced by the battle of the brave.'
segments.s166-g165: ازیشان (از + ایشان) is a prepositional/genitive phrase 'from them' placed before the noun for emphasis. دو یل is the counted noun phrase 'two heroes'. باید is a modal 'must/ought' that governs the following predicate; آراسته is a past/participial adjective 'equipped/arrayed' used predicatively — the copula ('باشند' or similar) is omitted (poetic ellipsis), so the clause means 'two heroes from them must be (made) equipped.'
segments.s166-g166: به میدان نبرد is a prepositional phrase 'to/into the field of battle' (به + میدان + نبرد). مرا خواسته is a verbal phrase built from the object pronoun مرا ('me') plus خواسته, the past participle/perfect of خواستن used here like 'has asked/desired' — the subject is omitted and must be recovered from context. Word order (prepositional phrase fronted, then object + participle) is typical of poetic narrative.
segments.s166-g167: یکی نامور بود is an existential/introducing clause 'one (a certain one) was renowned' where یکی = 'a certain' and بود is the past copula 'was'. نامش دمور is an appositive/identifying phrase: نام + enclitic ش ('his name') followed by the proper name دمور (here used as a name), i.e. 'his name was Damur.'
segments.s166-g168: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. همتا نبودش is 'had no equal' formed from همتا 'peer/equal' + نبود (negative past of بودن) with enclitic -ش referring back to the hero ('to him there was no equal'). به ترکان به زور is a compact idiom meaning 'among the Turks in (regard to) strength' — the repeated به marks the relation ('with respect to the Turks' and 'in strength').
segments.s166-g577: یکی is an indefinite pronoun meaning 'one'/'someone' placed before the noun; پاسخ‌نامه is the direct object 'a reply/answer-letter'. باید is a modal particle 'must/ought to' and نوشت is the bare infinitive 'to write' (classical Persian often omits an explicit auxiliary). Together they form 'one must write (a reply)'.
segments.s166-g578: پدیدار کردن is a verbal phrase meaning 'to make manifest / to reveal' (پدیدار as 'manifest' + کردن 'to make'). همه خوب و زشت is the object: 'all the good and the ugly/bad.' This infinitival phrase specifies the content/purpose of the reply mentioned in the previous segment.
segments.s166-g579: ز is the poetic preposition 'from / because of' introducing the cause (کین‌گر, 'the vindictive or hating man'). ببینم is first-person present ('I see' — classical style without می-), and سر او تهی is a predicate clause 'his head (سرِ او) is empty' with تهی as predicate adjective.
segments.s166-g580: درخشان شود is an adjectival predicate + verb 'become (shining)' where شود is a jussive/optative 'may become' or simple present 'becomes' in poetry. روزگار بهی (literally 'good time/age') is the subject placed after the verb for emphasis: 'may the good time grow radiant.'
segments.s166-g581: سواری is 'a rider/horseman' (direct object/what is sent). فرستم is first-person present/future 'I send / I will send.' به نزدیک تو is a prepositional phrase 'to your near[ness] / by your side', indicating destination.
segments.s166-g582: درفشان کن(م) is a light-verb construction where درفشان (literally 'shining, waving [a standard]') combines with کنم 'I will make/do' to mean 'I will make (it) shine / I will display/scatter (it)'. رای تاریک تو 'your dark counsel/plan' is the object: 'I will make your dark counsel shine/exposed.' کنم is first-person present used here as future/intention.
segments.s166-g583: امیدستم is past first-person 'I hoped/expected.' از marks the source/recipient of that hope ('from'), and کردگار جهان is a genitive sequence 'the Maker/Creator of the world.' Together: 'I hoped from the Creator of the world.'
segments.s166-g584: شناسنده is an active participle 'knower' qualifying the Creator; آشکار و نهان are the two coordinated adjectives 'manifest and hidden.' This short appositive names God's attributive knowledge: 'the knower of the manifest and the hidden.'
segments.s166-g529: بدان is formed from the prefix ب- + دان and here functions as an imperative 'know' or 'be aware'. تا introduces the condition (‘if/while’ in older Persian) and is followed by تو گستاخ باشی: تو is the 2nd-person pronoun and گستاخ is the predicate adjective 'insolent'; باشی is the present-subjunctive/optative form of بودن ('be') used after تا to state the condition. بدوی here is an adjective meaning 'primitive/uncultured' (used with گستاخ to describe the person).
segments.s166-g530: فروماند is a past/resultative adjective/participle meaning 'left/abandoned' or 'left behind'. اندر is the preposition 'in/within' and جهان is 'the world'; گفت‌وگوی is the noun 'conversation/talk' in the genitive construct ('the talk of the world'). Together they say one will be 'left behind in the world's talk'.
segments.s166-g531: ترا is the topical/object form 'to/for you' (ت + را); هم adds 'also/too'. ز introduces a comparison 'than/from' and اغریرث is a poetic name (a flower) used as the comparison term. ارجمند is the predicate adjective 'esteemed/precious' that applies to the preceding phrase.
segments.s166-g532: فزون نیست is a negated copular phrase: فزون 'more/increased' and نیست 'is not'—'there is no increase / it is not greater'. خویشی، پیوند و بند are a coordinated list 'kinship, bond and tie' that are the items said to have no greater amount or effect (i.e. they add no increase).
segments.s166-g533: میانش is میانی/میان + enclitic -ش 'in his/its midst' or 'between them' identifying location or the person affected. به خنجر is instrumental 'with a dagger'. بدو is the dative 'to him' (marking the person on whom the action is done). نیم is the object 'half', and کرد is the past tense 'did/made'—together 'cut him in half with a dagger'.
segments.s166-g534: سپه را is the definite object 'the army' (را marks the direct object/topic). به کردار او uses به + noun + pronoun to indicate means or manner 'by his conduct/behavior'. بیم کرد is a causative/perfective sequence 'caused fear' or 'made (them) fearful'—so 'he made the army fearful by his conduct'.
segments.s166-g535: نهانش is نهان 'hidden/secret' with the 3rd-person enclitic -ش 'him/it' — 'him in secret'. ببین is the imperative 'see/observe'. آشکارا is an adverb 'openly/evidently' and کنون is the temporal adverb 'now' — the sequence gives two imperatives: look at him secretly, then make/expose him openly now.
segments.s166-g536: چنین دان is an imperative clause 'know this/know thus' (چنین 'thus/this way' + دان 'know'). و ایمن مشو زو به خون is a prohibition: و 'and', ایمن 'safe/secure', مشو 'do not become' (negative imperative), زو 'from him' (ز + او), به خون 'by/with blood' — 'do not feel secure from him because of blood' (i.e. do not trust him on account of blood ties).
segments.s166-g57: نوازنده رود is a noun phrase in which نوازنده (player/musician) is linked to رود by a genitive/qualifying relation: 'player of the rūd' (rūd = a stringed instrument). با + noun is a simple comitative prepositional phrase 'with the cupbearer' that attaches to the subject before the verb.
segments.s166-g58: بیامد is an archaic/poetic past form 'he came' (3sg). بر تخت is a preposition + noun indicating motion/position 'to/onto the throne', and گوهرنگار is a post‑nominal adjective 'jeweled' modifying تخت; the whole phrase is the locative/complement of the verb.
segments.s166-g59: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/by' introducing the source or means. نالیدن is used as a verbal noun/gerund 'the sounding/lamenting' (poetic usage for the making of music), followed by a coordinated list of instruments/arts (چنگ و رود و سرود) which are the items producing that sound.
segments.s166-g60: به شادی is a prepositional phrase of manner 'with/in joy'. همی + داد is an archaic/perfective cluster where همی gives an iterative/continuous flavor to the past verb داد 'gave'. دل را is the definite object marked by را, and درود is the object 'greeting/blessing' that was given to the heart.
segments.s166-g61: چو functions as a temporal conjunction 'when'. خورشید تابنده is a noun + postpositive adjective 'the shining sun'. بگشاد is an archaic past verb (ب‑ + root) meaning 'opened/revealed', taking راز ('secret') as its object.
segments.s166-g62: به هرجای is a directional prepositional phrase 'to/throughout every place' (هرجای = 'wherever/every place'). بنمود is a past verb 'displayed/showed' and چهر ('face/appearance') is its object; از فراز is an origin/locative phrase 'from on high' placed after the object for poetic emphasis.
segments.s166-g63: سیاوش is the subject. ز ایوان is an ablative/ablative‑like phrase 'from the iwan/portico', and به میدان گذشت is a verb phrase 'passed to/into the field' where به indicates motion and گذشت is the past tense 'passed/entered'.
segments.s166-g64: به بازی is a manner phrase 'in play / as a game'. همی marks a customary or iterative past, and گرد میدان بگشت is a verb cluster meaning 'went about/rounded the field' where گرد functions adverbially 'around' and بگشت is an archaic past of گشتن 'to go/turn' (here: 'kept circling').
segments.s166-g217: همی is an older/prose/poetic imperfective marker here (marks a continuing or habitual action: “kept/was going”). رفت is the past form of رفتن “to go/leave”; together همی رفت = “he went off / he went on his way” with a sense of motion in progress. یکدل is an adjectival/compound epithet (“single‑hearted, resolute”) modifying the (omitted) subject; پر + از is the idiomatic construction “full of X” and takes the following nouns (کین و درد) as the content. The subject is omitted (supplied from context), so the clause literally reads “(he) went, (being) single‑hearted, full of hatred and sorrow.”
segments.s166-g218: بدانگه is a temporal adverb “at that moment/thereupon.” که introduces the subordinate clause (here functioning roughly as “when/that”), and خورشید شد لاژورد is a simple predicative clause: شد is the past of شدن “to become,” with لاژورد (lapis/azure) as the predicate noun describing خورشید. The whole segment gives a time frame: “at that moment when the sun became lapis‑blue.”
segments.s166-g219: همه شب is a time expression “all night / the whole night.” بپیچید (verb پیچیدن with the b‑ prefix used in classical/poetic narration) here means “tossed/turned” or “twisted about” (in sleep or restless thought) and is past tense. تا introduces the limit “until,” and روز پاک (“the clear/pure day”) is the temporal goal reached. Word order places the time phrase before the verb, typical in Persian.
segments.s166-g220: چو is the conjunction “when/as.” شب is the subject (here personified). جامه قیرگون is a noun phrase “the pitch‑black garment/robe” and کرد + چاک is a light verb construction: کرد (make/do) + چاک (a tear/gash) = “made a rent / rent (its) garment.” The phrase is a poetic personification of dawn: night ‘tearing’ its dark robe.
segments.s166-g221: سر is literal “head” (here part of the verbal phrase meaning ‘lifted his head’). مرد کین is a genitive/appositive phrase “the man of hatred / the vengeful man.” اندرآمد is an older/poetic compound meaning “came up / arose” (here ‘rose’ from sleep); ز خواب is the ablative “from sleep.” The clause thus reads “the vengeful man’s head rose from sleep” i.e. “the vengeful man awoke / lifted his head from sleep.”
segments.s166-g222: بیامد is past “came” (arrived). به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase “to near / toward (the) proximity of,” functioning adverbially, and افراسیاب is the proper name of the addressee/target. Together: “he came (moved) close to Afrasiab.”
segments.s166-g223: ز here is a classical/poetic preposition that can mark source, reason, or a beneficiary/target depending on context; in this line it introduces بیگانه (“the stranger/foreigner”) and indicates the place was prepared with respect to that stranger (best read “for the stranger” or “concerning the stranger”). پردخته (participle/adjective from پرداختن) = “prepared/arranged,” and کردند is the past plural “they made/did.” جای is the object “place/seat.” Word order is verb after object-adjunct (typical in older Persian poetic phrasing).
segments.s166-g224: نشستند is the past plural “they sat down” (note: the lemma senses listed in the metadata are for washing, but here نشستند is clearly “they sat”). و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” جستند is the past of جستن used here in the sense “they sought/searched for,” and هرگونه رای is the object phrase “every kind of counsel/opinion” (هرگونه = in any way/any kind; رای = counsel/plan). The clause reads “they sat and sought every kind of counsel.”
segments.s166-g377: بپرسی is a 2nd-person present/subjunctive form of پرسیدن used here in a hortative/narrative sense “you will ask” or “you ask (and then)”; و simply links the clauses (“and”); گویی would normally be from گفتن meaning “you say” but the provided lexeme list gives senses for ‘گوی’ as a noun, so here it functions as the speech-verb “you say/you will say”; کزان = از + آن contracted in poetry, a relative/prepositional “from which / from that”; جشن‌گاه is a compound noun “banquet- place / place of festivity.” Together the segment frames a quoted or reported speech: “you will ask and say, ‘From that banquet-place…’.”
segments.s166-g378: نخواهی is the simple negative of خواهی (here used as 2nd-person future/intent: “you will not / you will not wish”); همی is the old continuous/emphatic particle (here marking the ongoing/future action); کرد is the light verb “to do/make” used with نگاه following; کس را is the indefinite object “someone / anyone” marked by را; نگاه is the noun “gaze / look.” Word order is poetic (verb before object), and the clause means essentially “you will not let anyone cast a glance” or “you will not (permit) anyone to look.”
segments.s166-g379: به مهرت is a prepositional phrase ‘because of/for your love’ (به + مهرت); همی is the old continuous particle giving progressive/future sense; دل بجنبد is a 3rd-person singular verb phrase (دل = subject, بجنبد = ‘stirs/moves’), so ‘the heart will stir’; ز جای is the adverbial ‘from its place.’ The clause describes an emotional reaction: ‘because of your love the heart is moved from its place.’
segments.s166-g380: یکی is an indefinite index or vocative ‘one (of you) / someone / one person’; با + فرنگیس means ‘with Farangīs’; خیز is the imperative ‘rise!’ or ‘get up!’; ایدر is an older form meaning ‘here’; آی is an imperative/ vocative “come!” — so the sequence is a short imperative request: “One (man), rise and go with Farangīs; come here.”
segments.s166-g381: نیازست is an impersonal or existential predicate ‘it is necessary / needed’; ما را is the dative/object ‘for us’ (classical Persian often uses را with personal pronouns in such constructions); به دیدار تو = ‘to a meeting with you / to see you.’ Together: ‘It is necessary for us to see you’ (i.e. ‘we need to visit/see you’).
segments.s166-g382: بدان here is بد + دان (compound) functioning as an imperative/command or pointer ‘know this / be aware of that’; پرهنر is an adjective ‘full of art/skill — cultured, accomplished’; جان is the vocative or appositive ‘O soul / life / beloved’; بیدار is an adjective ‘awake, alert’; تو is the 2nd-person possessive/pronoun ‘your/you.’ The phrase addresses the addressee’s soul: ‘Know this, O cultured, awake soul of yours.’
segments.s166-g383: برین is the poetic locative built from بر + این (on this); کوه ما = ‘our mountain’; نیز = ‘also’; نخچیر هست = ‘there is game/prey’ (نخچیر = game/huntable prey, هست = exists). The clause states that the mountain holds game in addition to other things.
segments.s166-g384: ز جام زبرجد is a genitive chain ‘from (a) cup of peridot’ (ز = from/of, جام = cup, زبرجد = peridot); می‌و شیر هست lists two substances available from that cup: می (classical ‘wine’, here orthographically attached as می‌و) and شیر (‘milk’), with هست ‘there is.’ In older poetic Persian nouns may appear conjoined and the verb هست follows to assert existence: ‘there is wine and milk.’
segments.s166-g409: ندانم is the negative present/past-stative 'I do not know' (ne + دانم). که here is the complementizer 'that' introducing the subordinate clause; the name گرسیوز follows; نیکخواه is an epithet 'well-wisher' modifying گرسیوز. Word order follows classical Persian: complementizer + subject phrase.
segments.s166-g410: چه گفته‌ست is an embedded question 'what has (he) said' using گفته‌ست (perfect/past form 'has said'). از marks 'about/concerning' (از من = 'about me'). The sequence بدان is the archaic/poetic compound 'به + آن' meaning 'to that'; here it functions as a prepositional phrase 'to that court' (بارگاه = 'royal court').
segments.s166-g411: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آمد is the simple past 'came'. بران is a deictic 'to/at that' (thereupon) modifying شهر 'city'; نو is the adjective 'new' placed after the noun in this poetic word order.
segments.s166-g412: پذیره (reception/welcome) is used nominally and بیامد is past 'came' (here rendered idiomatically as 'was received' or 'he received a welcome'). ز ایوان marks origin 'from the iwan/portico', and به کو ('to where' / 'into the hall') is a poetic locative/goal phrase; the line compresses movement from the iwan into the interior.
segments.s166-g413: بپرسیدش is a past-tense verb 'they asked him' (بپرسید + object suffix -ش = 'him'). از introduces the content 'about'; here it begins a coordination 'از راه و ز کار شاه' meaning 'about the way(s) and about the affairs of the king.' وز is the poetic contraction of و ز (and + from/about) linking the coordinated nouns.
segments.s166-g414: Each phrase is introduced by ز 'from/about' (poetic usage). رسم سپاه = 'the custom/organization of the army' (رسم here = custom/tradition). The second coordinate ز تخت و کلاه lists two royal items — تخت 'throne' and کلاه (here 'crown/helmet') — joined by و 'and'. The repeated ز before each coordinate is emphatic/poetic.
segments.s166-g415: پیام ('message') is the direct object; سپهدار توران is a genitive noun phrase 'the commander of Turan'. بداد here is an archaic past form meaning 'gave' / 'sent' (ب + داد = 'he gave/sent'); the verb is past and completes the clause 'the Turanian commander sent a message.'
segments.s166-g416: ز پیغام او uses ز to indicate source/occasion ('on/at/because of his message'). گشت is the past of گشتن used copulatively 'became' and شاد is the predicative adjective 'glad'; the clause means 'Siyâvash became glad at his message.'
segments.s166-g417: چنین داد پاسخ که is a fixed literary phrase meaning 'he answered thus'. داد is the past verb 'gave' used with پاسـخ (answer) to form 'gave an answer'. که introduces the content clause that follows. با یاد اوی is a prepositional phrase 'with the memory of him' (یاد + encliticِ possessive اوی = 'his/of him').
segments.s166-g418: نگردانم is negative + verbal form meaning 'I will not turn / I will not avert' (first person, present/subjunctive/optative sense used idiomatically). از marks separation or source ('from'), تیغ پولاد روی is a compound description of the sword — تیغ 'sword', پولاد 'steel', and روی 'face/surface' together giving 'the steel-faced sword' or 'the steel blade's face'; the phrase as a whole means 'I will not turn (my) face from the steel sword.'
segments.s166-g419: من اینک places the speaker ('I, now'). به + infinitive (به رفتن) expresses purpose or readiness 'for going / to go'. کمر بسته‌ام is a common idiom 'I have girded my waist' (بسته‌ام = perfect of بستن 'to bind'), i.e. 'I have girded myself' or 'I am ready to depart.'
segments.s166-g420: عنان با عنان تو is a parallel, graphic expression 'rein with rein of you' (repetition of عنان emphasizes joining). پیوسته‌ام is the perfect first-person 'I am joined / I have become joined' — together: 'I have yoked my reins to yours' or 'I am joined to you rein to rein.'
segments.s166-g421: سه روز اندرین is a temporal phrase 'for three days in this...' (اندرین = 'in this'). گلشن زرنگار is a noun + adjective 'a garden adorned with gold' (زرنگار = 'gold-decorated' / 'gilded'), so the whole phrase means 'for three days in this gilded garden.'
segments.s166-g422: بباشیم is first-person plural optative/present 'let us be' or 'we will be/remain'. و links the clauses. ز + noun (ز باده) means 'from/with wine' or 'by means of wine'. سازیم is first-person plural 'we will make/do' and کار here means 'affair/occasion' — together 'and let us make an occasion/ merry-making with wine,' i.e. 'let us make sport with wine.'
segments.s166-g423: که at the start here introduces an explanatory clause 'for' or 'that'. گیتی سپنج است is a metaphor 'the world is a sponge' (سپنج = sponge). پر + noun (پر درد و رنج) means 'full of pain and suffering' (پر = 'full (of)'). The clause gives the reason: the world soaks up suffering.
segments.s166-g424: بد آن را که is an exclamatory/formulaic phrase 'bad/woe to that one who...' (بد = 'evil/bad'; آن را with را gives emphasis). که introduces a relative clause 'who'. با غم بود در سپنج means 'is/was with sorrow in the sponge' — poetically 'who remains sorrowful in this sponge-like world.' بود is the copula 'is/was' used here in a general sense.
segments.s166-g553: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause; یاد آمدش is a light-verb construction where یاد (remembrance) is the noun and آمدش (آمد + ـش) is the past 3rd‑person form 'came to him' (ـش = 'to him'). روزگار گزند is a noun phrase in apposition to یاد: روزگار here means 'fate/trying times' and گزند qualifies it as 'injurious/harmful'. Word order is typical Persian: conjunction — noun phrase — verb.
segments.s166-g554: کزو = 'from which / from whom' (compound of ک+ز+و) introduces the source/cause clause; بگسلد is 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive (بـ- prefixed present stem) of گسستن 'to rend/break', here expressing potential/severe result; مهر چرخ بلند is the object: مهر 'favour/kindness' governed by چرخ 'the high heaven' (چرخ بلند = lofty heaven). The phrase says that from those days the high heaven's favour could be severed.
segments.s166-g555: نماند is past form with negative sense 'did not remain / was left (no more)'; برو here is a poetic use meaning 'to go on / to pass' (literally 'to go'), combined with بر + بسی روزگار 'many days' (بر = 'for/over', بسی = 'much/many', روزگار = 'time/days'). Together the clause means 'many days did not remain to go on' i.e. 'not many days remained'.
segments.s166-g556: به‌روز جوانی is a temporal phrase 'on/in the day/time of youth' (به‌روز = 'on the day/of the day', جوانی = 'youth'); سرآیدش is a present/future‑oriented verbal form 'its/his end comes' (سرآمدن 'to come to an end') with enclitic ـش = 'to him/it'; کار is the noun 'affair/condition'. Together: 'in the time of youth his affairs come to an end.'
segments.s166-g557: دلش is 'his heart' (دل + enclitic ـش); گشت is the past form of گشتن used as 'became' here; پردرد is an adjectival compound 'full of pain / sore'; و links the next noun; رخساره زرد = 'his visage/complexion (رخساره) pale (زرد)'. The construction is copulative: 'his heart became pained and his face turned pale.'
segments.s166-g558: پر از X = 'full of X' (پر = 'full', از = 'of'); غم دل = 'sorrow of the heart' (a genitive/attributive sequence); و connects the next phrase: لب پر از باد سرد = 'lips full of cold wind' (باد = 'wind', سرد = 'cold'). The clause repeats the inner state: heart full of grief and lips affected by the cold wind.
segments.s166-g559: بدو = 'to him' (prepositional phrase introducing the addressee); گفت here functions as the speech verb 'he said' (past 3rd person) introducing direct discourse; هرچونک is an adverb 'whenever/however' and می‌بنگرم is 1st‑person singular present imperfective 'I look / I behold'. The structure is 'He said to him, "Whenever I look ..."' Note: گفت is the reporting verb and the embedded clause uses present imperfective for a habitual/perceptual statement.
segments.s166-g560: ببادافره بد is a compact poetic phrase: بادافره (lit. 'wind‑wrought' / 'blown about') qualified by بد 'bad/evil' — together describing something ill produced or borne by the wind; نه is a negation; اندر is a preposition 'in / into'; خورم is 1st‑person present (from خوردن) 'I take / I accept / I eat'. The clause functions as the apodosis of the quoted clause: 'I will not accept/bear this evil (that the wind has wrought).' (خورم here is used in the sense 'I accept / take upon myself' rather than literal eating.)
segments.s166-g425: چو بشنید is a temporal clause meaning “when (he) heard”; چو is the classical conjunctive particle 'when'. گفت here would normally be the past tense of گفتن 'said' but the provided senses list does not include the verbal sense; خردمند شاه is an appositive noun phrase 'the wise king' serving as the subject of گفت (i.e. 'the wise king said'). Word order is V (بشنید) before the reporting verb گفت and then the subject as a title/apposition, which is common in older poetic style.
segments.s166-g426: بپیچید is a past (preterite) third-person singular of پیچیدن with the prefix ب- often appearing in older/fixed poetic forms; here it means 'turned/turned aside'. گرسیوز is the proper name (subject of this verb) and کینه‌خواه is an epithet in postposition 'the vengeance-seeking / vengeful'. The verb + name + epithet order is typical: verb first (narrative past) then the person and his descriptive epithet.
segments.s166-g427: به دل گفت is an idiom meaning 'he said to his heart' = 'he thought to himself' (be + دل + گفت). ار is the archaic conditional 'if'. ایدونک is an old/colloquial particle here meaning 'perhaps/if so' attached to the conditional to soften it. با من به راه literally 'with me on the way' — با + personal pronoun + به راه marks accompaniment toward a place or occasion. Note گفت is again the reporting verb but in the construction به دل گفت it functions as 'thought/said inwardly.'
segments.s166-g428: سیاوش بیاید uses بیاید (subjunctive/optative sense 'should come / may come') after the conditional ار to express the hypothetical arrival. به نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase 'to/near the king' (به + نزدیک + شاه) indicating destination or presence. The verb form biâyad here expresses possibility/future in a conditional context.
segments.s166-g429: بدین is the fused classical prepositional phrase 'به + این' meaning 'by this / with this' and introduces the means. شیرمردی is an abstract noun 'manliness / heroism' following بدین to indicate instrument/means. و چندین خرد links 'and several/ many devices/wiles'—چندین modifies خرد (here 'stratagems' or 'cunning devices'). Note that in older Persian خرد can mean 'skill, stratagem' as well as 'wisdom'.
segments.s166-g430: کمان مرا is 'my bow' with کمان (noun) followed by the clitic object/possessive sequence مـ/ـرا in the manuscript segmentation: here together they mean 'my'. را is the definite/object marker attached after the clitic in the given tokenization. زیر پی is a compound prepositional/adverbial expression 'زیر پی' meaning 'underfoot/subject to pursuit' (زیر = under, پی here = track/foot/pursuit), and بسپرد is the past/subjunctive third-person singular of سپردن 'to entrust/hand over/consign' — together 'to consign (my bow) to being trampled/put under pursuit'. Grammatically the clause gives the result/intent introduced by the preceding conditional context.
segments.s166-g431: سخن گفتن من is a gerundial nominal phrase 'my speaking / my speech' (سخن + گفتن) with من as the possessor. شود is the present/future-in-past or potential 'become' (third-person singular), and بی‌فروغ is a predicate adjective 'without radiance / dim' describing the state of the speech. The clause says that the speaker's words might lose their luster.
segments.s166-g432: شود again is 'will become / will be', پیش او means 'in his presence / before him' (پیش = before/in front of, او = him), and چاره من دروغ is a noun phrase 'my remedy/solution (چارهِ من) [will be] false' where دروغ is 'falsehood' or 'a lie'. Word order places the verb first (شد/شود) then the locative phrase then the predicate noun phrase, a pattern common in narrative lines.
segments.s166-g153: یکی is an indefinite subject meaning 'someone' (here not the numeral 'one'); با + name marks the opponent 'with Siavash'; نبرد آورد is a poetic compound where نبرد ('battle') + آورد (past of آوردن 'to bring') together mean 'waged battle/engaged in combat'. The verbal phrase is past tense and predicated of 'یکی'.
segments.s166-g154: This clause says what was accomplished: سر here means 'head' → by extension 'chief/leader' (i.e. the leaders). سرکشان is the plural 'the rebellious ones' (lemma listing in the data is not ideal), and زیر گرد آورد is a poetic expression meaning 'caused to be under (his) control / subdued'. The verb آورد is again past 'brought/made', and زیر carries the sense 'subject to / under the control of'.
segments.s166-g155: نیوشنده is a noun 'the listeners' (those who listen); بودند is the past‑tense plural copula 'they were/stood (there)'. لب با گره is a verbless descriptive phrase 'with lips tied/knotted' — a poetic idiom meaning they kept silent, lips tightly closed while listening.
segments.s166-g156: به پاسخ بیامد is a set phrase 'in answer came' (به marks the relation 'in/at (the) answer', بیامد is past 'came'). گروی زره is a terse poetic nominal phrase meaning 'the one of/with armor' or 'the armor-clad (man)' who comes forward; گروی is derived from گرویدن/گرفتن in a sense of 'one who adopts/assumes', here tied to زره 'armor'.
segments.s166-g157: منم here is 'I too' (not the predicate 'I am'); گفت is the past 'said' introducing reported speech; شایسته is the adjective 'worthy/fit', and کارکرد is a noun 'deed/task/performance' — together shایسته کارکرد = 'fit for the deed/task'. The copular/linking structure is condensed as typical in poetry.
segments.s166-g158: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'; نیست is the existential/negative 'there is not' or 'is not'; او را uses را as the definite object/affected‑party marker ('for him' / 'he has no...'); کسی is the indefinite 'anyone/anybody' in a negative context; هم‌نبرد is a compound meaning 'match/equal in battle' or 'rival/combatant'. The whole clause means 'if there is no one to match him in battle'.
segments.s166-g159: سیاوش is the subject; ز is a poetic contraction of از meaning 'from/at' (here 'on hearing' or 'at the words of'); گفت here stands for 'the words/speech' (the previously spoken remark); گروی زره again denotes 'the armor‑clad (man)/the speaker who took up armor' — so the phrase as a whole reads 'Siavash, at the speech of the armor‑clad (man)...'.
segments.s166-g160: برو کرد is a poetic past construction meaning 'he went away/turned off' (برو 'go' + کرد auxiliary). The trailing three words are a string of descriptive adjectives: پرچین (full of folds/wrinkles), رخان (relating to the face — a poetic form), and پرگره ('full of knots' = brows knit/tightly furrowed). Together they modify Siavash's visage: he turned away with a furrowed, knotted face.
segments.s166-g73: دو مهتر = 'two chiefs/elders' is the plural subject. نشستند is a past tense, 3rd-person plural verb 'they sat' (نشستن) placed after the subject as typical in Persian. بر تخت زر is a prepositional phrase ('on the golden throne') that follows the verb and marks location.
segments.s166-g74: بدان تا introduces a purpose clause: 'بدان تا' here functions like 'so that / with the intent that' and تا introduces the subordinate clause of purpose. کرا is an archaic relative/interrogative meaning 'who/whom'. برفروزد is a 3rd-person singular optative/subjunctive or present-stem meaning 'may make shine / illuminate' (used after تا to express purpose). هنر is the object 'skill/art' — 'to make skill shine' = 'to display skill.'
segments.s166-g75: بدو here means 'to him' (i.e., 'he said to him'). گفت is the past-verb 'he said' introducing direct speech; the provided senses list is for the noun 'conversation' and doesn't match the verbal use (hence -1). گرسیوز‌ای شهریار is a vocative: the name Garsivaz plus the vocative particle ای and شهریار ('O king' / 'sovereign') addressing the addressee.
segments.s166-g76: هنرمند is a vocative/appositive 'skillful one' or 'artist' describing the addressee. وز here functions as a classical connective meaning roughly 'of/from' or 'and/of the' linking خسروان ('the kings') to یادگار ('a memento/legacy'); the provided senses for وز do not reflect this usage. خسروان is the plural 'kings' and یادگار is a predicate noun meaning 'remnant' or 'memorial' — 'a memorial of the kings.'
segments.s166-g77: هنر is the subject 'skill/art.' بر گهر is a prepositional phrase 'over/on the jewel.' نیز is an adverb 'also/likewise.' کرده گذر is a compound verbal idea where کرده (perfect participle) combines with گذر (from گذر کردن 'to pass') to mean 'has passed' or 'has passed by' — so together 'skill has also passed over the jewel.'
segments.s166-g78: سزد‌گر is a conditional phrase 'سزد گر' = 'if it is fitting' (سزد = 'befit', گر = 'if'). نمایی is a subjunctive/imperative form of 'to show' (here, 'show' / 'you should show'); classical Persian often uses the subjunctive after conditional particles. به ترکان marks the indirect object 'to the Turks.' هنر is the object 'your art/skill.'
segments.s166-g79: This is a string of instrumental prepositional phrases: به نوک سنان = 'by/with the spear-tips' and به تیر و کمان = 'by/with arrow and bow.' The preposition به marks the instrument/means; نوک سنان = 'the points/tips of spears.'
segments.s166-g80: زمین آورد is a verbal phrase: آورد is the past-tense 3rd-person singular 'brought/caused' and زمین is the object 'the earth' (i.e., 'upon the earth' or 'to the ground'). تیرگی is the direct object 'darkness/gloom.' یک زمان is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in one moment' / 'at the same time' (suddenly).
segments.s166-g145: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (classical variant of از) introducing the source; یاران is the plural noun 'companions/followers' (object of ز); یکی is an indefinite numeral 'one / a certain'; شیر جنگی is a compound noun meaning 'war-lion' (a fierce warrior), with شیر as the head noun 'lion' and جنگی as an adjective 'warlike'; بخوان is an imperative form (2nd person singular) of خواندن used here to mean 'call' or 'summon' in the narrative sense (command: 'call/bring').
segments.s166-g146: برین is the fused form بر + این ('upon this'), introducing where to set/seat the man; تیزتگ is a descriptive compound (تیز + تگ) functioning as an epithet 'sharp‑bladed / sharp‑mounted' qualifying the following noun; بارگی here functions as an instrument/thing for bearing or mounting (literally 'burden-bearing' in the lexicon) and is the object to be placed upon; برنشان is an imperative (literally 'put on / set upon'), from برن‑ implying 'place/put on' (command: 'seat him on it').
segments.s166-g147: گر is the conditional 'if'; ایدونک is an archaic/classical particle or demonstrative ('this sort / thus') used to mark the particular banner being talked about; رایت is the noun 'banner/standard' (subject); نبرد here is a noun 'battle' and combines with the following predicate; منست is a predicative adjective meaning 'is fitting / is suitable / appropriate' — together 'رایت نبرد منست' = 'the banner is fit for battle.'
segments.s166-g148: سر is the noun 'head' (here plural/collective idea 'the heads of ...'); سرکشان is a plural/collective form referring to 'the headstrong / rebellious ones' (contextual sense: proud chiefs); زیر is the preposition 'beneath / under'; گرد is used as 'dust' or the dust kicked up around a standard (a poetic image), so 'زیر گرد' = 'under its dust' / 'brought low'; منست repeats the predicate 'is fitting/appropriate' or functions as an assertion about their being placed under it.
segments.s166-g149: بخندید is a past-tense verb 'he laughed' (3rd person singular, narrative past); گرسیوز is the proper name 'Garsivaz' (subject); نامجوی is an epithet or byname attached to him — the sequence is a simple past clause 'Garsivaz Namjuy laughed.'
segments.s166-g150: همانا is an emphatic adverb 'verily / truly / indeed'; خوش آمدش is a verbal phrase meaning 'it came well to him' → idiomatically 'it pleased him' (خوش as adjective 'pleasant', آمد with enclitic object ش = 'to him'); گفتار اوی is a genitive phrase 'his speech/words' (گفتار 'speech' + اوی 'his/her' as enclitic), so the clause states that his speech pleased him.
segments.s166-g151: به یاران is a dative phrase 'to the companions' (به marking the indirect object/audience); چنین گفت is a fixed phrase 'thus said' (چنین 'thus' + گفت 'said' in narrative use); کای is a vocative particle 'O!' (addressing someone) and begins the direct address 'کای سرکشان' = 'O, you headstrong ones.'
segments.s166-g152: که introduces a content question or indirect question 'who/which'; خواهد is the present/future auxiliary 'wishes / will' (here 'who wishes'); the second که is a subordinator linking to the verb گردد 'become' (گردد is present/passive/third-person singular 'become' or 'be made'); به گیتی is a prepositional phrase 'in/on the world'; نشان is the noun 'sign/mark/renown' — together: 'who wishes that he should become a mark upon the world?'
segments.s166-g41: This is a nominal group: پرستار (attendant) together with the quantifier چندی (several) indicates 'several attendants'. The construction به + noun (به زرین کلاه) is a poetic/older Persian way to indicate being clothed/attired in or having (here: wearing a golden cap). Word order places the description after the head noun, typical in Persian poetry.
segments.s166-g42: فرنگیس is the proper-name subject/center of the phrase. با تاج ('with a crown') is a prepositional phrase describing her wearing a crown. در پیش‌گاه ('in the presence' / 'before the court') states the location; در is the locative preposition and پیش‌گاه is the formal noun for audience/court. The whole segment is a verbless descriptive clause typical of epic poetry, supplying who was brought and where.
segments.s166-g43: فرود آمد is a compound verbal expression meaning 'descended' (فرود as 'descent' + آمد 'came' = 'came down/descended'). از تخت is the source phrase 'from the throne'. The conjunction و links the next clause; بردش is برد (took) with the object suffix -ش ('her'), and نثار ('offering, as a gift') qualifies how she was taken: 'took her as an offering.'
segments.s166-g44: بپرسیدش is a past, transitive verb form meaning 'he questioned/asked her' (classical texts sometimes keep the prefix ب- in past forms). از / ز (both used here) mean 'about, concerning' and introduce the topics of the questioning: شهر (the city) and شهریار (the king). The object pronoun -ش on بپرسیدش refers back to the woman taken.
segments.s166-g45: دل و مغز (heart and brain) are linked by و and followed by the possessor name گرسیوز in postnominal position, a common poetic order for 'the heart and mind of Garsivaz.' آمد به جوش is an idiom: آمد (came) + به جوش ('to a boil') meaning 'was stirred up/grew enraged or passionate.' The verb is in past tense describing a sudden internal agitation.
segments.s166-g46: دگرگونه‌تر شد uses the comparative/derivative form دگرگونه‌تر ('more changed/other') plus شد ('became') to mean 'he changed' or 'became different.' The phrase به آیین و هوش ('in manner/custom and in understanding/awareness') specifies the domains in which he changed; آیین here means 'conduct/custom' and هوش 'intellect/awareness.'
segments.s166-g47: به دل گفت is an idiomatic reflexive thought expression: literally 'he said to (his) heart' = 'he thought to himself.' The subordinate clause سالی چنین بگذرد uses بگذرد (subjunctive/present-form with classical ب- prefix) to express 'such a year will/pass (may) pass' and functions as the content of his thought.
segments.s166-g48: This is a concise, somewhat idiomatic negative past statement. سیاوش is the subject. کسی را is the direct object (anyone/someone) with the object marker را. به کس is a prepositional phrase 'to/with anyone' or 'as anyone'; نشمرد is the negative past of شمردن ('to count/consider') meaning 'did not consider/compare.' Together the literal sense is 'Siyavash did not count anyone as anyone,' i.e. 'Siyavash made no one comparable to another' (he made no comparisons or regarded no person as matching another).
segments.s166-g601: Subject + prepositional phrase + verb. 'سیاوش' is the subject. 'به گفتار او' (literally 'to/by his speech') marks the means/agent that persuades him. The verb 'بگروید' carries the classical narrative prefix بـ plus the root گروید (گرویدن) and here functions as a past narrative: 'he yielded/was persuaded.'
segments.s166-g602: 'چنان' = 'thus/so' links this clause to the previous one. 'جان بیدار او' = 'his awake/quick soul' where the pronoun 'او' follows the noun for emphasis ('his'). 'بغنوید' (بـ + غنود/غنو) is again the classical narrative form meaning 'fell asleep' or 'slumbered' — here a past action applied to 'جان'.
segments.s166-g603: 'بدو' = 'to him' (dative). 'گفت' is the past verb 'said' (here treated as past narration). 'ازان در' literally 'from that door/gate' or 'from that place'; 'که' introduces the reported clause 'that...'; 'رانی سخن' = 'the queen's speech/words' (رانی as 'rani/queen' with سخن 'speech'). Word order is typical classical Persian for reported speech: [to him] said [from that place] that [the queen's words ...].
segments.s166-g604: This is the content of the reported speech. The opening 'ز' = 'from' (poetic), 'پیمان' = 'covenant/oath' and 'رایت' = 'banner/standard' are coordinated by 'و'. 'نگردم' is a negated verbal form (ن + گردم) expressing 'I will not turn/abandon' in Ferdowsi's narrative style (گردیدن = 'turn/turn away'); the final 'ز بن' = 'from the root/foundation' (i.e. utterly/from its base).
segments.s166-g605: 'تو' is the vocative/subject 'you'. 'خواهشگری کن' is an imperative construction: خواهشگری (act of entreating / be a suppliant) + کن (do) = 'be a petitioner' or 'entreat (as I ask)'. 'مرا زو بخواه' = 'ask for me from him': 'مرا' (me with object marker 'را'), 'زو' = contraction of 'از او' ('from him'), and 'بخواه' is the imperative/command 'ask (for)'.
segments.s166-g606: 'همی' is an adverbial particle meaning 'always/still' emphasizing continuity. 'راستی جوی' is a light verb construction where 'راستی' = 'truth' and 'جوی' (from جستن) = 'seek' ('seek truth'). 'و' = 'and'. 'بنمای' = بـ + نمای (imperative of نمودن) 'show!/display!'; 'راه' is the object 'way/path'. Together: 'always seek truth and show the way.'
segments.s166-g89: This is a relative clause introduced by که (which) modifying a preceding noun. در جنگ مازندران is a prepositional phrase 'in the Mazandaran war', followed by the past second-person verb داشتی 'you had/possessed' — typical Classical Persian word order: relative marker + circumstantial phrase + verbal predicate.
segments.s166-g90: به نخچیر functions as a purposive/role phrase 'as prey' (به + نخچیر), بر شیر is the locative/on-phrase 'upon a lion', and the verb بگذاشتی is past second-person 'you placed/put'. The sequence places the beneficiary/purpose before the target and finalizes with the past verb.
segments.s166-g91: به آوردگه رفت is a simple past clause 'he went to the place of assembly' (به + noun + رفت). نیزه بدست is an instrumental/adjunct phrase 'spear in hand' placed after the verb phrase to indicate his posture/armament.
segments.s166-g92: عنان را is the definite object with the object marker را. بپیچید is the past-tense verb 'he twisted' (classical prefix ب- on the past stem). چون پیل مست is a simile 'like a drunken elephant', with چون introducing the comparison and مست as an adjectival predicate.
segments.s166-g93: This coordinate clause pairs two past actions: بزد (he struck/struck down) with نیزه as instrument/object, joined by و, then برگرفت 'he picked up/took up' with آن زره 'that armor' as its object. Word order: verb + instrument, conjunction + verb + object.
segments.s166-g94: زره را topicalizes the armor with the object marker را. نماند is the past 'remained' used impersonally here ('there remained'), followed by the negator ایچ 'nothing / not at all' and the list بند و گره 'strap(s) and knot(s)'. The structure means 'there remained no strap or knot on the armor.'
segments.s166-g95: از آورد indicates extraction 'from (it) he brought', with نیزه the object 'the spear'. برآورد here functions as a second verbal action 'raised/produced' and راست is an adverb/adjective 'straight' modifying the action. The two verbal forms present consecutive actions: he brought/drew out the spear and set/held it straight.
segments.s166-g96: زره را is the object 'that armor' with the marker را; بینداخت is the past 'he threw/cast'. زان سو که خواست is a locative-relative phrase 'to that side which he wished' (زان سو = 'that side', که خواست = 'that he wanted'), so the clause means 'he cast the armor to whatever side he desired.'
segments.s166-g505: هرانجا که = a relative locative 'where(ever) that'; روشن بود = predicate 'was clear/bright' (روشن here = 'clear/evident') in past tense; راستی is the noun 'truth' placed after the verb for poetic inversion (i.e., 'where truth was clear').
segments.s166-g506: فروغ دروغ is a noun‑noun compound meaning 'the radiance/gleam of falsehood' (دروغ modifies فروغ); آورد is a past tense verb 'brought/caused', and کاستی is the resulting abstract object 'diminution/deficiency'.
segments.s166-g507: نمایم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive (classical/poetic) 'I show / I will show'; دلم = دل + enclitic م 'my heart' (دلم را = 'my heart' with the object marker را); بر افراسیاب uses بر as a directional preposition 'to / toward Afrasiab' (i.e., 'I will present my heart to Afrasiab').
segments.s166-g508: درخشان‌تر is the comparative form 'more shining / more brilliant' (تر = comparative); از introduces the standard of comparison 'than'; بر سپهر آفتاب = 'on/upon the firmament the sun' → 'than the sun in the heavens' (بر here = 'on', سپهر = 'sky/heavens', آفتاب = 'sun').
segments.s166-g509: تو is the addressed 'you'; دل را ('the heart' + object marker را) is the object; به جز شادمانه = 'except for joy/joyfulness' (به جز = 'except'); مدار is a poetic prohibitive imperative 'do not turn/make' (here functioning as 'do not give/appoint').
segments.s166-g510: روان را = 'the soul/spirit' as object; به بد در گمانه = a locative/goal phrase 'into bad/evil suspicion' (بد qualifies گمانه 'conjecture/suspicion'); مدار repeats the prohibitive 'do not place/put' — overall: 'do not put the spirit into evil conjecture.'
segments.s166-g511: کسی = 'anyone/someone'; کاو here functions as a fused relative 'که او' = 'who/that' (classical/poetic usage); دم اژدها = 'the dragon's breath' (دم = breath/moment); بسپرد is a verb 'entrusts/commits' (third person singular).
segments.s166-g512: ز = poetic 'from' introducing the source/authority; رای جهان‌آفرین = 'the counsel/will of the world‑creator' (رای = plan/will, جهان‌آفرین = world‑creator); نگذرد is a negative present 'does not pass' i.e. 'does not escape / will not pass beyond' — together: 'does not escape the Creator's decree.'
segments.s166-g137: سیاوش (proper name) is the subject; بدو is the older/poetic combination of به + او meaning 'to him' (indirect object) placed before the verb. گفت is the main verb 'said' (here introducing reported speech). کین رای نیست is the embedded predicate: کین ('vengeance') modifies رای ('counsel/plan'), and نیست negates it — literally 'a counsel of vengeance does not exist'.
segments.s166-g138: نبرد is the noun 'battle/strife' and برادر 'brother' is the affected participant; کنی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive form of کن (to do/make) used here in a subordinate sense ('to make/force [one] to fight'). جای نیست is a fixed phrase 'there is no place/occasion'. Together the clause means 'this is no place to make (one) fight his brother.'
segments.s166-g139: نبرد دو تن is a genitive construction 'the battle of two persons' (دو تن = two people). جنگ و میدان بود uses the copula بود 'was' after two predicate nouns joined by و: 'was war and (was) a field/arena' — a poetic way to say the encounter was like an all-out battle on a battlefield.
segments.s166-g140: پر از + noun is the pattern 'full of ...': پر (full) + از (of) + خشم (anger) attaches to دل (heart) as its predicate: 'the heart was full of anger'. چهره خندان بود is a parallel clause ('the face was smiling') with the copula بود at the end shared by the two predicates; the juxtaposition creates the contrast between inner anger and outward smile.
segments.s166-g141: ز گیتی is a prepositional phrase 'from/of the world' placed at the start for emphasis. برادر here is vocative ('O brother'). توی means 'in/among' (here 'among you'), and شاه را is the definite object 'the king' marked with را; the verb that governs it follows in the next segment. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic emphasis.
segments.s166-g142: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle ('indeed/you even') placed before the verb. زیر نعل آوری is literal 'you bring/put under the horseshoe' (آوری is 2nd person present of آوردن). ماه را (the moon, marked by را) is the object; poetic word order places آوری before its object for rhythm/emphasis. The phrase is metaphorical: 'you would even place the moon under the horseshoe.'
segments.s166-g143: کنم is 1st-person present/subjunctive (used here with future-like force) 'I will do'. هرچ 'whatever' modifies گویی (here a 2nd-person present/subjunctive or relative 'you say' / 'that you say'), so کنَم هرچ گویی = 'I will do whatever you say'. به فرمان تو is an instrumental phrase 'by your command' specifying authority.
segments.s166-g144: برین is the fused phrase بر + این ('on this' / 'regarding this'). نشکنم is negative 1st-person present/subjunctive 'I will not break' used to express a firm promise. رای و پیمان تو are the direct objects 'your counsel/opinion and your pledge/covenant' — together: 'I will not break your counsel and your pledge regarding this.'
segments.s166-g1: نگه کرد is a verbal phrase formed from the noun نگه 'gaze' + کرد (past of کردن) meaning 'cast a look' or 'looked'. The subject گرسیوز نامدار follows the verb in poetic inversion; نامدار is an epithet 'renowned' modifying the name گرسیوز.
segments.s166-g2: This is a noun phrase giving the object: سواران 'horsemen' qualified by ترکان 'the Turks' and گزیده 'chosen/elite', with the numeral هزار 'a thousand' placed after the modifiers in poetic order. Together they mean 'a thousand chosen Turkish horsemen.'
segments.s166-g3: خنیده is a past/perfect form meaning 'sang' or 'having chanted' and here modifies/attaches to سپاه 'the army' as the agent. اندرآورد is past tense 'brought in' or 'brought about', and گرد is an adverbial particle 'around/about' — together (اندرآورد گرد) describe the host moving/coming around (i.e. encircling or drawing near).
segments.s166-g4: بشد is past 'became' (it/they became), with شادمان 'joyful' as the resulting state. تا here functions in the temporal/causal sense 'as' or 'when' (introducing the circumstance), and سیاووش گرد indicates 'around Siyavash' or 'as Siyavash came/approached' — so the clause means 'they became joyful as Siyavash drew near.'
segments.s166-g5: چو بشنید is a temporal clause 'when he heard' (چو = 'when', بشنید = past 'he heard'). بسپرد + راه is an idiomatic use of سپردن with راه 'road' meaning 'he set out on the road' / 'he took the road' — here بسپرد is past of سپردن functioning intransitively as 'set forth.'
segments.s166-g6: پذیره شدش is a light passive/experiential construction: پذیره (reception/welcome) + شدش ('became his' / 'befell him') meaning 'he was received' or 'a reception became his'. تازیان 'the Tazians/Arabs' is the agent and با سپاه 'with a host' indicates accompaniment — 'the Tazians received him with an army.'
segments.s166-g7: گرفتند is simple past 'they seized/took (hold)'. مر is an archaic oblique form (like مرا/به‌من) meaning 'to me' or 'at my (side)' used adverbially here, یکدگر 'one another' is the reciprocal object, and را is the definite/object marker; کنار gives the spatial 'beside/at the side of.' The clause thus means 'they seized one another beside/at my side.'
segments.s166-g8: This is a straightforward clause: سیاوش is the subject, بپرسید is past 'asked' (he asked), از 'of/about/from' introduces the target of the question, and شهریار means 'the king' — 'Siyavash asked the king.'
segments.s166-g593: This phrase gives a distance. The numeral phrase 'صد و بیست' (hundred and twenty) uses 'و' as the internal conjunction between hundreds and tens. 'فرسنگ' is the unit (farsang/parasang) following the numeral. The preposition 'ز' (poetic variant of 'از') marks origin: 'ز ایدر به چین' = 'from here to China' where 'ایدر' is an archaising demonstrative 'here' and 'به' introduces the destination.
segments.s166-g594: 'همان' here functions as a demonstrative/equative marker meaning 'the same' or 'likewise' linking the second distance to the first. 'سیصد و سی' is another compound numeral with 'و' joining hundreds and tens. 'به ایران‌زمین' is the prepositional destination 'to the land of Iran.'
segments.s166-g595: 'ازین سو' = 'from/on this side' (ز + این written as ازین + سو), an adverbial phrase giving location/side. 'همه' is the universal subject 'all/everyone.' 'دوستدار' is a predicative noun 'admirer/supporter.' The verb 'تواند' is the 3rd-person singular of 'توانستن' used in classical/poetic Persian to state ability or future/habitual occurrence ('can/will be'); here it applies to the subject 'همه' even though it is singular in form (poetic agreement).
segments.s166-g596: Two coordinated predicates 'پرستنده و غمگسار' ('a worshipper/devotee and a consoler/companion-in-sorrow') are linked by 'و'. The final 'تواند' again supplies the verbal force ('will be / can be') for both coordinated nouns; in classical style the auxiliary appears once and scopes over the coordination.
segments.s166-g597: 'وزان سو' (وز + آن + سو) = 'from that side / on that side' — a classical compound adverb. 'پدر' is the noun 'father.' 'آرزومند' is an adjectival participle/compound meaning 'desiring/longing' or 'hopeful.' The final word written 'تست' in the manuscript is the archaic copula (classical 'تُست' = 'thou/you are'); none of the provided senses match that copulative use, so it is marked as unmatched.
segments.s166-g598: This clause contains two coordinated nominal predicates about the subject: 'جهان بنده خویش' = 'the world (is) his servant' where 'بنده' is predicative 'servant' and 'خویش' is the possessive element (here split orthographically into 'خوی' + clitic 'ش' = 'his/own'). The 'و' links the second predicate 'پیوند' ('bond/link'), and the closing 'تست' is again the archaic copula applying to both coordinated predicates.
segments.s166-g599: بهر کس is a dative/prepositional phrase 'for each person' (بهر = for/to). یکی نامه‌ای is 'one letter' with the indefinite accusative -ای on نامه; کن is the imperative 'do/make', and دراز is the adjective 'long' placed after the verb in this poetic order (more ordinary prose would be نامه‌ای دراز کن). The whole is an imperative: 'Make/write a long letter for each person.'
segments.s166-g600: بسیچیده باش is a hortative/imperative instruction built from the past participle بسیچیده ('well‑arranged/composed') plus باش 'be!' — literally 'let it be well‑composed.' و links the clauses, and درنگی مساز is a negative imperative 'do not make a pause/delay' (درنگی = 'a pause/delay', مساز = negative imperative 'do not make'). Together: 'Arrange it carefully and make no delay.'
segments.s166-g313: پشیمان شد is a two-part predicate: پشیمان (regretful) + شد (past-tense 'became'), so 'he became regretful'. از marks the cause/target of regret ('about/of'), followed by رای و کردار خوی(ش) 'his counsel and deeds' — خوی + enclitic ش is a possessive construction meaning 'his own'. Word order is adjective + شد then prepositional phrase.
segments.s166-g314: همی is a poetic/progressive particle meaning 'still/continually' here. کژ دانست is a light-verb construction where دانست (here 'deemed/considered') takes کژ ('crooked/wrong') as its object/adjective: 'he considered ... crooked'. بازار خویش 'his market/affairs' is a possessive phrase (خوی + ش).
segments.s166-g315: چنین داد پاسخ is literally 'thus gave answer' (چنین 'thus', داد 'gave', پاسخ 'answer'); که introduces the following quoted/embedded clause ('that' / direct speech). زین سخن is a literary form (= از این سخن) meaning 'from these words' or 'in regard to this speech'.
segments.s166-g316: نه ... نه is the correlative 'neither ... nor'. سر نیک بینم literally 'I see no good head' — here سر نیک functions idiomatically as 'a good outcome / good sign' with بینم ('I see' — present/subjunctive form used for perception). بلا را نه بن: را is the object marker attached to بلا ('the calamity'), and بن ('root, foundation') is the second object negated by نه, so 'nor the root/cause of the calamity.'
segments.s166-g317: بباشیم is a first-person-plural hortative/subjunctive ('let us be / let us remain'). تا introduces the subordinate clause that follows (here 'until' / 'so that'). رای گردان سپهر is a poetic noun phrase: رای 'will/counsel' modified by گردان 'turning/revolving' with سپهر 'heaven' as the locus — 'the heavens' shifting counsel.'
segments.s166-g318: چگونه is 'how'. گشاید (from گشودن) is an optative/present form meaning 'will open' or 'will reveal' — here indicating how the heavens' will will 'open' or manifest. بدین کار = 'to/for this affair' (بدین = به + دین/this in archaic usage), and چهر 'face/appearance' is the thing that will be shown; word order is verb before object, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s166-g319: به هر کار 'in/for every matter' is a prepositional phrase. بهتر درنگ از شتاب is a comparative statement: بهتر ('better') + درنگ ('a pause/delay') + از ('than') + شتاب ('haste') — literally 'for every task, delay is better than haste.'
segments.s166-g320: بمان is the imperative/hortative 'remain/stay'. تا introduces the temporal clause 'until' and برآید ('arise/appear') is used for the sun; بلند آفتاب is 'the high/lofty sun' (adjective before noun in poetic position). The whole clause means 'stay until the sun rises high.'
segments.s166-g65: چو is a temporal subordinating conjunction 'when'; چو + subject + آمد forms a temporal clause 'when Garsivaz came'. بینداخت is a simple past (preterite) verb 'threw/tossed'; the following گوی is its object (classical/poetic word order allows verb before object here).
segments.s166-g66: سپهبد is the subject noun 'the spahbed'; پس is an adverb 'then'. گوی بنهاد is a verb phrase where بنهاد (past/polite form of نهادیدن) means 'placed/put'; روی functions as a postpositional/adverbial 'on/upon'. Word order places the object (گوی) before the verb (بنهاد) in this poetic line.
segments.s166-g67: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; او is the third-person subject 'he'. گوی is the direct object 'the ball'. در زخم چوگان is a prepositional phrase 'in/into the hollow (wound) of the mallet' modifying how/where he took the ball, and گرفت is the past-tense verb 'took/grasped/struck'.
segments.s166-g68: هم‌آورد here is an intransitive verb meaning 'was equal to / matched (him)'; it introduces the action of the next clause. او is the subject 'he'. خاک میدان گرفت is a separate clause where خاک میدان 'the dust of the arena' is the object and گرفت 'took/raised' describes kicking up or seizing the dust (i.e. raising dust by movement).
segments.s166-g69: ز here is the poetic ablative 'from'. ز چوگان او is an ablative/genitive phrase 'from his mallet' (چوگان = mallet). گوی is the subject 'the ball', شد is the auxiliary/past 'became' and ناپدید is the predicate adjective 'invisible/vanished' — together 'the ball became invisible/vanished from his mallet.'
segments.s166-g70: تو گفتی is the past second-person 'you said' (verb sense not listed in the provided senses, so marked -1). سپهرش is the noun سپهر 'sky/heavens' with the enclitic -ش 'his' as subject; همی is the old/prose particle indicating progressive or continuous aspect; برکشید is a past-tense verb 'lifted/rose'. Grammatically this is a reported clause introduced by 'you said' (i.e. 'you said that his sky/heavens were rising').
segments.s166-g71: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past 'he commanded/ordered'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that' / 'that they ...'). تخت زرین is the noun phrase 'golden throne' (تخت = throne, زرین = golden). نهند is the classical present/subjunctive/plural form of نهادن 'to place' used here in the subordinate clause after تا to express the commanded action.
segments.s166-g72: به میدان is a prepositional phrase 'into/to the arena/field' (به indicating direction/place). پرخاش modifies ژوپین to mean 'a gambeson (ژوپین) for assault/combat' (پرخاش = aggression/assault). نهند repeats the subjunctive/plural 'they place' and is coordinated with the previous نهند under the same تا-clause — two ordered actions.
segments.s166-g9: This is a compact, slightly inverted clause: به ایوان (to the iwan) is the goal expressed before the verb, کشیدند is the past 3rd‑person plural of کشیدن meaning 'they moved/led (away/to)', and زان جایگاه ('from that place') gives the source; زان is the poetic form of از آن. The noun جایگاه ('place') is the source referred to by زان.
segments.s166-g10: سیاوش is the subject; بیاراست is a past 3rd‑person verb (بیاراستن) meaning 'he adorned/arrayed'; جای سپاه is a noun phrase 'the place of the army' with جای (place) modified by سپاه (army). Word order is Subject–Verb–Object (typical narrative).
segments.s166-g11: دگر روز is an adverbial phrase 'the next/another day'; گرسیوز is the subject; آمد is the past 3rd‑person singular 'came'; پگاه ('dawn/early morning') is a temporal adverb placed after the verb (poetic ordering).
segments.s166-g12: بیاورد is past 3rd‑person 'he brought' and takes خلعت ('a robe of honour') as its direct object. ز (poetic از) introduces the source phrase نزدیک شاه 'near the king / from the king's presence' — together meaning 'brought the robe from the king'.
segments.s166-g13: سیاوش is the subject. The sequence ب + دان here functions together as the older/poetic 'بدان' (to/at that), pointing to the robe; خلعت شهریار is the noun phrase 'the king's robe' (خلعت modified by شهریار). In other words: 'Siyavash, at that robe of the king...'.
segments.s166-g14: نگه کرد is a light verb construction: نگه (gaze/look) + کرد (did) = 'he looked/gazed'. و شد is 'and he became' (شد past). The simile چون گل اندر بهار = 'like a flower in spring' describes his change of appearance/joy; اندر is the older preposition for 'in'.
segments.s166-g15: نشست here means 'sat down/settled' (past action). از بر باره is a compact, somewhat archaic phrasing meaning 'on/for the occasion' or 'because of the occasion' (از...باره). گام زن is a participial adjective 'step‑striking' (i.e., striking/marking steps) describing the action or manner associated with the occasion; the whole segment sets the scene for the gathering.
segments.s166-g16: سواران ایران is a genitival noun phrase 'the horsemen/riders of Iran' (ایران specifying whose riders). شدند (past 3rd‑pl) + انجمن (assembly/meeting) forms a predicative 'became an assembly' — i.e., they gathered together into an assembly.
segments.s166-g489: دلی دارد is literally 'has a heart' (دلی = 'a heart') with دارد third-person singular present 'has'. از تو here expresses the cause/agent 'because of you'. پر از is the idiom 'full of' and takes a following noun phrase (درد و کین = 'pain and hatred'). Word order is Subject (دلی) + verb (دارد) + adverbial/cause (از تو) + predicate complement (پر از ...).
segments.s166-g490: ندانم is ن + دانم, present tense first-person with negation 'I do not know'. چه is the interrogative 'what'. خواهد is third-person singular modal/future of خواستن used here as 'will/shall' or 'wants to', and جهان‌آفرین is the agent noun 'the world‑creator/Creator'. The clause is an independent question-like statement about what the Creator will do.
segments.s166-g491: تو دانی = 'you know' (تو subject, دانی second-person present of دانستن). که introduces the subordinate content clause. من دوستدار توام: دوستدار is a predicative adjective/noun 'fond/lover/devoted', and توام is a poetic/clitic form marking first-person copula meaning 'I am (your...)' — together 'I am fond of you'.
segments.s166-g492: به هر نیک و بد is an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'in every good and bad' (به = 'in/at', هر = 'every', نیک و بد = 'good and bad'). ویژه modifies the following noun as 'special/especially', and یار توام repeats the predication 'I am your companion' (یار = 'companion', توام = 'I am' as above). Overall: 'in good and bad alike I am especially your companion.'
segments.s166-g493: نباید is an impersonal negative modal meaning 'one should not / it ought not'. که introduces the following subordinate clause. فردا gives the time 'tomorrow / later'. گمانی بری is a poetic phrasing meaning 'to entertain a suspicion/guess' (گمانی = 'a supposition'; بری here is an older/poetic verbal form meaning 'to bear/carry/hold' a thought). The literal sense: 'one should not tomorrow hold such a supposition.'
segments.s166-g494: که 'that' introduces reported content. من بودم is 'I was' (بودم = past first-person of بودن). آگاه is predicate 'aware'. زین is the fused prepositional phrase ز + این = 'of this' (classical contraction meaning 'from/of this'), and داوری means 'judgment/adjudication'. Together: 'that I was aware of this judgment.'
segments.s166-g495: سیاوش بدو گفت = 'Siavash said to him' (سیاوش subject, بدو = dative 'to him', گفت = past 'said'). مندیش is an archaic/poetic negative imperative of اندیشیدن: 'do not think/assume' (مَنْدیش = 'do not imagine'). زین again is the contracted classical form 'from this / because of this'. The whole phrase is the reporting clause + imperative: 'Siavash said to him, "Do not imagine from this..."'.
segments.s166-g496: که introduces the content of the admonition. یارست is a predicative phrase 'is (a) friend/ally' where -ست is the copula. با من = 'with me' (meaning 'on my side' or 'friend to me'), and جهان‌آفرین is the subject 'the Creator' (literally 'world‑maker'). Word order places the predicative phrase before the subject for emphasis: 'that the Creator is my friend.'
segments.s166-g185: برآشفت is a past, intransitive/perfective verb meaning 'became angry' or 'flew into a rage' and appears before the subject (typical of Persian poetic word order). گرسیوز is the subject (proper name). از + noun (از کار) here indicates cause/attribution ('because of/at') and اوی is an older genitive/enclitic form meaning 'his', so از کار اوی = 'at/because of his deed'.
segments.s166-g186: This clause uses an inverted order: پر از غم شدش دل literally 'full of sorrow became-to-him heart' — شدش is شد (past of شدن) plus the enclitic -ش (to him/his), so 'his heart became full of sorrow.' The second half (پر از رنگ روی) uses پرِ + از + noun to mean 'full of (a quality)', here 'رنگِ روی' = 'complexion/colour of the face', describing his changed appearance.
segments.s166-g187: وزان is the fused sequence و + زان ('and from that' / 'then from that'), introducing movement from a previously mentioned place. تخت زرین is a noun + adjective ('golden throne'), and به ایوان شدند uses به + place plus شدند (past, 3rd-plural of شدن) in a poetic sense meaning they proceeded/moved to the iwan — poetic/archaic usage where شدن functions as a stative/motion verb.
segments.s166-g188: تو گفتی is the simple past 2nd-person ('you said') with تو as the explicit subject (a poetic register). که introduces a reported clause. بر اوج کیوان شدند: بر + اوج = 'on/upon the summit', کیوان (astral name) is the locus, and شدند is the 3rd-plural past of شدن used here to mean 'they were raised/ascended' (poetic; شدن used intransitively).
segments.s166-g189: نشستند is the past 3rd-plural 'they sat/they held a sitting' (here 'they remained/celebrated'). یک هفته is a duration phrase 'for one week'. با + instruments (نای و رود) marks accompaniment; نای (ney) is a reed flute, رود in this musical context is a string instrument (old lute-like instrument).
segments.s166-g190: This is a list of accompaniments/pleasures joined by و. می‌و (here read as می و) denotes wine (or libations) in classical Persian listings; ناز refers to dalliance/affection/coquetry; رامشگران are entertainers/comforters/minstrels; سرود is 'song' or 'ode'. The items are coordinated, describing the festivities.
segments.s166-g191: به هشتم = 'on the eighth (day)' (time adverbial). به رفتن گرفتند is the idiom گرفتن + infinitive, here گرفتن (گرفتند) with به + infinitive رفتن meaning 'they began to go' or 'they took to going/setting out' (گرفتن + verb = to begin that action). ساز is the direct object placed after گرفتند: 'they took up the instruments' (i.e., prepared to depart with their instruments).
segments.s166-g192: This lists the agents: بزرگان ('the great ones' / nobles) and گرسیوز (proper name). سرفراز is an adjective postposed to Garsivaz meaning 'proud, illustrious, or honored', so 'the great men and Garsivaz, proud.' The sequence follows normal Persian coordination (noun(s) + adjective modifying the closest noun).
segments.s166-g457: This is a compact reporting clause in classical Persian. بدو (to him) is a prepositional/dative phrase placed before the verb; گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (here represented as a speech-root); گرسیوز is the subject (the speaker); نامدار is an epithet 'renowned' placed after the name.
