segments.s166-g458: مرا این سخن نیست با شهریار is an impersonal/psychological construction meaning 'I have no desire/occasion (for) this speech with the king.' مرا (m+را) yields the dative 'to me' (often rendered 'I have' in English), این سخن is the thing in question, نیست negates existence/intent, and با شهریار marks the person involved ('with the king').
segments.s166-g459: نه ... آمدستم is a negated past: 'I did not come.' نه از دشمنی specifies the denied motive ('out of enmity/hatred'), and به رنج indicates the consequence/state ('into suffering' or 'to cause suffering'). The verb آمدستم is first-person past 'I came/arrived'.
segments.s166-g460: The second negated clause parallels the first: نه از چاره دورم = 'nor am I, for lack of a remedy/means, distant' (i.e. 'I am not at a loss'). دورم is predicate 'I am distant/removed' with از چاره marking what I'm not distant from (lack). به مردی و گنج ('in/with manhood and treasure') specifies the domains in which he is not lacking.
segments.s166-g461: ز گوهر ('from the jewel') gives source, مرا+را yields the dative 'to me' (myself as experiencer), با دل indicates location 'in/with the heart', اندیشه is the subject 'thought', and خاست is the past 'arose'/'sprang up'. The sequence is poetic: 'a thought arose in my heart from the jewel.'
segments.s166-g462: که introduces the content/result clause: 'that remembrance came to me (i.e. I remembered) of those true words.' یاد آمدم is a common phrasing meaning 'remembrance came to me' (impersonal English: 'I remembered'); زان points to 'those' and links to سخنهای راست 'true words.'
segments.s166-g463: The clause places origin and occurrence: نخستین ('the first') modifies بدی ('evil'); ز تور indicates source 'from Tur' (proper name); ایدر 'here' marks location; آمد is the past 'came.' Word order is poetic but yields 'the first evil came here from Tur.'
segments.s166-g464: که introduces the explanatory clause: 'because/from whom the divine farr departed.' برخاست here means 'departed/was taken away' (an idiom for loss of divine favor), ز + و (زو) = 'from him', and فره ایزدی is the compound 'divine farr/glory'.
segments.s166-g129: سیاوش بدو گفت is a short reporting clause: proper name (سیاوش) + indirect-object clitic phrase (بدو, “to him”) + past verb گفت (“said”). کین خود مگوی is the reported imperative: کین “revenge/vengeance” is the object/topic, خود is an emphatic/reflexive pronoun (“yourself”), and مگوی is the negative imperative (مگو) with the poetic final -ی — “do not say/mention.” The whole segment is literally “Siyavash said to him: ‘Do not mention revenge yourself.’”
segments.s166-g130: که here introduces the explanation/cause (“for/because”). تو is the emphatic second-person pronoun. مهتری is a predicate meaning “preeminence/foremost” (the copula ‘is’ is omitted in Classical Persian). شیر is a metaphorical noun “lion,” and و links the second predicate پرخاشجوی (“quarrelsome, aggressive”). The phrase reads “for you are preeminent — a lion and quarrelsome.”
segments.s166-g131: همان اسپ تو is a demonstrative + noun + possessive structure: “that very steed of yours.” شاه اسپ is a nominal predicate “king (شاه) of steeds (اسپ)” — i.e. “a chief/king among horses.” منست (from منس) functions as a predicate/copula meaning “is fitting / is (so).” The order is typical: possessor follows the noun and the predicate follows the noun phrase.
segments.s166-g132: کلاه تو is “your helmet.” آذر گشسپ is a compound/name (آذر + گشسپ, i.e. Azar Gushasp); here it identifies whose helmet it is or with whom it is associated. منست again predicates “is (so)/belongs (appropriately).” The whole clause states that the addressee’s helmet is (or is like) Azar Gushasp’s (to the speaker).
segments.s166-g133: جز از خود literally “except from yourself” — an idiomatic way to say “except you.” ز (poetic from of از) + ترکان marks the source/group “from the Turks.” یکی برگزین is an imperative: یکی (“one / someone”) + برگزین (imperative ‘choose!’). Word order places the exception and source before the imperative.
segments.s166-g134: که introduces a relative clause modifying یکی (“one who…”). با من بگردد uses با + pronoun (من) + verb بگردد (present/subjunctive of گشتن/گردیدن) meaning “will go/roam/turn with me” — here “accompany me.” نه بر راه کین is a negative adverb (نه) + locative/prepositional phrase بر راه کین (“on the road/path of vengeance”) meaning “not on the path of revenge” (i.e. not for vengeance).
segments.s166-g135: بدو گفت is again “he said to him” (بدو = “to him”). گرسیوز‌ای is the vocative form of the name Garsivaz with the vocative particle ای attached. نامجوی is an epithet formed from نام (“name/reputation”) + جوی (“seeker”) — a descriptive vocative “O seeker of fame.” Together this is direct address: “He said to him, ‘Garsivaz, O fame-seeker…’ ”
segments.s166-g136: ز بازی is an ablative/associative phrase “from/of the play/game.” نشانی is the noun “sign/token/mark.” نیاید is the negative present/subjunctive/optative form meaning “does not/will not come” or “should not come,” and بروی (here) indicates the direction/recipient — “upon you” or “to you” (literally ‘come/go to you’). The clause thus says “no sign from the game will come to you” — i.e. “do not expect any token from this game.”
segments.s166-g209: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' introducing the whole clause; تا is the temporal conjunction 'until' that sets the limit; به درگاه افراسیاب = 'to/into the court of Afrasiab' (به + noun phrase). Word order is poetic but translates as 'until he reached Afrasiab's court.'
segments.s166-g210: نرفت is the past negative 'did not go / did not flow' (3rd‑person); اندران جوی = 'in that channel/stream' (اندران = 'therein'); جز introduces the exception 'except'; تیره آب = 'dark water.' The verb applies to the stream/flow idea, so the sense is 'nothing/no current went there except dark water.'
segments.s166-g211: چو = 'when' introducing a temporal clause; نزدیک modifies the following phrase as 'near / approaching'; سالار توران سپاه is a genitive/attributive sequence = 'the commander (سالار) of the army (سپاه) of Turan (توران)' — poetic word order puts modifiers together.
segments.s166-g212: رسیدند is the plural past 'they arrived' (subject understood from context, e.g. the troops); و = 'and'; هرگونه here functions adverbially 'in every way / about every kind (of thing)'; پرسید is past 'asked'; شاه is post‑posed subject 'the king' (poetic subject after verb).
segments.s166-g213: فراوان modifies the verb phrase as 'much / at length'; سخن گفت is the verbal phrase 'spoke (words)' = 'spoke at length'; و = 'and'; نامه (direct object) is 'the letter'; بداد here appears as a past verb 'gave (the letter)', but the listed senses do not match a simple 'gave'—the form is archaic/poetic (ب + داد).
segments.s166-g214: بخواند is an archaic/epic past 'he read/recited' (the initial b- is a classical prefix); و = 'and'; بخندید = 'he laughed'; و = 'and'; ز = poetic preposition 'from/because of'; و (second و) is a coordinator linking the phrase before گشت; گشت شاد = 'became happy.' Together: 'he read and laughed, and from that became glad.'
segments.s166-g215: نگه + کرد = the verb 'looked/gazed' (نگه 'gaze/watch' with کرد as light verb); گرسیوز is the subject 'Garsivaz' placed after the verb (poetic order); کینه‌دار is a post‑positive adjective 'resentful / full of hatred' describing him.
segments.s166-g216: بدان here functions as به + آن = 'at/to that' (poetic/demonstrative use: 'at that'); تازه رخساره شهریار = 'the king's fresh/new countenance' (تازه = fresh/new, رخساره = face/complexion, شهریار = the king). Together: 'he gazed at that fresh face of the king.'
segments.s166-g121: بمیدان is a prepositional/adverbial phrase (به میدان) meaning 'in the field/battlefield' placed at the start for emphasis; کسی نیست is an existential negation 'there is no one'; همتای تو literally 'your equal/peer' (همتا + possessive تو) is the logical predicate. Word order is topic (place) → existential → predicate.
segments.s166-g122: هم‌آورد functions as 'a match / rival' (poetic compound); تو‌گر is a conditional particle 'if you...' or 'if [it were that] you...'; ببالای تو ('on high' / 'at your height') is a locative phrase modifying the hypothetical confrontation. The clause is an incomplete conditional clause continuing the thought from the first half.
segments.s166-g123: گر introduces a conditional 'if'; ایدونک is a small colloquial/poetic particle here (emphatic/illustrative) modifying the verb; بردارم is first-person singular subjunctive/optative of برداشتن 'to take/remove' ('were I to take'); از پشت زین is a prepositional phrase 'from behind the saddle' giving the source. The verb form expresses a hypothetical action tied to گر.
segments.s166-g124: ت + را here represent the object مرا/ترا ('you' as definite object) split into suffix and object marker; ناگهان is the adverb 'suddenly'; برزنم is the verb phrase 'I would fling/beat/throw down' (first-person optative), followed by the goal phrase بر زمین 'onto the ground'. Together they complete the conditional consequence. Note: several orthographic splits in the word list represent one object form in normal spelling.
segments.s166-g125: چنان دان is an imperative/optative 'consider/think it so' (دان = 'consider'); که introduces a content clause 'that ...'; از تو دلاورترم is a comparative construction 'more valiant than you' (دلاورترم = 'I am braver'). Word order: imperative → complementizer → comparative clause.
segments.s166-g126: باسپ و بمردی is a pair of adverbial/nominal phrases 'in/with [my] arms (or army/shield) and in manhood/courage' (با + سپ, با + مردی in fused poetic form) joined by و; ز تو marks the standard of comparison 'than you'; برترم 'I am superior' (first-person predicate). This repeats and reinforces the comparative claim.
segments.s166-g127: وگر = 'and if' (conditional); تو مرا is the object 'you [me]' (here split in the word list as م + را); برنهی is the verb in the subjunctive/optative 'you cast/throw down' (poetic form); بر زمین 'onto the ground' is the goal. The clause sets a hypothetical: 'and if you cast me down...'.
segments.s166-g128: نگردم is a negative optative/indicative 'I will not turn / I will not go back' (first person); بجایی here functions to indicate direction/place 'to a place' (poetic use); که introduces a relative clause 'where they seek (جویند)'; کین 'vengeance/hatred' is the object of جویند. Overall: 'I will not turn/go to the place where they seek vengeance.'
segments.s166-g297: چو is a conditional 'if' (here introducing a subordinate clause). جویی is the 2nd-person present/imperative-stem meaning 'you seek' or 'you ask for'; دگر modifies it as 'something else/another'. ز + و together represent 'from him' (poetic contracted form), and تو is the subject 'you' linked to the following predicate. بیگانگی is a nominal predicate 'estrangement/alienation' that is left without its verb until the next segment (i.e. this whole chunk functions as the subject/topic of the next verb).
segments.s166-g298: کند is a 3rd-person singular present/future-like verb 'does/makes' (classical narrative present); رهنمونی is a noun 'guidance/guide' used metaphorically as 'serves as a guide' (i.e. 'will lead'); به here means 'into/towards' and governs the following abstract noun; دیوانگی is the goal 'madness'. Together: '(it) makes/serves as a guide into madness.'
segments.s166-g299: یکی here means 'one (kind/type)'; دشمنی is the noun 'enmity/hostility' and باشد is the classical present/optative copula 'is' (here stating identity/definition). اندوخته (paired with the next word) means 'what is stored/saved' (a stock or reserve). The phrase sets up a definition: 'One kind of enmity is (the act of) ...'.
segments.s166-g300: نمک را: نمک ('salt') is used idiomatically (here = favours/kindness received) and را marks it as the definite/direct object. پراگنده is a participial/adjectival form 'scattered' (describing the action done to the saved salt). بر + سوخته = 'upon the burned' (بر = on/upon; سوخته = scorched/one who has been burned), so the whole phrase describes the hostile act: 'to scatter one's saved salt upon the burned.'
segments.s166-g301: بدین is the fused prepositional phrase (به + این) 'to/into this' (here introducing the location/context). داستان is 'tale/account'; زد is the narrative verb 'struck in/uttered/added' (classical poetic usage for 'said' or 'interposed'). یکی 'a certain/one' modifies رهنمون 'guide/counsellor' (here 'a certain counsellor'). The whole unit means 'to this story a certain guide interposed/added (a remark)'.
segments.s166-g302: که is the subordinating/quotative 'that' introducing the reported speech. بادی is the noun/adjective 'a wind' (poetic form); the second که is a relative 'that/which'. از خانه 'from/out of the house' (از = from; خانه = house). آید is the 3rd-person present 'comes'; برون is the adverb 'out/forth'. Together they begin the quotation: 'that a wind which comes out of the house ...'.
segments.s166-g303: ندانی is the 2nd-person present negative of 'to know' (literally 'you do not know'). تو is the explicit pronoun 'you'. بستن is the infinitive/masdar 'to close/stop' (what you do not know how to do). برو is an imperative 'go' or an interjection 'be gone'; here it addresses the traveller. رهگذار is the vocative/noun 'wayfarer' or 'passer-by'. The segment reads as an address: 'You do not know (how) to bar (him); go, wayfarer.'
segments.s166-g304: وگر is the conditional 'if' (but if). بگذری is the 2nd-person present subjunctive 'you pass' (or 'you should pass'); نگذرد is the 3rd-person present negative 'it does not pass' (here impersonal—'will not let pass' or 'does not pass by'); روزگار is an abstract noun often 'fate' or 'the times'—here best read as 'fate/the world'. So: 'and if you pass, fate/the world will not let you pass.'
segments.s166-g305: سیاووش is the subject (the legendary prince). داند is the present third-person singular verb 'knows' (classical/plain form). همه کار تو is the object: همه 'all' qualifies کار 'affairs/deeds', and تو is the possessive 'your' placed after the noun in this classical word order (noun + possessor).
segments.s166-g306: هم … هم is the correlative 'both … and'. از (here) and the poetic variant ز both mean 'from'. The two prepositional phrases 'از کار تو' and 'ز گفتار تو' parallel each other: 'from your deeds' and 'from your speech/words'; تو again marks possession.
segments.s166-g307: نبینی is a negative present form 'you do not see' (verb before subject here). تو is the explicit subject 'you'. ز (contracted here with او as زُ or 'زو') means 'of/from him'. جز introduces the exception 'except', and همه intensifies 'all' (i.e. nothing but). درد و رنج are paired nouns 'pain and suffering'.
segments.s166-g308: پراگندن is the infinitive/verbal noun 'the scattering' or 'to scatter' used as a noun-like complement listing what is seen: 'the scattering of ...'. دوده, نام, گنج are the coordinated objects: 'smoke, name/fame, and treasure'.
segments.s166-g309: ندانی is 'you do not know' (second-person negative). که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'). پروردگار here means 'raiser/one who nurtures' (not the religious 'Lord'); پلنگ follows as the object of that nurturing: 'the raiser of the leopard.'
segments.s166-g310: نبیند is third-person negative 'he does not see'. ز 'from/of' marks source. پرورده is a past-participle noun 'that which was reared' or 'the reared one' (the leopard he reared). جز introduces the only-exception 'except'. درد و چنگ 'pain and claw' gives the result: from his rearing he finds nothing but suffering and the beast's claw (i.e. harm).
segments.s166-g311: چو is the temporal/concessive conjunction 'when'. افراسیاب is the subject. این سخن 'these words/this speech' is the object. باز جست is a literary expression 'leaped again' used here to mean 'uttered/said again' or 'repeated.' The verb جست (from جستن) is used idiomatically for an abrupt utterance.
segments.s166-g312: همه گفت is 'everyone said' (همه as collective; classical usage allows گفت in this construction). گرسیوز آمد is the reported content 'Garsivaz has come.' درست is an adverbial/emphatic comment 'indeed/rightly/true' qualifying the statement.
segments.s166-g433: This chunk is a short clause built around the infinitive ساختن (‘to make/devise’) preceded by the necessity modal باید (‘must’). یکی چاره is a noun phrase meaning ‘a certain remedy/one solution’ (literal ‘one remedy’), کنون is the adverb ‘now’ modifying ساختن, and the verb-like infinitive ساختن completes the idea: ‘a remedy must now be made/devised.’ Word order is flexible in classical Persian so the infinitive follows the modal and adverb.
segments.s166-g434: This is an infinitival purpose/accusative phrase functioning as the content of چاره ساختن: دلش را (the object, with the possessive enclitic ش) + را (definite/object marker) marks what is acted on; به + راه بد indicates the target course (‘onto a bad way’), and انداختن is the infinitive ‘to cast/place/bring about’. Together: ‘to cast/place his heart upon a bad path’ — i.e. to lead or mislead his heart.
segments.s166-g435: زمانی (here ‘for a while’) sets a temporal frame; همی + بود is the classical continuous/past construction meaning ‘was still / continued to be’; و links to the following clause; خامش (‘silence’ or ‘being silent’) is used as the predicate/description and بماند (‘remained’) is the stative verb meaning ‘stayed / lingered’. Overall: ‘for a time he remained silent.’
segments.s166-g436: دو چشمش = ‘his two eyes’ (دو numerally modifying چشمش). بروی here is the bound/prepositional form (برِ روی) meaning ‘on/onto/toward’ (classical contraction), followed by the name سیاوش as the location/target; بماند again is the stative verb ‘remained.’ The clause says his two eyes remained directed at Siavash. (Note: the provided lexicon entries do not list the prepositional reading for بروی, so that entry is treated as an infixed preposition here.)
segments.s166-g437: فرو ریخت is a compound verbal phrase: فرو (‘down/away’) + ریخت (here the verbal noun/verb idea ‘fell/poured’) = ‘poured down / fell forth.’ از دیدگان marks the source ‘from (his) eyes.’ آب زرد is a noun phrase ‘yellow water’ (a poetic image for tears). Word order places the source phrase after the verb in this classical line.
segments.s166-g438: This clause explains purpose/means: به آب دو دیده = ‘by/with the water of the two eyes’ (به used here in the instrumental sense ‘by/with’), همی again signals ongoing action, and چاره کرد is an idiomatic verbal collocation ‘made/attempted a remedy’ (کرد = ‘did/made’). So: ‘he tried to remedy it with the water of his two eyes’ (i.e. by shedding tears).
segments.s166-g439: Here the subject Sیاوش comes first, then the object pronoun ورا (‘to/at him’, classical object form) and the verb دید (‘saw’) — classical S–O–V order with object before verb. پرآب چهر is a postpositive descriptive phrase: پرآب (‘full of water’, i.e. tearful) modifying چهر (‘face’). Together: ‘Siavash saw him — a face full of tears.’
segments.s166-g440: بسان کسی = ‘like someone’ (a simile). کاو here is the archaic fused relative (classically kā-ū = ‘که او’ = ‘who/that he’), introducing the relative clause; بپیچد is a present/subjunctive-form verb meaning ‘turns / bends / inclines’ (ب- prefix + پیچد), and به مهر = ‘toward love/affection’ (مهر = love/affection). So the simile reads ‘like someone who turns toward love/affection.’
segments.s166-g25: به زودی is an adverbial phrase meaning “soon/shortly” (به here forming an adverb with زودی). مرا is the first‑person object ('me') composed here of م + را (the object marker را follows separately). با + سواری دگر = “with another rider/horseman,” where با is the preposition 'with' and دگر modifies سواری as 'another/other'.
segments.s166-g26: بگفت is the past narration verb 'he said / he told' introducing reported speech; اینک is the temporal particle 'now' or 'behold'; شو is an imperative 'be/go' (archaic imperative use); شاه را is the king with the object marker را (here marking the addressee/target of the tidings); مژده بر is the noun 'tidings' followed by بر used in the sense 'to/toward' — the clause is ordered poetically, so the imperative 'شو' governs the following action 'bring/give tidings to the king.'
segments.s166-g27: همان is a pointing determiner 'that / the same'; مادر is 'mother'; کودک here means 'child' with the sense 'infant / little one'; ارجمند is an adjective 'esteemed / precious' modifying کودک (i.e. 'the noble/precious child').
segments.s166-g28: This phrase is elliptical/poetic: جریره is a (rare/poetic) noun placed before سر بانوان بلند and here functions as the thing located 'on' them (in context a head‑ornament); سر بانوان means 'the heads of the ladies' and بلند 'lofty/high' qualifies سر. Word order is noun + location phrase: 'a jariyeh upon the lofty heads of the women.'
segments.s166-g29: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb 'he commanded/said' (often used for royal speech); یکسر is an adverb here meaning 'at once / straightaway'; به فرمانبران is a prepositional phrase 'to the obedient (ones)/those who follow orders' (فرمانبران functions as a plural nominal 'the obeyers/retainers').
segments.s166-g30: زدن is an infinitival/nominal form used as the complement of the command 'to do X' (here 'to apply/put on'); دست آن خرد is the direct object 'the hand of that little one' (آن 'that', خرد here 'little' rather than 'wisdom'); بر زعفران uses بر with the substance/instrument 'زعفران' — literally 'to put (apply) (saffron) on the hand of that child.'
segments.s166-g31: نهادند is the past tense verb 'they placed / they put'; بر پشت این نامه‌بر is the locative phrase 'upon the back of this letter‑carrier / messenger' (بر = on/upon, پشت = back, این = this, نامه‌بر = messenger/letter‑carrier).
segments.s166-g32: که introduces a relative clause 'who/which/that'; پیش + name means 'before / in the presence of' (here 'in front of / to Siyâvash'); خودکامه is an epithet 'self‑willed / autocratic' modifying the name; بر here is the preposition 'toward / to' and the clause is left open/elliptical at the line break (typical of epic diction).
segments.s166-g569: بدو گفت = 'he said to him': بدو is the dative/enclitic phrase 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb for emphasis; گفت is the past tense of گفتن ('said') (here used as the reporting verb). گرسیوز‌ای نامجوی is a vocative: the name with the vocative particle ای attached after Garsivaz and followed by the epithet/name 'Namju'.
segments.s166-g570: ترا آمدن پیش او نیست روی is an idiomatic negative statement: ترا (ت + را) marks 'you' in a fronted, emphatic/dative way; آمدن is the infinitive 'to come/appear'; پیش او = 'in the presence of him'; نیست is the negative copula 'is not'; روی literally 'face' — the phrase 'نیست روی' means 'you do not have the face/nerve' or 'it is not fitting for you to appear.' Word order is somewhat inverted for emphasis (object/target fronted).
segments.s166-g571: به پا اندر آتش شدن = 'to set foot into the fire': به پا 'with foot' / 'to put a foot', اندر = 'in/into', آتش = 'fire'. نشاید + شدن is an impersonal modal construction: نشاید = 'it is not fitting/should not', followed by the infinitive/nominal verbal شدن 'to become/to be done' (classical usage) to express 'it is not fitting to ...'.
segments.s166-g572: نه بر موج دریا بر ایمن بدن = 'nor is the body safe on the sea's waves': بر موج دریا = 'on the wave(s) of the sea' (بر = 'on'), بر ایمن بدن = 'the body is safe' with the negative continuation implied by the preceding نه; ایمن = 'safe', بدن = 'body'. The copula is omitted (classical Persian often omits 'is').
segments.s166-g573: همی خیره بر بد شتاب آوری: همی is an archaic/prosodic marker meaning 'continually/always' attached to a verb; خیره بر بد = 'fixedly/gazing at evil or misfortune' (خیره = 'staring'), شتاب is a noun 'haste', and آوری (present stem with the old ending) is the 2nd/3rd-person present form of آوردن meaning 'bring/cause'. Together it says 'you keep staring at misfortune and bring about/hasten it.'
segments.s166-g574: سر بخت خندان به خواب آوری: a noun phrase + verb: سر بخت خندان = 'the smiling head/top of fortune' (سر = 'head/top', بخت = 'fate/fortune', خندان = 'smiling'); به خواب آوری = 'you bring to sleep' (به + خواب 'to sleep', آوری = 'cause/bring'). The verb again is the archaic present form 'آوری' meaning 'you cause/bring about.'
segments.s166-g575: ترا من همانا بسم پایمرد: ترا (ت + را) is fronted for emphasis 'to you'; من = 'I' (subject); همانا = 'verily/truly' (emphatic); بسم = 'enough' (predicate adjective), پایمرد = 'steadfastness/endurance' (noun). The clause says emphatically 'I truly have enough steadfastness' with the topical dative 'ترا' marking the addressee.
segments.s166-g576: بر آتش یکی برزنم آب سرد: a terse, poetic promise: بر آتش = 'upon the fire'; یکی = 'one' (a single/one time; an intensifier); برزنم is here a 1st-person verb form (archaic -م ending) meaning 'I will throw / I will cast' (the listed senses for برزنم didn't include this verbal sense, so it's treated as an irregular/poetic verb form); آب سرد = 'cold water' (object). So the literal sense is 'I will cast one cold water upon the fire' (i.e. quench it at once).
segments.s166-g441: بدو گفت is a compact clause meaning “he said to him”: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (ب + دو) and گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (here used in past). نرم‌ای برادر is a vocative/adverbial phrase: نرم‌ای 'softly/gently' (an adverbial or interjection with the enclitic ای) modifies گفت, and برادر is the address 'brother' (vocative). The clause introduces the following question as direct speech.
segments.s166-g442: غمی هست is a simple predicative clause 'there is a grief/sorrow' (غمی = grief as subject, هست = copula/existential 'is'). کان را بشاید شنود is a relative/subordinate clause modifying غمی: کان (archaic 'that'/'which') + را (object marker) introduces the clause, بشاید is an archaic impersonal/modal form meaning 'it may / it is possible', and شنود is a 3rd-person verb 'hear' placed at the end (classical word order: verb-final). Together: 'a grief that one may hear of.'
segments.s166-g443: گر is the conditional 'if'. از شاه ترکان is an ablative/agent phrase 'on account of / from the Turk king' (از + شاه ترکان). شدستی دژم is an archaic past/perfect second-person form: شدستی functions as 'you became' (archaic formation) and دژم is the predicate adjective 'sullen/grim' describing the subject (you).
segments.s166-g444: به دیده درآوردی is a verb phrase literally 'you brought to the eye' (به + دیده = 'to the eye'); درآوردی is a past tense/perfect 'you produced/caused to appear'. از درد نم is a source/causal phrase 'a tear from pain' (از marking source, درد = sorrow/pain, نم = tear). The verb at the end produces the result 'you caused a tear to appear in your eye because of sorrow.'
segments.s166-g445: من اینک همی با تو آیم به راه places the subject and adverbial elements before the verb: من 'I', اینک 'now', همی (classical aspectual particle) gives the sense of immediate/ongoing action, با تو 'with you', and آیم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive 'I come' (verb appears after these elements). به راه 'on the road' is the goal/route phrase following the verb.
segments.s166-g446: کنم جنگ is an idiomatic causative/performative construction: کنم 'I will make/do' + جنگ 'war' = 'I will wage war' or 'I will make war'. با شاه توران سپاه is the accompanying/target noun phrase introduced by با 'with' — here meaning 'with the army of the Shah of Turan' or 'against the Shah of Turan with an army' depending on context; سپاه (army) is the head noun with توران specifying the domain.
segments.s166-g447: بدان is an imperative 'know' formed of the prefix ب- plus دان 'know' (here presented together as the command 'know this'). تا introduces purpose/result 'so that / in order that'. ز بهر چه is a classical expression meaning 'for what reason / why' (ز بهر = 'for the sake of'), and آزاردت is a past/present result 'it troubles/hurts you' with the enclitic تُ marking 'you' as affected.
segments.s166-g448: چرا here begins the explanation 'because': چرا 'because/why'. کهتر از خویشتن is a comparative phrase 'one inferior/lesser than yourself' (کهتر = inferior/smaller, از = than/from as the comparative marker, خویشتن = oneself/yourself). داردت is the verb 'he has/holds you' (دارد with the clitic ت = you), meaning 'he keeps/possesses/holds you' in the sense 'someone of lesser rank holds you (down or in place)'.
segments.s166-g385: گذاریم is first-person plural optative/subjunctive meaning 'let us (do)'; it governs the following duration phrase یک چند 'a short while' (here quantifying how long). The conjunction و links to باشیم (subjunctive/imperative 'let us be'), and شاد is the predicative adjective 'happy/merry' completing باشیم.
segments.s166-g386: چو is the temporal subordinating particle 'when'; آیدت is the present/past-form verb with clitic 't' ('comes to you' / 'befalls you'). از introduces the source/possessor phrase, شهر آباد is 'the prosperous city' (شهر 'city' + آباد 'prosperous/inhabited'), and یاد is the noun 'memory/remembrance' that is the thing which 'comes to you'.
segments.s166-g387: The clause begins with the preposition به + noun pattern (به رامش) meaning 'in/with tranquility' followed by بباش, an imperative/jussive form 'be' (addressing someone). و coordinates the next phrase, به شادی 'in joy', and خرام is a jussive/imperative verb 'stride/prance' describing how the addressee should move; the subject ('you') is omitted as normal in Persian imperatives.
segments.s166-g388: می‌و جام is a poetic coordination meaning 'wine (می) and cup (جام)' treated as the theme; با من 'with me' attaches to that phrase. چرا is the interrogative 'why' and شد is the past-tense verb 'became' (or 'did it become'), so the sequence چرا شد حرام asks 'why did (it) become forbidden' — حرام being the predicate 'forbidden'.
segments.s166-g389: برآراست is a past/perfective verb meaning 'set up/arranged' (here 'prepared' or 'made ready'); گرسیوز is the proper-name subject. دام ساز is a compound-like phrase built from دام 'trap' and ساز 'device/means' — together indicating the object 'a trap' or 'one who makes/sets a trap' (i.e. he prepared a snare).
segments.s166-g390: This is a two-part descriptive phrase in parallel: دلی پر ز کین = 'a heart full of kīn' and سری پر ز راز = 'a head/secret (سری) full of secrets.' پر ز is the poetic construction 'full of' (پر 'full' + ز 'of'), linking the noun to the quality (کین 'hatred/vengeance', راز 'secret'). The coordinating و connects the two parallel members.
segments.s166-g391: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; نزدیک 'near' modifies the following noun phrase شهر سیاوش 'the city of Siyâvash'. رسید is the past tense 'arrived/reached' (here 'came near' or 'reached near to'), so the whole segment means 'when (he) came near Siyâvash's city.'
segments.s166-g392: ز (poetic از) marks source/'out of' — ز لشکر 'from the army'. زبان‌آوری here names the role/quality related to speech (literally 'speech/eloquence') used as the thing chosen (i.e. a spokesman or someone to speak). برگزید is the past-tense verb 'chose/selected'. Together: 'he chose from the army (someone) for speaking/eloquence.'
segments.s166-g265: سپردم is a first‑person past tense verb 'I entrusted/handed over'. بدو is the dative clitic phrase 'to him/unto it' (built from به + دومحور) and follows the verb. کشور و گنج خویش is the direct object: کشور 'realm/country' and گنج 'treasure' are coordinated by و, and خویش is the possessive construction (خوی + ش) marking 'my/one's own'. Word order is verb — indirect object — direct objects, which is common in older Persian poetic prose.
segments.s166-g266: نکردیم is the past negative of کردن in first‑person plural 'we did not (do/make)'. یاد here is a noun 'remembrance/mention' and sits after نکردیم as its object (i.e. ‘we did not make mention’). از governs the nouns that follow and here means 'of/about' (concerning), linking یاد to غم و رنج خویش 'our sorrow and suffering' (خوی + ش again marking possessive). The clause thus literally says 'we did not make remembrance of our sorrow and suffering.'
segments.s166-g267: به + خون is a prepositional phrase 'by/with blood' (به here functions as an instrument/means — 'by'). نیز means 'also/likewise'. پیوستگی is a noun 'connection/bond' and ساختم is the first‑person past 'I made/built', so the clause reads 'I also made/established a bond by blood.' The verb follows its noun object (پیوستگی) in typical poetic order.
segments.s166-g268: دل is the noun 'heart'. از here means 'of/from' and introduces the source/content of that emotion: کین 'hatred/vengeance' + ایران 'Iran'. بپرداختم is the first‑person past of پرداختن with an object introduced by از; پرداختن can mean 'to deal with, to rid/attend to' in literary use, so here it conveys 'I dealt with/dispensed with my heart's hatred of Iran' (i.e. I let go of or removed that enmity).
segments.s166-g269: بپیچیدم is a first‑person past (ب + پیچیدم) meaning 'I turned (aside)/I withdrew' in poetic usage. از governs the objects that follow: جنگ 'war' and فرزند 'child/offspring'. روی here is a noun 'face' and appears after فرزند to form a descriptive phrase ('a child of [noble] face') — poetic ordering places the modifier after the noun in this line.
segments.s166-g270: گرامی is an attributive adjective 'honored/dear'. دو دیده is the idiomatic epithet 'two‑eyed' (a conventional praise referring to someone's beloved or noble one). سپردم repeats the first‑person past 'I entrusted/handed over'. بدوی here is problematic: as written it could be read as an adjective 'primitive/original' (senses supplied) but in context it functions like a dative/goal ('to him' or 'to those'), so its precise lexical sense is ambiguous in the provided sense list; the line thus means 'I entrusted the honored, two‑eyed one to him.'
segments.s166-g271: پس از is the compound preposition 'after' (پس here = 'after', از = 'from/of' in the phrase). نیکویها is the plural 'good/excellent deeds/qualities' and هرگونه رنج means 'any kind of suffering' ('هرگونه' = 'any kind', رنج = 'suffering/hardship'). Together the phrase sets the temporal/conditional background: 'after virtues and all manner of suffering.'
segments.s166-g272: فدی here denotes 'sacrifice/ransom' (a noun) and کردن is the light/auxiliary verb forming the verbal phrase 'to sacrifice' (فدی کردن). کشور و تاج و گنج are the coordinated objects 'realm/country, crown, and treasure' — things one would give up or sacrifice. The construction has an elliptical quality in poetry: the actor is implied (I would sacrifice).
segments.s166-g97: A nominal phrase naming the actors. سواران is the plural noun 'horsemen/riders', immediately followed by the proper name گرسیوز (Garsivaz) and then the compound دام‌ساز (دام + ساز) meaning 'trap-making' or 'trappers'. Poetic word order allows these modifiers to sit after the head noun without an explicit ezafe; together they mean 'Garsivaz's horsemen, who set traps.'
segments.s166-g98: برفتند is a 3rd-person plural past tense verb ('they went/advanced'). The prepositional phrase با نیزه‌های دراز follows, with با introducing the instrument ('with') and the adjective دراز following the plural noun نیزه‌های (typical Persian adjective-after-noun order). The whole segment reads 'they advanced with long spears.'
segments.s166-g99: فراوان is an adverb/adjectival adverb meaning 'in great numbers/plentifully' and modifies the verb بگشتند (3rd-person plural past 'they turned/returned'), while گرد followed by زره is a circumlocution meaning 'around the armor' (گرد + noun = 'around X'). The clause thus means 'many turned/circled around the armor.'
segments.s166-g100: ز is the poetic form of از ('from'); میدان is the place 'the field/arena'; نه is the negator 'not'; بر شد is a compound where بر functions as a verbal prefix ('up/off/away') and شد is the past auxiliary 'became/was' — together here 'came off' or 'was lifted/loosed'. زره یک گره is the object phrase 'a single knot/fastening of the armor' (note the poetic noun + numeral/quantifier order). Altogether: 'From the field not a single knot of the armor came off.'
segments.s166-g101: A simple SOV clause: سیاوش (subject, proper name) + سپر (direct object 'shield') + خواست (past tense 'asked/wanted'). The trailing phrase گیلی چهار is a postpositive specification (poetic order) describing the shield as 'گیلی' (lit. 'clayey' or of a certain material/quality) and 'چهار' ('four'), i.e. a shield described as four-fold or four-part.
segments.s166-g102: This gives the shield's composition. دو چوبین = 'two (of them) wooden' where the noun ('shields' or parts) is omitted and چوبین ('wooden') follows the numeral (Persian allows numeral + adjective to stand for 'two wooden ones'). و links the second half, and ز آهن uses the poetic ز = از ('of/from iron') with آبدار as a qualificative adjective after آهن. Grammatically, the sequence lists the four components: two wooden and two iron (tempered).
segments.s166-g103: کمان خواست is another S-O-V clause: 'he asked for a bow.' The instrument phrase با تیرهای خدنگ follows, with با introducing the arrows used ('with') and خدنگ qualifying تیرهای — here 'خدنگ' is an adjective-like word meaning 'dagger-like/barbed', so تیرهای خدنگ = 'barbed/pointed arrows.'
segments.s166-g104: This clause gives the shooting action and its results. شش (six) is the numeral placed before the locative phrase اندر میان ('into/into the midst'), followed by زد (past 'he struck/shot') — Persian allows numeral + locative + verb to express 'he shot six into the middle.' The remainder, سه چوبه به تنگ, reads as the object/result: 'three (of the) posts/poles at the narrow (pass/defile)' — چوبه = 'post/pole/beam' and به تنگ indicates location 'at the narrow place.'
segments.s166-g369: چنین (thus) sets the clause as a consequence or continuation. تا + past verb برآمد ('arose') here has a temporal/conditional sense: 'when/until ... arose.' برین is a poetic compound (بر + ین) that qualifies روزگار and means 'that/aforementioned' (i.e., 'that time'); روزگار is the head noun 'time/age.' Word order is typical poetic Persian: temporal clause 'چنین تا برآمد برین روزگار' = 'thus, when that time arose.'
segments.s166-g370: پر از + noun means 'full of ...' (پر = full, از marks the substance 'of'). درد و کین are coordinated nouns 'pain and hatred/vengeance.' شد is the past of شدن used as 'became' (resultative), and دل شهریار is the subject in the verbless order common in poetry (literal order: 'full of X became the heart of the king' = 'the king's heart became full of X').
segments.s166-g371: سپهبد is the subject ('the spahbed, general'). چنین modifies دید and the following clause ('thus'). دید here is a past verb; in context it functions idiomatically as 'formed/held (an opinion or plan)' — literally 'saw a counsel.' یک روز is a temporal adverbial 'one day' and رای is the direct object 'opinion/plan/counsel.' The order is Subject — manner — Verb — object.
segments.s166-g372: که introduces a subordinate content clause giving the content of that counsel: 'that ...'. پردخت is a noun here (poetic/archaic) — most straightforwardly read as 'payment/tribute' (though the word has other historical senses); ماند is past 'remained/was left.' ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of,' and بیگانه جای = 'the stranger's place' (بیگانه = stranger/foreigner, جای = place/position). The clause literally: 'that the payment remained from (in) the stranger's place.'
segments.s166-g373: به introduces the indirect object, here the addressee 'to Garsivaz.' این داستان is the direct object 'this story/tale.' برگشاد is a poetic past form (برگشود/برگشاد) meaning 'opened' or 'unfolded/revealed' — here 'recounted' or 'related.' Word order is 'to Garsivaz this tale he revealed.'
segments.s166-g374: ز marks source: 'from the affair/deed of Siavash.' کار سیاوش = 'the affair/deeds of Siavash.' بسی is an adverb meaning 'much' or 'many' (classical usage) and is followed by کرد یاد — a periphrastic expression 'made remembrance' = 'recalled/remembered.' The verb cluster 'کرد یاد' literally 'made remembrance' functions as 'remembered.'
segments.s166-g375: ترا is the clitic object 'to you' shown orthographically as ت + را (enclosed here as two items). گفت is the past 'said/told.' ز ایدر = 'from here' (ز = from/of, ایدر = here). بباید شدن is a modal construction: بباید ('must/ought') + شدن ('to become/happen') yields an obligation passive/impersonal sense — 'it is necessary that (you) be made to go / depart' or more simply 'you must go/leave.' The clause reports what was told to 'you.'
segments.s166-g376: بر او = 'toward/for him' (literally 'upon him'). فراوان is an adverb/adjective 'abundantly/much.' نباید is the impersonal negative modal 'it should not / one must not.' بدن in the manuscript list is the noun 'body' but in this syntactic slot it functions as the lexical verb 'to give' (likely a scribal/orthographic variant of بدند/بدهند or an archaic form), so the intended meaning is 'one should not give him much.' The line therefore forbids giving him abundance.
segments.s166-g473: ندانی is the negative present 2nd-person singular form (ن + دان + ی) meaning 'you do not know' and here functions as a direct address or rhetorical remark. تو is the explicit 2nd-person pronoun; in poetry it often follows the verb for emphasis. خوی بدش is a noun phrase: خوی 'disposition' modified by بد 'bad/evil' with the enclitic possessive ش 'his' attached to بد, so literally 'his bad disposition'. بی‌گمان is a sentence adverb 'undoubtedly/surely' adding emphasis.
segments.s166-g474: بمان is an imperative/jussive 'stay/remain'. تا introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'until' and governs the verb بیاید, here a present/subjunctive meaning 'comes' or 'may come'. بدی is the object 'evil/harm'; را is the definite‑object marker marking زمان 'time' (زمان is the noun 'time/occasion') as the thing whose coming is awaited.
segments.s166-g475: نخستین is an ordinal/adjective 'first'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. اغریرث is a poetic noun/name (here left untranslated as a proper poetic term). اندازه گیر is a two-part phrase where اندازه 'measure' is followed by گیر (agent noun ‘taker’), yielding 'measure‑taker' or 'one who takes/sets a measure'—together functioning as a descriptive compound modifying or linked to the preceding noun phrase.
segments.s166-g476: که is a relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing the relative clause. بر دست او is an agent/instrument phrase ('by/at his hand' — بر + دست + possessive او). کشته شد is a passive construction: کشته (past participle 'killed/slain') plus شد (copula/auxiliary 'became/was'), so 'was killed'. خیره is an adjective/participle 'staring/astonished' and خیر here is a noun 'goodness' (the two words form an adjectival/comment clause 'astonished at the good').
segments.s166-g477: برادر بد is a noun phrase 'the evil/wicked brother' (noun + adjective). از کالبد is a prepositional phrase 'from the body' (از + کالبد). هم 'also/even' adds emphasis; ز (poetic variant of از) + پشت 'from behind' specify source or direction. The word order is typical poetic Persian: modifiers and prepositional phrases follow the head for emphasis.
segments.s166-g478: چنان is a discourse adverb 'thus/so'. پرخرد is a compound adjective 'por-kherd' (literally 'full of kherd') used here descriptively (sense choices allow 'very wise' or 'very small'; contextually it functions as an epithet). بیگنه is an adjectival/noun-form meaning roughly 'the hapless/alien/strange one' and را is the object marker attaching to that noun. بکشت is an archaic/perfective verb form 'killed' (poetic past) where the b- prefix appears in older forms.
segments.s166-g479: ازان پس is a temporal/discourse sequence 'after that/thereafter'. بسی here functions as an intensifier 'many/much' (classical usage 'basi' = 'a great many'). نامور 'renowned' modifies the following noun, and بی‌گناه 'innocent/blameless' further describes them; together they form the plural noun phrase 'many renowned innocents'.
segments.s166-g480: شدستند is a compound/poetic form meaning 'they became' (3rd person plural of شدن) indicating a resulting state. بر دست او is an idiomatic phrase 'into his hand / into his power' where دست carries the sense of 'power/control' rather than merely 'hand'. بر تباه places them 'upon ruin' or 'ruined'—together giving 'they fell into his power and were ruined.'
segments.s166-g49: This is a verbless nominal clause: همش 'all of it' (topic) + پادشاهیست 'is kingship' (predicate). The following هم ... و ... string lists parts of that kingship: هم introduces 'also/both' and ties تاج ('crown') to گاه (here a poetic noun meaning a seat/place, i.e. 'throne'). Word order places the copular idea as a single phrase 'همش پادشاهیست' followed by the constituents in apposition.
segments.s166-g50: Parallel continuation of the previous apposition: همش 'all of it' again heads the list, followed by coordinated nouns 'گنج / دانش / سپاه' connected with و and repeated هم for emphasis ('all treasure, all learning, and all the army'). The repeated هم emphasizes that each item is fully included.
segments.s166-g51: نهان modifies دل ('hidden heart') and خویش is the possessive element ('his' / 'his own') realized as خوی + ش; پیدا کردن here means 'to make apparent' or 'to show'. The verb نکرد (negated past) at the end gives the meaning 'he did not reveal/make apparent'.
segments.s166-g52: همی ... بود is a simple past progressive/continuative ('he was still/continued to be'). پیچان is an adjectival predicate meaning 'twisted/troubled' and is joined with رخساره زرد ('face/complexion pale') by و; adjective زرد follows the noun رخساره as its attribute.
segments.s166-g53: بدو گفت is the dative-fronting construction common in older/poetic Persian: 'to him he said' (بدو = 'to him/unto him', گفت = 'he said'). برخوردی functions here as a noun meaning 'a portion/relief/benefit' (a single encounter/relief), followed by از رنج خویش 'from his suffering' (خویش = his/own, split as خوی + ش).
segments.s166-g54: This is the continuation or content of the speech: همه سال 'all year' (here 'the whole year' / 'throughout the year'), شادان دل 'joyful-hearted' (compound attributive: adjective + noun), followed by از گنج خویش 'because of/from his treasure' — note again خویش formed from خوی + ش indicating possession.
segments.s166-g55: نهادند is a past plural verb 'they placed / they set' (object follows): نهادند در کاخ زرین دو تخت = 'they placed two thrones in the golden palace.' Prepositional phrase در کاخ زرین marks location, and دو تخت is the direct object (two thrones).
segments.s166-g56: نشستند is past plural 'they sat / sat down' (verb); the following phrase شادان دل 'joyful-hearted' describes their disposition, and نیک‌بخت is an adjectival/nominal epithet 'fortunate' applied to them. The sentence links the action of sitting with their joyful and fortunate state.
segments.s166-g353: پس (adverb) at the start is a connective meaning 'then/therefore'. The sequence افراسیاب (proper name) + اندر آن (prepositional phrase 'in/within that') gives the location, followed by بسته (past participle 'closed/enclosed') and شد (past of شدن) used here to mark the change/state 'became/was' — together 'was enclosed there'. Note اندر is an archaic/poetic preposition.
segments.s166-g354: غمی گشت is a small clause where گشت (become, turn) indicates the arising of a state: 'grief arose' or 'he became sorrowful'; the subject (he/Afrasiab) is omitted and understood. و links the next clause اندیشه پیوسته شد, where پیوسته (adjective/adverb 'continuous/constant') follows the noun اندیشه and شد ('became') marks the change of state: 'his thought became continuous/constant.'
segments.s166-g355: همی is a classical adverb here (sense 'still/continually' or an emphatic marker). از شتابش is an ablative/genitive phrase 'from his haste'; به آمد is a classical ordering where به (dative/locative particle) precedes آمد ('came') and درنگ (noun 'pause/delay') is the thing that 'came' — together: 'a pause came to/over his haste' or more naturally 'he paused / a delay came upon his haste.'
segments.s166-g356: که introduces a result or purpose clause ('so that/that'). پیروز باشد uses باشد as a present/subjunctive-form verb meaning 'may be / is (to be) victorious' — classical Persian often uses باشد to express wish or result. خداوند سنگ is an epithet/title-like noun phrase; syntactically it reads as 'the lord/sovereign (خداوند) of the stone (سنگ)' or 'stone-lord' in poetic usage, functioning as the subject of the clause.
segments.s166-g357: In classical word order the predicate ستوده نباشد ('is not praised' / 'should not be praised') precedes the subject phrase سر بادسار ('the head of the wind-lord' or 'wind‑chief's head'). نباشد is the negative present/subjunctive form used in narrative or gnomic statements.
segments.s166-g358: بدین is the classical contracted form (به + این) meaning 'with/unto this' or 'thus'; here the annotator split it into ب + دین, but دین as listed does not match 'این' — that token is therefore annotated below with -1. داستان is the object 'tale/story'. زد (from زدن) is used idiomatically here meaning 'struck' or better in narrative 'began/sounded/told' the tale. یکی هوشیار is an indefinite subject 'a certain alert/wise man.' Word order places the verb زد before the agent phrase, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s166-g359: که‌گر (classical compound 'که گر') introduces a conditional 'if'. باد خیره combines the noun 'wind' with the adjective خیره ('dazed/blind/astonished') modifying it. بجستی is a past-form (perfective) meaning 'you were moved/you leapt/you were carried' (subject is second person implicit from context). ز جای ('from your place') is the source phrase. The whole clause is a conditional protasis with omitted explicit subject.
segments.s166-g360: نماندی is the past/perfective negative-form 'you would not remain / did not remain' with the same implied second-person subject carried over from the conditional. The series بر و بیشه و پر و پای is an enumerative list linked by و ('on/over' or various loci/things: 'on (rock/ground) and grove and feather/wing and foot'), and the verb precedes these items as the predicate in classical style: 'you would not remain on X or Y or Z.'
segments.s166-g449: و‌گر is an archaic conditional particle meaning “and if.” دشمنی is the noun subject ‘hostility/enmity.’ آمدستت is a contracted/perfective verb phrase: آمدست (has come/arrived) with the enclitic -ت ‘to you,’ so ‘has come upon you.’ پدید is a predicate/adverb meaning ‘manifest/appeared’ and here completes the sense ‘has appeared.’ Word order is slightly inverted (verb+predicate) as typical in poetry.
segments.s166-g450: که introduces a subordinate clause (‘such that/that’) describing the consequence. تیمار ‘care, solicitude’ and رنجش ‘suffering’ are coordinated by و. بباید is the present/obligative auxiliary ‘must/ought (to),’ and کشید is the infinitival/root form of کشیدن here meaning ‘to bear/endure’ — the lexical sense ‘to endure’ is not among the listed nominal senses, so it is irregular in theprovided list.
segments.s166-g451: من is the emphatic first‑person pronoun ‘I’; اینک is the adverb ‘now.’ به هر کار is a prepositional phrase ‘in/for every affair/matter.’ یار توام is a predicative phrase: یار ‘companion/ally’ plus توام, a fused verbal/copulative form meaning ‘I am with you / I am your …’ (classical Persian suffix -ام attached to a pronominal base), so ‘I am your companion.’
segments.s166-g452: چو here means ‘when’ (a temporal conditional). جنگ آوری is a second‑person verbal phrase ‘you bring/you take to war’ (i.e. ‘you go to war’). مایه‌دار is an adjective 'wealthy/rich in resources' and with توام (‘I am…’) yields ‘I am your resource/provider’ or ‘I am rich for you’ — poetically ‘I will supply you in war.’ Word order places the temporal clause before the result clause.
segments.s166-g453: ور is an older conditional particle equivalent to ‘if.’ ایدونک is an adverbial/poetic particle meaning roughly ‘now/at this moment’ (its exact shade is archaic). نزدیک qualifies the following name and means ‘near,’ so نزدیک افراسیاب = ‘Afrasiab is near.’ The clause as a whole is a protasis (‘if now Afrasiab is near’).
segments.s166-g454: ترا is the combination of 2nd‑person enclitic -ت plus the object/topic marker را: here functioning together as ‘to/for you.’ تیره is a predicate adjective ‘dark/ominous.’ گشتست is the perfective/passive‑style form ‘has become/has turned.’ بر is the preposition ‘upon/on,’ and خیره آب is a prepositional phrase ‘on/over the (staring/calm) water’ — خیره is an adjective ‘astonished/staring’ used poetically with آب ‘water.’ Together: ‘a dark fate has become yours upon the water.’
segments.s166-g455: به گفتار is a prepositional phrase ‘according to the speech/words (of).’ مرد is the noun ‘man/person.’ دروغ آزمای is a nominal phrase formed from دروغ ‘falsehood/lie’ plus آزمای (verbal stem meaning ‘to test’ used adjectivally): together they describe the man as one who ‘tests (or tries) by falsehood’ or as a ‘tester of lies’ — a compact poetic compound attributing the act of testing/lying to the man.
segments.s166-g456: کسی is the indefinite pronoun ‘someone.’ برتر از تو is a comparative phrase ‘higher/better than you.’ گرفتست is the perfective ‘has taken/has seized,’ and جای is the noun ‘place/position/post.’ The sequence states that ‘someone superior to you has taken the position.’
segments.s166-g273: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is a poetic/temporal particle meaning 'now/at such a time' and modifies the whole protasis. من is the (emphatic) subject 'I'. بدسگالم is an adjective with the pronominal/attributive suffix -م ('ill‑fated (I)') describing the speaker. The clause is elliptical (the copula or modal verb is implied) and links forward to the result clause in the next segment.
segments.s166-g274: ز + گیتی برآید is a prepositional phrase + verb: 'arise/come forth from the world' (برآید is 3rd sing. present/subjunctive or jussive 'should arise/appear'). یکی is indefinite 'someone/a certain one'. گفت و گوی here is the compound for speech/conversation ('someone will speak' / 'someone will have words'); word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s166-g275: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him' (direction or beneficiary). بر بهانه = 'as an excuse / on the pretext of'. ندارم is the present negative 'I do not have' (subject pronoun omitted). ببد is an archaic/past form (here parenthetical/copular) meaning 'it was' / 'would be', used elliptically — the clause sets up the speaker's lack of excuse.
segments.s166-g276: گر introduces a conditional 'if'. از من بدو is a directional phrase 'from me to him' (از = from, من = me, بدو = to him). اندکی is an adverb 'a little'. بد is a noun/adjective 'ill/bad' meaning harm or misfortune here. رسد is 3rd singular present/subjunctive/indicative 'reaches' or 'befalls' — together: 'if from me to him a little harm should reach/befall'.
segments.s166-g277: زبان 'tongues/speech' is the nominal subject. برگشایند is 3rd plural present/jussive 'may they open / they open' used optatively ('may ... open'). بر من is the prepositional phrase 'against me'. مهان 'guests' is the agent/actor; word order is inverted for poetic effect (normally مهان زبان را بر من برگشایند).
segments.s166-g278: درفشی 'banner / standard' names the role; شوم is 1st person singular optative/subjunctive 'may I become' or 'I may be' (here an ill wish/curse on the speaker). در میان جهان is a locative phrase 'in the midst of the world.' The clause expresses an adverse wish about the speaker's reputation.
segments.s166-g279: نباشد is a negative third‑person verb form 'would not be / is not' (here used predicatively). پسند is the noun 'pleasure/approval' and جهان‌آفرین is a genitival title 'Creator of the world' (the possessor of the پسند). The phrase means 'this would not be to the liking of the Creator.' Word order is inverted for emphasis.
segments.s166-g280: نه نیز functions as 'nor/also not' (نه = not, نیز = also/likewise). از بزرگان is 'by/from the great ones' (i.e. the great men). روی زمین is a fixed locative 'upon the face of the earth' — together: 'nor (would it be pleasing) to the great ones on the earth.'
segments.s166-g281: This clause states an existential negative with a comparative. ز is a poetic variant of از ('from/than') introducing the comparison; دد 'enemy' is the class being compared, and تیزدندان‌تر is the comparative adjective formed with the suffix -تر ('more sharp‑toothed'). از شیر marks the standard of comparison ('than the lion'). The clause ends with نیست, the negative copula 'is not/does not exist.'
segments.s166-g282: که introduces a subordinate explanatory clause ('for/that'). اندر دلش is a locative phrase 'in his/its heart' (اندر = in; ش = enclitic 'his/its'). بیم شمشیر is a genitive/compound meaning 'fear of the sword' (بیم + noun), and نیست negates it ('there is no'). The whole clause gives the reason for the first clause.
segments.s166-g283: اگر marks a conditional 'if.' بچه‌ای is an indefinite noun 'an offspring/child' (the -ی gives indefiniteness). از پدر uses از in the causal sense 'because of/from the father' and دردمند is a predicate adjective 'distressed/in pain.' In classical Persian the adjective can follow the noun without an explicit copula.
segments.s166-g284: کند is the present/simple verb 'does/makes' used here to state the outcome of the condition (a general or habitual result); it can carry a sense similar to 'will' in conditional contexts. مرغزارش is a possessed noun 'its meadow' (مرغزار + enclitic ش), and پناه از گزند is a noun phrase 'a refuge from harm' where از marks the source/what to be protected from. The verb plus this noun phrase means 'makes/serves as a refuge.'
segments.s166-g285: This is an interrogative built with the impersonal/adjectival سزد‌گر 'is it fitting/proper'. The subordinate clause uses آید 'comes/happens' plus the clitic بدو 'to him' to express 'evil comes to him,' and از پناه 'from (his) refuge' indicates the source. The sentence asks rhetorically whether such an event would be proper.
segments.s166-g286: پسندد is a verb 'would [he] approve/like'; the subject is the following noun phrase داور هور و ماه ('the judge of hور and moon') — a poetic or divine judge. چنین modifies the whole proposition ('such/this way'). The word order is inverted (verb before subject) as is common in poetic Persian questions.
segments.s166-g287: ندانم is the negative present 'I do not know.' جز introduces the exception 'except/other than,' and آنکش functions here as a substantive/adjective 'that (formidable) one' — an emphatic referent; بخوانم is the first-person present/subjunctive form of خواندن used in the sense 'to summon/call' (sense 2) and به در means 'to the court/door' (به = to, در = gate/palace/royal presence). The clause says 'I know no one except that one to call to the court.'
segments.s166-g288: وز here begins the second coordinate action and functions like 'and/from' (classical/written contraction of و + از); ایدر is 'here.' فرستمش is the verb 'I send (him)' with the enclitic object -ش; نزد پدر means 'to/into the presence of the father' (نزد = in the presence of). The sequence expresses the speaker's intended action: 'and from here I will send him to his father.'
segments.s166-g113: بدو گفت is a short clause meaning “he said to him.” بدو is the prepositional form (be + pronoun) ‘to him/unto him,’ placed before the verb for emphasis; گفت is the past tense 3rd-person singular of گفتن (‘said’). گرسیوز‌ای شهریار is a vocative: the name گرسیوز (Garsivaz) followed by ای + شهریار (= “O king”), addressing him directly.
segments.s166-g114: به ایران و توران ترا نیست یار is a negated existential statement: به + place names gives the domain (‘in/among Iran and Turan’), ترا is the 2nd-person clitic + topical/object marker (ت + را) meaning ‘for you,’ نیست is the predicate ‘is not/there is not,’ and یار is the noun ‘ally/companion.’ Word order places the domain first, then the indirect object (ترا), then the negated copula and noun.
segments.s166-g115: بیا تا opens a purpose/hortatory clause: بیا (imperative/hortative “come” or “let us”) + تا (‘so that/in order that’). من و تو is the coordinated subject ‘I and you,’ and به آوردگاه is the directional phrase ‘to the battlefield’ (به + noun). The clause sets up purpose for the following verb.
segments.s166-g116: بتازیم is a first-person-plural subjunctive/hortative form (the older b- prefix with present-stem) meaning ‘let us rush/charge’ or ‘may we ride swiftly.’ هر دو (‘both of us’) emphasizes the two subjects. به پیش سپاه is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘before/in front of the host/army’ (به + پیش + سپاه), with پیش used as ‘before/ahead (of).’
segments.s166-g117: بگیریم is the 1st-person-plural subjunctive/hortative of گرفتن here meaning ‘let us take/gird’ (used idiomatically with کمر = ‘gird the loins’). هردو + دوال (‘both, the twofold’) reinforces that the action applies to both persons. کمر is the object ‘waist/loins’ (the thing being girded); the verb and object together express ‘let us gird our loins.’
segments.s166-g118: به کردار جنگی is an adverbial phrase: به used here to mean ‘in/by’ (manner), کردار ‘deed/behavior’ with جنگی qualifying it as ‘warlike’ or ‘for war’ — ‘in the manner of war/for war.’ دو پرخاشخر follows as a description: دو = ‘two,’ and پرخاشخر is an adjective/noun meaning ‘quarrelsome/aggressive one,’ so the phrase describes them as ‘two quarrelsome/fierce fighters.’
segments.s166-g119: ز ترکان is a genitive/partitive phrase ‘of/from the Turks’ (used as ‘among the Turks’). مرا is split here into M + را in the token list; syntactically مرا = ‘me/for me’ marks the person compared. نیست is the negated existential/copular verb ‘is not/there is not,’ and همتاکسی is the noun ‘an equal/one of equal rank.’ The whole line says literally ‘Of the Turks for me there is not an equal,’ i.e. ‘among the Turks there is no one my equal.’
segments.s166-g120: چو here functions as a temporal/conditional ‘when/if.’ اسپم (‘my steed’) is the direct object of the following verb; نبینی is the negative 2nd-person present/subjunctive of دیدن used as ‘you do not/will not see.’ ز اسپان بسی literally ‘from among horses many’ — بسی is an intensifier/quantifier (here ‘many’/‘much’), so the clause says ‘when you see my steed, you will not see many like it among horses.’
segments.s166-g345: تو خوانی که is a reporting clause: تو (you) + خوانی (present-tense verb 'call/declare') and که introduces the subordinate content clause. ایـدر (here) is an adverb placed before the clause's object, and مرا را is the object in old poetic order (مـ + را split here) with را marking definiteness/emphasis; بنده باش is a jussive/imperative phrase ('be a servant') with باش as the copular-imperative following the predicative noun بنده.
segments.s166-g346: This is a coordinated predicate introduced by two prepositional phrases headed by به: به خواری and به مهر من. Each به marks a circumstantial role ('in/with'), آگنده is a participial/adjectival predicate meaning 'filled', and باش is the imperative/coprular verb ('be'); together they mean 'be filled with humiliation and with my love.' Note مهر من places the possessor after the noun (my love).
segments.s166-g347: ندیدست is a classical perfect/past-structure with negative sense 'has not been seen'; here it carries the negation and is followed by کس used in the sense 'no one' (classical word order places the negated verb before the subject). جفت (pair/match) takes the following با + noun phrase (با پیل شیر = 'with an elephant and a lion' understood as the two members of the pair).
segments.s166-g348: نه here functions as a negative particle (neither/ nor) introducing contrasted nouns. دمان (an older word for 'skirt/hem' or edge) follows آتش specifying 'fire on the hem/edge' — از + بر denotes position 'from/on (the) top', and آب زیر uses زیر 'under/below' to indicate the lower element. The prepositions/particles are archaic/poetic positional uses rather than modern idiomatic phrases.
segments.s166-g349: اگر introduces a conditional clause. بچه شیر is a compound noun 'lion-cub' (بچه + شیر), and ناخورده is a prefixed negative participle ('not having eaten/drunk') placed postpositively to qualify the cub (i.e. 'not yet suckled'). The final شیر here is the homograph repeating the word with a different sense (see senses mapping): the two شیر forms serve different roles in the phrase.
segments.s166-g350: بپوشد is a third-person singular present/subjunctive (classical) meaning 'covers / clothes' (here in the protasis of the condition). کسی is an indefinite subject 'someone', and در میان حریر is a locative phrase 'in the midst of silk' (در = in, میان = midst/middle, حریر = silk); the order places the agent before the location, as typical in Persian clauses.
segments.s166-g351: به گوهر شود is a resultative phrase: به + noun (گوهر 'jewel/essence') + شود (becomes). باز 'again' follows the verb for emphasis. چون شد here is the classical use of چون as temporal 'when/as' (classical prose/verse usage), with شد repeating 'became'; سترگ is a noun 'sight/eye' used predicatively ('when the eye/sight became large/aware'). The sequence is a poetic, somewhat inverted, temporal/result clause.
segments.s166-g352: نترسد is negated present/future ('will not fear' / 'does not fear') formed with the negative prefix ن-. ز (poetic form of از) marks origin/reason 'from/at'; آهنگ in this context means 'onset/approach/charge' (an old poetic sense derived from 'aim/intention'); پیل بزرگ is the noun phrase 'great elephant' serving as the source/agent of that approach.
segments.s166-g169: بیامد is a 3rd-person singular simple past (archaic literary form) meaning 'he came'. بران is an adverbial/relative particle here meaning 'on that' or 'regarding that' and attaches to the following noun phrase. کار بسته میان is a noun phrase: کار 'affair' followed by the adjective/participial بَسته 'bound/settled' (past-participle used attributively), with میان 'between' indicating that the affair is between parties (the object of میان is omitted and understood from context).
segments.s166-g170: به نزد is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to the presence of' (به = 'to', نزد = 'near / in the presence of'). جهانجوی is an epithet 'world-seeking' (adjective) modifying شاه. شاه کیان is the title 'the Kayan king' (کیان used as dynastic/title word); the adjective precedes the noun as is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s166-g171: سیاوش is the subject (proper name). بورد is an archaic past form meaning 'he took' (بُرد/بورد), here a simple past verb. بنهاد is another past verb (بنهادَ/بنهاد) meaning 'he placed/put' or 'he set' and here governs روی 'face' as its object; together the two verbs indicate successive actions ('took' and then 'set his face'). روی is the noun 'face' (literal or idiomatic 'turned his face/toward').
segments.s166-g172: برفتند is 3rd-person plural past 'they went/they departed'. پیچان is an adverb/adjectival participle meaning 'turning/twisting' (describing the manner). دمور here is a proper name (the same Dâmur mentioned in the context) rather than the dictionary senses provided. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. گروی in this line functions as a noun meaning 'company/band/followers' (poetic form related to گروه/گرو) rather than the verb sense in the lemma list.
segments.s166-g173: ببند is an imperative (2nd-person singular or general imperative form) meaning 'close (in)' or 'bind/fasten' used here as a command or narrative exhortation. میان is a preposition/adverb 'among / between'. گروی (poetic form of گروه/گرو) functions as 'the band/company/host' in this context; زره 'armor' is the noun that specifies among what they should close in.
segments.s166-g174: فرو is an adverbial prefix/adverb meaning 'downward / inwards' and combines with the following verb to show direction or force. برد here is used as a past action 'he bore/drove' (classical past of بردن), so فرو + برد gives the sense 'drove down' or 'thrust in'. چنگال is the object 'talon / claw'. و is 'and'. برزد is an archaic past verb meaning here 'struck' or 'smote' (the lemma's dictionary senses are archaic; context shows an active transitive sense). گره is the object 'knot' or 'node' struck.
segments.s166-g175: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. زین 'saddle' is the noun of origin. برگرفتش is the past-tense complex برگرفت + -ش (enclitic) meaning 'he took it/up' or idiomatically 'he sprang up' — here it indicates rising from the saddle. به میدان is the directional phrase 'into the field/arena' (به = 'to/into', میدان = 'field/arena'), and فگند is an archaic past form 'he threw' (فَگَند = 'cast/hurled'), used here with the subject understood from the previous verb.
segments.s166-g176: نیازش is نیاز + enclitic -ش meaning 'his need' (or 'he had no need'); نیامد literally 'did not come' is used idiomatically as 'was not necessary / was not needed'. به introduces the instruments 'for / in the way of' and is followed by a coordination گرز و کمند (گرز 'mace/club', کمند 'lasso/snare'), meaning he did not require either weapon.
segments.s166-g33: بگویش is the imperative/commandal form بگو + enclitic -یش 'to him', so 'tell him'. که introduces a subordinate clause. هر چند is the fixed concessive phrase 'although / however (much)', here linking to the speaker's condition; من سالخورد is a predicative phrase 'I (من) aged' — the copula is omitted in this poetic register, so آن 'I am old' is understood without an explicit هست.
segments.s166-g34: This is the main clause: بدم 'my badness / my fault' (admitted condition) followed by پاک یزدان, an inverted adjective + noun 'the pure Yezdān (God)' — poetic word order puts the adjective before the divine name. مرا (here shown split as م + را) is the object 'me' with را as the definite/accusative marker, and شاد کرد is a compound verb 'made (شاد)'; کرد is the past of کردن used causatively: 'made me happy.'
segments.s166-g35: سیاوش is the proper name (subject). بدو is the allative clitic/adverbial 'to him' (به + او contracted), marking the addressee. گفت here is the past verb 'said' introducing direct speech (in the provided senses list it appears as a conversational noun form; functionally it is the past of گفتن). گاه مهی is a compact phrase 'a time/occasion (گاه) (that is) moon‑like/beautiful (مهی)' — poetically 'a moon‑like (one)' or 'a shining/beautiful presence.'
segments.s166-g36: ازین تخمه = از + این (here written از + ین) + تخمه 'from this seed/stock/offspring' (i.e. from this lineage). هرگز مبادا is a fixed negative wish 'may ... never' (هرگز = 'never', مبادا = 'may it not happen / lest'). تهی 'empty / lacking' is the predicate of the wish: 'may [it] never be lacking.' The whole clause expresses a blessing/wish that none of this lineage be without a moon‑like (person).
segments.s166-g37: فرستاده را داد چندان درم is an object phrase + verb: فرستاده را 'the messenger' with را marking the definite object, داد is the past of دادن here 'gave', and چندان درم 'that much money/so many dirhams' (چندان = 'to that extent / so much', درم = dirhams). Word order places the object before the verb, a normal option in Persian and common in poetry.
segments.s166-g38: که introduces a result clause 'so that / such that'. آرنده گشت uses گشت (past of گشتن) 'became / turned' and آرنده here means something like 'cheerful/changed (in mood)' in context (the supplied senses 'bringer/producing' are from a headword; the intended poetic sense is a change of mood). از کشیدن دژم is a prepositional phrase 'from کشیدن دژم' — کشیدن here is a verbal noun 'to draw/wear/assume' and دژم 'sullenness, gloom' — together 'from wearing/assuming sullen looks', i.e. 'ceased to be sullen.'
segments.s166-g39: به کاخ فرنگیس رفتند شاد is a locative/goal + motion clause: به کاخ فرنگیس 'to Farangis's palace' (به + noun + name), رفتند is the past plural 'they went', and شاد is an adverbial/adjective 'happy/joyful' modifying their going (poetic placement after the verb).
segments.s166-g40: This clause has an inverted word order: بدید 'saw' is the verb followed by the nominative phrase آن بزرگی فرخ نژاد. آن بزرگی = 'that great one / that greatness' (آن + a noun meaning 'great one'); فرخ نژاد is a compound adjective + noun 'fortunate-born / of lucky lineage' (فرخ = fortunate, نژاد = lineage). So literally 'saw that great, fortunate-born one.' In poetry subjects and objects often follow verbs in this way.
segments.s166-g257: ازان خواب بد is a prepositional/ablative phrase (ازان = ‘from/after that’) modifying the clause that follows. چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. دلم is دل + enclitic م (my heart) and شد (past of شدن) is the verb 'became' — دلم شد غمی = 'my heart became sorrow/grief'.
segments.s166-g258: به مغز اندر آورد is a verb phrase meaning 'brought into the mind' (به ... اندر = 'into', مغز = 'mind/brain', آورد = 'brought/caused to enter'). لختی کمی is an adverbial phrase 'for a short while' (لختی = 'a short while'; کمی = 'a little').
segments.s166-g259: نبستم is negative past (ن‑ prefix + بستم) meaning 'I did not join/enter (take part)'. به جنگ = 'into/against the war' (به + noun), سیاوش names the opposing party, and میان here means 'in the midst of / during' (middle). The whole phrase says 'I did not join/take part in Siavash's battle.'
segments.s166-g260: ازو نیز is 'from him also'; ما را uses ما with the object/topic marker را (so 'to us' / 'for us'); نیامد is the negative past 'did not come'; زیان is the affected noun 'harm/damage'. The construction is impersonal-ish: 'to us no harm came from him.'
segments.s166-g261: چو = 'when'. تخت پرمایه is a noun + adjective sequence 'the throne rich in resources' (classical word order: noun then modifier). پدرود کردن is the verbal phrase 'to bid farewell' or 'to take leave', and کرد is the past tense auxiliary 'he did/said' producing 'he bade farewell.'
segments.s166-g262: This is a two-part equative/causative image: 'خرد تار کرد' = 'he made wisdom (خرد) the warp (تار)'; verb کرد = 'made/did'. The second clause 'و مرا پود کرد' = 'and he made me the weft' — مرا is me + را (object marked), showing that 'me' is the patient of کرد. Note classical omission of را in the first equative is common.
segments.s166-g263: ز here is the poetic/prepositional 'from' (ز فرمان من = 'from my command' = 'away from / contrary to my order' depending on context). یک زمان is an adverbial 'for a time' / 'for a while'. سر نتافت is object (سر = 'head') + نتافت (negative past of تافت = 'to turn/bend'), so 'he did not turn his head.'
segments.s166-g264: چو = 'when'. از من = 'from me' (source). چنان نیکویها is a plural noun phrase 'such good things/favors' (note the plural -ها). بیافت is a classical verb meaning 'befell' or 'came upon' (here: 'such good things befell/occurred from me').
segments.s166-g321: ببینم is the first‑person subjunctive/optative of دیدن used here as a volitive 'let me see / I should see'. که introduces the indirect question ("what … is"). رای جهاندار is a genitive phrase 'the sovereign's counsel/opinion' (رای as 'counsel/plan'), and چی ست is the verbless question 'what is it' with ست as the copula 'is'.
segments.s166-g322: This clause is an independent question about direction. رخ شمع is a poetic image 'face like a candle' (شمع as metaphorical light). چرخ روان is a compounded image 'the moving heaven/sky' (چرخ = sky; روان here = moving/flowing), سوی کیست is 'toward whom is it' with کیست (کی + ست) as the interrogative 'who is (the one)'.
segments.s166-g323: وگر is the conditional 'if/and if'. سوی درگاه 'toward the court' (درگاه = royal court/presence). خوانمش is a 1st‑person singular present form with enclitic object -مش meaning 'I call/summon him' (here 'summon him'), and باز means 'again' (repeated action). Word order is poetic but logic is 'if I summon him again to the court…'.
segments.s166-g324: بجویم is the 1st‑person subjunctive/volitive of جستن 'I will seek/ask'. سخن here means 'speech / words / a question' (i.e. I will speak/ask). تا introduces purpose/result 'so that / in order that', and چه دارد به راز is 'what he has in secret' with دارد = 'he has' and به راز = 'in secret'. The whole clause expresses intent: 'I will ask to find out what secret he has.'
segments.s166-g325: Word order is inverted for emphasis: نگهبان او من = 'guardian/warder of him am I' (subject complement before the pronoun). بسم is an older/poetic short form meaning 'it is enough' (بس + م), and بی‌گمان is an adverb 'without doubt / certainly' qualifying the assertion.
segments.s166-g326: همی is a durative particle 'still / will (continually)'. بنگرم is the 1st‑person subjunctive/optative of نگرستن 'I will look/watch'. تا here means 'until' or 'so as to find out', and چه گردد زمان literally 'what the time becomes' = 'what time will bring / what will happen'.
segments.s166-g327: چو functions as 'when'. ز (poetic from از) is the preposition 'from', and و here works as a connective 'then/so' in sequence. کژیی آشکارا شود = 'a crookedness/fault is revealed' with آشکارا 'openly/evidently' and شود 'becomes/is revealed'.
segments.s166-g328: که introduces the result clause 'that/so that'. با چاره دل = 'with remedy (چاره) the heart' (دل), but here means 'even if there are remedies' or 'with remedies at hand'. بی‌مدارا is an adjectival/nominal state 'merciless, unyielding', and شود is the copular 'becomes'. The clause states the consequence: the heart grows merciless in response.
segments.s166-g225: بدو گفت = 'he said to him'. بدو is the archaic/poetic form of به + او ('to him/to her') and precedes the verb; گفت is the past tense 'said' (the sense 'to say' is not in the provided senses list, so it is treated as -1). The following words are vocatives addressing the addressee: گرسیوز‌ای شهریار = 'Garsivaz, O king'.
segments.s166-g226: سیاوش (subject) + جزان دارد (predicate) + آیین و کار (object). جزان here is a compressed classical expression meaning جُز آن (other than that / different) — i.e. 'has something other' — so دَردد (دارد) is present 'has'. آیین و کار is a coordinated noun phrase 'custom and conduct'. Word order is topic/subject first, verb + object after.
segments.s166-g227: فرستاده آمد ز کاووس شاه is a straightforward past clause: فرستاده (messenger) is the subject, آمد is the past tense verb 'came', and ز (poetic از) marks origin: 'from Kavus the king'. Word order is subject–verb–prepositional phrase.
segments.s166-g228: نهانی بنزدیک او چند گاه is an adverbial phrase. نهانی (secretly) modifies the action; بنزدیک (poetic بِنزدیک = 'close to/near') + او ('him') gives 'near him'; چند گاه = 'for some time' or 'a while' (چند = several/a few, گاه = time). The whole string says he remained secretly near him for a while.
segments.s166-g229: ز روم و ز چین نیزش آمد پیام = 'A message came to him from Rome and from China as well.' The preposition ز (from) marks origins; چین here is the country 'China' (classical usage). نیزش is نیز + enclitic -ش referring back to 'him' = 'also to him'. آمد is past 'came' and پیام = 'message/tidings'.
segments.s166-g230: همی یاد کاووس گیرد به جام is a compact classical clause: همی is a habitual particle meaning 'continually/often'; یاد گیرد literally 'takes to remembrance' = 'remembers' or 'calls to mind'; کاووس is the subject (Kavus); به جام 'at the cup' (literally 'to/into the cup') is an idiomatic locative indicating the drinking/hall setting where he recalls or speaks of the tidings. گیرْد is present/past-form here used in the classical narrative sense 'takes/holds (in memory)'.
segments.s166-g231: برو انجمن شد فراوان سپاه: برو here functions as a connective ('thereupon' / 'then' in classical usage) followed by انجمن شد 'an assembly was made/formed'. فراوان سپاه = 'a great/plentiful host (army)'. شد is the past passive/auxiliary 'became/was formed'.
