segments.s166-g232: بپیچید ازو یک زمان جان شاه is a terse classical line: بپیچید (from پیچیـدن, literally 'turned' but in military/classical usage can mean 'surrounded' or 'closed in on') + ازو ('from him' or 'of him') gives the sense of attack/assault on the person; یک زمان = 'in one moment/in an instant'; جان شاه = 'the king's life' (جان = life). Together: in an instant the king's life was taken/departed. Note the verb form is past and the clause is compressed in epic style.
segments.s166-g177: وزان پس is a classical sequence meaning 'after that / from that time' (وزان = 'from that time', پس = 'then'). بپیچید is the simple past (3rd person singular) of پیچیدن with the prefixed ب- (common in Classical Persian past forms) meaning 'he turned'. سوی is the preposition 'toward'; دمور here is a proper name/place (not one of the listed common senses) and functions as the object of سوی.
segments.s166-g178: گرفت is the past transitive 'he seized/took'. آن is the demonstrative pronoun 'him/that' as direct object. بر + گردن = برِ گردن 'upon the neck' (بر here = 'on/upon'), او is the pronominal 'his/he' clarifying whose neck, and به زور is an adverbial phrase 'by force' (به = 'by', زور = 'strength/force'). The clause order is subject-verb then object phrase and instrument/adverbial.
segments.s166-g179: چنان is the adverb 'so/thus' introducing a result clause. خوارش is خوار + ـش (the adjective 'lowly/despised' with the 3rd-person object clitic -ش = 'him'), so the phrase means roughly 'he treated him humiliatingly' or 'he humbled him'. از پشت زین 'from off the saddle' is a simple prepositional phrase (از = 'from', پشت = 'back/behind', زین = 'saddle'). برگرفت is past tense 'he lifted/removed' (برگرفتن used here in the sense 'took up / took off').
segments.s166-g180: که introduces the result clause 'so that / so ... that'. لشکر is the subject 'the army/host'. بدو is the prepositional/benefactive 'at him/to him' (showing the army's reaction directed toward that person). ماند is the past 'remained / was left' (here 'were left'), and اندر شگفت is a locative phrase 'in astonishment' (اندر = 'in/within', شگفت as noun 'wonder/amazement').
segments.s166-g181: چنان 'so' again sets up a consequence. پیش here means 'in the presence of / before', so پیش گرسیوز آورد literally 'he brought (him) before Garsivaz'. آورد is the past 3rd‑person 'brought/placed before'. خوش after the verb qualifies the manner ('pleasantly / neatly / becomingly'); adjectives after آورد describe how the action was done in Classical Persian.
segments.s166-g182: که introduces the subordinate clause. گفتی here is a noun 'saying / it is said' (an older usage), followed by ندارد 'there is not / there is no' and کسی 'anyone'. زیرکش is the noun 'undershirt/undergarment'. The full clause is idiomatic and compact: literally 'that (it is) said there is no one [with] an undershirt' — i.e., a conventional/elliptical way to report an extreme state (the exact nuance is idiomatic and can be read as 'so ... that one would say no one had an undershirt').
segments.s166-g183: فرود آمد is a compound past 'he alighted / came down / dismounted' (فرود as noun/verb 'descent' with آمد 'came'). از باره literally 'from the load/burden' (بارة can mean 'load' or 'case' in older usage) indicates the thing he dismounted from; بگشاد دست is a verbless sequence where بگشاد (past participial/verb 'opened/loosened') applies to دست 'hand' — i.e. 'he loosened/spread his hand(s)'. The sequence shows Classical Persian compact coordination: dismount + free the hand.
segments.s166-g184: پر از خنده is an adjectival phrase 'full of laughter' (پر = 'full (of)', از connects the filler), functioning as a circumstantial modifier of the following action. بر تخت زرین نشست is straightforward: بر = 'on/upon', تخت زرین = 'the golden throne' (noun + adjective), نشست is the past 'sat down' (3rd person). The whole phrase means '(he) sat upon the golden throne, full of laughter.'
segments.s166-g329: ازان پس is a fixed classical sequence meaning “from/after that” (از + آن + پس). نکوهش نباید به کس is an impersonal negative construction: نباید (should not / ought not) governs the infinitival/noun phrase نکوهش ‘reproach’, with به کس marking the indirect object/target (“to/against anyone”). Word order is poetic (prepositional phrase after the negated predicate chunk).
segments.s166-g330: مکافات بد جز بدی نیست بس is an assertive nominal clause: مکافات بد ‘retribution/punishment for evil’ is the subject, followed by a restrictive/exceptive clause جز بدی نیست ‘there is nothing except evil’. بس at the end is emphatic (‘enough’ / ‘that suffices’). The tense/copula is expressed by نیست (is not), so the phrase denies any recompense other than evil.
segments.s166-g331: چنین گفت is a past-tense reporting formula ‘thus said’. گرسیوز is the speaker’s name and کینه‌جوی is an epithet placed after the name in apposition (‘the vengeful’). The whole segment is a reporting clause that introduces direct speech.
segments.s166-g332: که here functions as the complementizer introducing the content of what was said; ای شاه is a vocative address (‘O king’). بینادل is a rare/uncertain epithet (textual/lexical uncertainty — treat it as an epithet in the vocative), and راست‌گوی (‘truthful, frank’) is another epithet joined by و. The segment is therefore direct address introduced by that complementizer.
segments.s166-g333: This is a nominal/elliptical clause headed by the name سیاوش followed by بران, an archaic/poetic locative/adverbial particle meaning roughly ‘upon that’ / ‘thereon’ (or ‘as for him’). The following nouns آلت و فر و برز are a coordinate list (instruments/arms, royal glory, exaltation). No finite verb appears here—the clause is a poetic noun phrase describing what pertains to Siyâvash.
segments.s166-g334: بدان is a compact classical form (ب + آن) functioning deictically/relationally ‘to/for those’ or ‘to that’; it connects the previous list to the following specific items. ایزدی qualifies شاخ as ‘divine horn’. آن is the demonstrative ‘that’ pointing to the specific تیغ and گرز (sword and mace). The whole segment names the concrete divine weapons associated with the preceding abstract qualities.
segments.s166-g335: بیاید is a classical/dubitative/future-oriented form of بیامدن meaning ‘he will come’ / ‘may come’. It is followed by the prepositional phrase به درگاه تو (‘to/into your presence/royal court’) and the comitative با سپاه (‘with an army’). Word order places the verb before the place/comitative phrases, which is common in poetry.
segments.s166-g336: شود is the present/future copula/auxiliary ‘will be / will come to pass’. The doubled بر ... بر sequence marks two prepositional relations: شود بر تو (’will happen to you / upon you’) and بر تیره خورشید و ماه (’a falling-on / a doom of the sun and moon’). تیره qualifies خورشید و ماه as dark/ominous; the phrase evokes a calamitous eclipse or dire omen involving sun and moon.
segments.s166-g401: که is a subordinating/complementizer 'that' linking this clause to the previous line. The core clause here is 'هر باد را بست باید' = 'every wind must be stopped'; هر modifies باد ('every wind'), را is the definite/object marker attached to باد, and باید is the modal 'must/ought to' that scopes over the verb بست (past-stem used here with a deontic meaning 'be stopped/closed'). میان at the end of this segment is a prepositional/adverbial element ('between / in the middle') that connects to the following infinitive phrase.
segments.s166-g402: تهی کردن is an infinitive construction: تهی (empty) + کردن ('to make / to empty') = 'to empty'. آن جایگاه کیان is the direct object of that infinitive: آن 'that' + جایگاه 'place/seat' + کیان 'of the Kians/kings' (possessive relation). Grammatically the infinitive phrase gives the means/purpose by which the preceding clause is to be satisfied (i.e. stop every wind by emptying that royal seat).
segments.s166-g403: Simple past clause: فرستاده (subject 'the messenger') + نزد سیاوش ('in the presence of / at the side of Siyavash') + رسید (past 'arrived/reached'). نزد here marks location/'in the presence of' rather than the modern locative postposition کنار.
segments.s166-g404: Two short coordinated actions performed by the same subject (the messenger). زمین را ببوسید = 'he kissed the ground' (زمین + object marker را + past verb ببوسید). کاو را بدید = 'he saw him' with کاو used as an archaic/pronoun-like form meaning 'him/that one' (here the addressee), followed by را object marker and بدید 'saw' (past).
segments.s166-g405: چو functions temporally here as 'when'. پیغام گرسیوز او را بگفت = 'Garsivaz's message told him' / 'when Garsivaz delivered the message to him': پیغام 'message', گرسیوز (proper name), او را object 'to him', and بگفت (past) 'told/delivered'. Word order is typical older Persian with object marked by را before the verb.
segments.s166-g406: سیاوش غمی گشت = 'Siyavash became (filled with) grief': غمی is a substantive meaning 'grief' and گشت is the past of گشتن used as a copular/becoming verb 'became'. و اندر نهفت = 'and he hid it within' with اندر 'in/within' and نهفت (past participle/verb) meaning 'hid/concealed' (i.e., concealed it inwardly).
segments.s166-g407: Compound adjectival/verb phrase: پراندیشه 'full of thought / pensive' modifies the subject, then بنشست is the past 'he sat down'. بیدار دیر = 'awake for a long time' where بیدار 'awake' and دیر 'long (of time)' form a temporal phrase describing duration.
segments.s166-g408: همی here is an iterative/adverbial 'continually / repeatedly'. گفت would normally be the past of 'to say', but the provided sense list does not include that meaning (so marked below as not matched); the phrase رازیست این را به زیر is a small clause: رازیست 'it is secret' (رازی + copular -است), این را 'this (thing)' with را as object marker, and به زیر 'under/beneath' implying 'keep it beneath / keep it secret'. The whole segment means he kept saying repeatedly that this matter was secret and should be kept under cover.
segments.s166-g521: ندانی is the negative present form ('you do not know') with همی as a habitual/continuous adverbial particle ('ever/indeed'); چاره is the noun 'remedy/solution'. از + مهر is a prepositional phrase 'from/of love' and باز here functions adjectivally as 'free/away' (i.e., 'free from love'). Word order is topic-comment: the verb phrase ندانی همی introduces the comment about چاره.
segments.s166-g522: بباید is a modal present/prescriptive form 'one must/it is necessary'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...'. بخت بد آید is a standard predication ('bad fortune comes'); آید is present/future‑oriented 'comes/happens' and فراز is a locative/adverbial 'from on high / from above' placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s166-g523: همی is the habitual adverb 'continually/constantly'. مر is the clitic/weak pronoun 'me' and ترا is the 2nd‑person object clitic (ت) plus the object marker را written separately — together indicating 'you' as a definite object; بند و تنبل are coordinated nouns 'bond/fetter and laziness' that function as the objects/characterizations in relation to فروخت. فروخت is 3rd‑person past 'sold' with an omitted subject. The sequence shows objects and descriptors placed before the verb, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s166-g524: به introduces an indirect object or beneficiary 'to/for'; اورند is a proper name (or place) receiving the action. چشم خرد ('the eye of wisdom') is a genitival compound where خرد qualifies چشم; را is the definite object marker attaching to that whole phrase. بدوخت is past 'sewed' (here used metaphorically, 'sewed up/closed'), so the clause literally says 'he sewed up the eye of wisdom (to/for Orund)'.
segments.s166-g525: نخست is a sentence‑initial adverb 'first/at first'. آنک is a relative/demonstrative 'that/who' introducing the agent clause. داماد is the predicate noun 'son‑in‑law/groom' and کردت is the past tense verb کرد plus the 2nd‑person object suffix ت ('made you'); به دام is a prepositional phrase 'into a trap/snare' indicating the result/location. The verb+suffix construction shows classical Persian object clitics attached to verbs.
segments.s166-g526: بخیره is an adverb 'safely/fortunately' placed before the verb شدی 'you became'. زان ('from that' / 'because of that') links the following noun سخن ('word/speech/news') as the cause; شادکام is a predicative adjective 'happy/fortunate'. The clause therefore means 'you became safely/fortunate because of that word.'
segments.s166-g527: و دیگر is a connective 'and then/afterwards'. کت is the noun 'coat/cloak' and از خویشتن ('from himself') is a prepositional phrase marking separation; دور کرد is a causative/perfective construction 'caused (it) to be distant' = 'removed/took off'. The verb کرد is 3rd‑person past with the object expressed earlier.
segments.s166-g528: به روی بزرگان is a locative/face phrase 'in front of/on the occasion of the great ones' (روی used idiomatically as 'in the presence of'). یکی is an indefinite numeral/qualifier 'a certain/one' and سور is 'feast/banquet'; کرد again is 3rd‑person past 'made/did' — together 'he made a feast for the nobles.'
segments.s166-g585: که introduces a subordinate/purpose clause ('so that' / 'that'); او is the third-person subject pronoun; بازگردد is a third-person singular present/subjunctive (optative) of بازگشت/بازگرداندن used here to express 'may he return' or 'he should return'; سوی راستی is a prepositional phrase ('toward uprightness') with سوی before the noun راستی.
segments.s166-g586: شود is a third-person singular present/become (used jussively here: 'let it be'); دور ازو is an adjectival phrase 'far from him' where ازو is the fused preposition + pronoun ('from him'); کژی و کاستی are coordinated nouns ('crookedness and deficiency') that are the semantic subject of شود (i.e. 'may crookedness and deficiency be far from him').
segments.s166-g587: وگر is the conditional 'if'; بینم is first-person singular present/subjunctive of دیدن used with the conditional ('if I see'); اندر سرش literally 'in his head' but idiomatically 'in his mind' (سر + pronominal clitic ش = 'his'); هیچ here functions as an emphatic/indefinite ('any' / 'the slightest') modifying تاب, and تاب is used metaphorically ('glimmer/trace') — the noun phrase 'هیچ تاب' means 'any slightest sign'.
segments.s166-g588: هیونی is an object noun 'a beast/savage creature' (here accusative object placed before the verb for poetic effect); فرستم is first-person singular present ('I send' / 'I will send'), used to state a future action; هم is an adverbial emphasis ('also/even'); اندر شتاب is a prepositional adverbial phrase 'in haste' specifying manner. Word order (object before verb, adverbial after) is typical of the poem's elevated style.
segments.s166-g589: تو is the second-person subject 'you'; زان سان is a fixed phrase 'in that way' (زان = 'from/that', سان = 'like/as'); که introduces a relative clause 'which/that' qualifying سان; باید expresses necessity ('ought/must'); به زودی is an adverbial phrase 'quickly/soon' and بساز is a second-person singular imperative/prescriptive verb 'do/make/accomplish' addressing the addressee.
segments.s166-g590: مکن is the negative imperative 'do not'; کار بر خویشتن is a prepositional noun phrase 'a task/affair upon yourself' (کار = 'affair/work', خویشتن = 'yourself'); بر دراز ('بر' + دراز) means 'long'/'for a long time' — together the clause warns 'do not make the matter long upon yourself' i.e. 'do not prolong the task.' The negator precedes the whole object phrase.
segments.s166-g591: برون ران is an imperative verbal phrase 'drive/send forth' (برون = 'out/forth'); از ایدر is the source 'from here' (ایدر = archaic 'here'); به هر کشوری is the destination 'to every country' with به introducing the dative; the verb meaning 'drive/send' is imperative and the object is stylistically omitted or understood. Note: ران here functions as the verb 'drive' (imperative of راندن) in the poem's language.
segments.s166-g592: بهر is a preposition meaning 'for / for the sake of'; نامداری 'renown' and هر مهتری 'and every preeminence/distinction' are its objects — a purposive phrase explaining the goal of sending him to other lands. The coordination with و links the two abstract goals.
segments.s166-g193: یکی is the indefinite 'a certain / someone' placed before the noun; نامه is the object 'letter'; بنوشت is an archaic past form meaning 'wrote' (older/poetic prefix بـ before the verb). نزدیک شاه here marks the addressee/location 'near the king' — i.e. 'to the king' — with the adjective-like preposition following the verb phrase.
segments.s166-g194: پر از is the common construction 'full of' (پر + از + noun). لابه in this poetic context functions as 'entreaty / lamentation' (an older meaning), followed by پرسش 'questions' and نیکخواه 'good wishes / well-wishing' — the coordinating و links the list of contents.
segments.s166-g195: ازان پس is a temporal discourse phrase 'after that / thereafter'. مراو را is a definite/marked noun phrase (مراو is obscure here — likely a name or old form — with the object marker را attached). بسی هدیه داد follows Persian order where quantifier/adjective + object precedes the verb: 'gave many / ample gifts.'
segments.s166-g196: برفتند is the past plural 'they departed / went away'. زان شهر آباد شاد is a prepositional phrase 'from that city, prosperous and joyful' where زان = 'from that/thereupon', شهر = 'city', and the adjectives آباد and شاد follow to describe it.
segments.s166-g197: به رهشان is locative: به + ره (way) with the possessive suffix شان 'their' → 'on their way'. سخن رفت is an idiomatic past construction meaning 'a talk took place' (literally 'speech went'); یک با دگر is an idiom 'one with the other' = 'between them / with each other.'
segments.s166-g198: ازان introduces the topic 'about/from that'; پرهنر is an epithet 'learned / cultured' qualifying شاه 'the king'. آن بوم و بر is a fixed poetic pair 'that homeland and its region/territory' (بوم و بر = homeland and land), with و joining the two elements.
segments.s166-g199: چنین گفت is the formulaic 'thus said'; گرسیوز is the speaker's name; کینه جوی is a postpositive epithet meaning 'hatred-seeking / vindictive', describing Garsivaz's disposition.
segments.s166-g200: که introduces the reported clause ('that...'). مارا is the clitic/object 'us' used as experiencer; ز ایران here gives the cause or relation 'because of / from Iran'. بد آمد is an impersonal/experiential phrase 'it would (seem) bad / be unfavorable', followed by بروی (infinitival/verb sense 'to go') — the whole clause expresses that 'it would be bad for us to go with respect to Iran' (the exact nuance is idiomatic and a little ambiguous in the poetry).
segments.s166-g361: سبکسار مردم is an appositive phrase meaning a light‑minded or frivolous person of the people; here سبکسار functions as a characterising word applied to مردم. نه والا بود places the negation نه before the predicate والا (noble) + past copula بود, so the clause says he "was not noble." Word order is typical poetic Persian: noun/epithet followed by the negated predicate.
segments.s166-g362: و گرچه begins a concessive clause ('and although'). به تن is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in respect to the body / in build' (به + تن). سروبالا is a poetic compound adjective ('cypress‑tall', 'tall/upright') modifying the omitted subject (he), and بود is the past copula 'was.' The clause therefore concedes physical tallness despite the lack of nobility.
segments.s166-g363: برفتند (they went/left) is the past plural verb; پیچان is an adverbial/participial modifier meaning 'twistingly' or 'by winding paths' that qualifies برفتند. و links the following noun phrase لب پر سخن, which literally is 'lip(s) full of speech' and functions adverbially ('with lips full of words'). Note the noun + پر ('full') word order: لب + پر + سخن.
segments.s166-g364: پر از کین is a conventional construction 'full of hatred' (پر + از + noun). دل is the affected noun ('heart') and stands before the causal prepositional phrase از روزگار کهن, where the second از expresses cause/agency ('because of' or 'from') and کهن qualifies روزگار as 'ancient/old times.' The whole phrase reads 'a heart full of hatred because of the ancient times.'
segments.s166-g365: بر شاه رفتی is literal 'you went/took yourself to the king' (بر = toward/to + شاه = king; رفتی = second‑person past). زمان تا زمان is a reduplicative adverbial phrase meaning 'time to time' or 'from time to time' and modifies the verb, so the clause says 'you repeatedly went to the king.'
segments.s166-g366: بداندیشه گرسیوز بدگمان is a nominal phrase listing epithets: بداندیشه ('ill‑intentioned' or 'malicious') and بدگمان ('suspicious') both modify the name گرسیوز (Garsivaz). There is no copula—the adjectives are in apposition to the name, characterising him.
segments.s166-g367: ز هرگونه رنگ اندرآمیختی is an instrumental/causal clause: ز (by/from) + هرگونه رنگ ('every kind of color') followed by اندرآمیختی (second‑person past 'you mixed in' or 'you blended together'). The construction expresses means: 'by blending all sorts of colors.'
segments.s166-g368: دل شاه ترکان برانگیختی lists the affected nouns before the verb: دل شاه ('the king's heart') and ترکان ('the Turks') are the objects of برانگیختی ('you aroused/roused/provoked' — second‑person past). Persian often omits the object marker را and places multiple objects in sequence before the verb in poetry.
segments.s166-g545: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' placed before the noun phrase to modify the whole action. دل is the noun 'heart' (the thing given) and بدادی is an older poetic past form meaning 'you gave' (2nd person singular past); the verb follows the object here (object before verb), and به گفتار او is a prepositional phrase ('to his speech/words') specifying the recipient/target of that giving.
segments.s166-g546: همی is an iterative adverb ('kept/continually') that marks ongoing or habitual action. بگشتی here is a 2nd-person verb form meaning roughly 'you turned/wandered' (the verb and adverb combine to mean 'you kept turning'). گرد is a postposition/adverb meaning 'around/about' and teams with تیمار او ('his care' = the object/concern) to yield 'you kept turning around his care.'
segments.s166-g547: درختی is an indefinite noun 'a tree' (poetic word order allows it before the adjective), بد is the adjective 'bad/evil' qualifying it, and این ('this') points to that tree. برنشانده is a past participle from نشاندن/نشاندن-like compound (literary) meaning 'set/placed/ planted', and به دست ('by hand') indicates the instrument/agent — together: 'this bad tree planted by hand.'
segments.s166-g548: کجا is an interrogative adverb 'where' (here rhetorical), and بار او is 'his burden/load' (a nominal phrase). The following nominal sequence 'زهر و بیخش کبست' is a verbless descriptive clause: زهر 'poison' joined by و to بیخش ('root/essence' in the lemma) with کبست an obscure/archaic word acting as a predicate or qualifier. The line therefore poses a rhetorical question about the tree's produce/quality and then names it (with some words archaic or uncertain).
segments.s166-g549: همی here is the iterative adverb 'kept/continually' and governs the following verb; گفت is used as the act of speaking (in the corpus entry it's listed as 'conversation' but here functions as the verb 'said/kept speaking'). The clause is coordinated with و and describes مژگان 'eyelashes' modified by the predicative construction 'پر از آب' = 'full of water' with زرد ('yellow' or 'pale') qualifying that water.
segments.s166-g550: This clause parallels the previous one: پر ('full (of)') is used predicatively before the complement (a common poetic inversion: 'پر افسون دل' = 'heart full of charm'). افسون is a noun 'charm/enchantment' modifying دل 'heart.' The coordination و links to لب 'lips' which are likewise described as 'پر از باد سرد' — 'full of cold wind' (باد = wind, سرد = cold), producing a series of parallel predications.
segments.s166-g551: سیاوش is the proper name (subject). نگه کرد is a light-verb construction meaning 'cast/kept a gaze' or simply 'looked' (نگه 'gaze' + کرد 'did' = 'he looked/kept watch'). خیره is a participial adjective 'staring/astounded' modifying the action. بدوی in this line is an archaic/poetic form whose exact sense is not clear from the provided lemma list (it likely functions as a directional/dative element 'at you' or similar), so its precise sense is uncertain.
segments.s166-g552: ز is the poetic preposition 'from,' and دیده 'eye' names the source. نهاده is the past participle/verb 'placed/let fall' (here idiomatically 'let fall from his eyes' = 'shed'). به رخ 'onto the face/cheek' uses به as a directional preposition and رخ 'face' as the target; بر دو جوی literally 'upon two streams' describes the manner/location (i.e. tears running down in two streams). The whole phrase describes tears falling from the eyes onto the face in two rivulets.
segments.s166-g81: This is an inverted, somewhat archaic word order for the idiom دست به کار زدن (‘to set one’s hand to a task’). سیاوش is the actor; به بر زد functions with زد (past of زدن, 'to strike/set') and the prefixed به/بر combine with دست and کار to mean 'set about the task' — literally ‘Siyavash struck (began) with his hand at that work.’
segments.s166-g82: Two coordinated past actions. به زین اندر آمد is a poetic way of saying ‘he came into the saddle’ (i.e. mounted), where اندر آمد = 'came in/into'; ز تخت نشست follows and literally reads 'from the throne he sat' with ز = 'from' (poetic) and نشست = 'sat / seat' — here best understood as 'left the throne / took his leave of the throne' in context.
segments.s166-g83: زره را is the definite object (را marks it); به هم = 'together' and بر ببستند is past plural of بستن with the prefix بر (here 'they bound/fastened on'). پنج at the end gives the number (five layers) and is placed postpositively for emphasis — 'they bound the armor together (in) five (pieces/layers).'
segments.s166-g84: که introduces an explanatory/cause clause ('for' / 'because'). از یک زره تن is an ablative phrase 'from/with a single armor (to the body)'; رسیدی here is used in the sense 'befell / would have happened to you' (تصادف/واقعه), and به رنج = 'into suffering.' The whole clause expresses a counterfactual consequence: had there been only one cuirass, suffering would have befallen your body.
segments.s166-g85: نهادند is the past plural of نهادن 'to place/set'; here it governs an implied object (the armed men/armor) and is followed by بر 'on' and خط آوردگاه 'the battle-line/battlefield,' i.e. 'they placed (them) on the battle-line.'
segments.s166-g86: نظاره is a noun 'gaze/observation' used here as a verbal idea ('they looked/observed'); برو is an archaic/elliptical element (literally 'go' but here functioning as an exhortative/attentional particle akin to 'behold' or 'look'), followed by بر ز هر سو سپاه = 'upon/from every side, the host.' The clause means they looked round and saw armies on every side.
segments.s166-g87: A straightforward noun phrase: سیاوش is the subject, یکی is the numeral 'one' modifying نیزه 'spear,' and شاهوار is an adjective 'regal/lofty' placed after the noun in poetic word order: 'Siyavash (had) one lofty spear.'
segments.s166-g88: کجا introduces a question or rhetorical 'whence/where'; داشتی is the 2nd-person past/imperfect of داشتن 'you had / did you have'; از پدر is 'from (your) father' and یادگار is 'a keepsake/memento.' Together: 'From where did you have it, a memento from your father?' (rhetorical about the spear's provenance).
segments.s166-g17: همه شهر و برزن is a coordinated noun phrase ('all the city and the squares/plazas') serving as the scene/set of people. یکایک is an adverb placed after the noun phrase meaning 'one by one' or 'each in turn'. بدوی is a poetic/archaic verbal form (from the root بدو/دویدن) meaning 'rushed' or 'ran' and here qualifies the action of the subject; it appears in a condensed poetic form rather than modern colloquial morphology.
segments.s166-g18: نمود is the past form of نمودن and functions here as the main/predicative verb ('appeared/did/showed') or as a light verb that completes the scene set by the preceding verbal phrase. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. سوی is a preposition meaning 'toward'. کاخ is the noun object 'palace'. بنهاد is the past of نهادن ('set/placed') used with روی ('face') to form the idiom 'بنهاد روی' = 'set/turned (their) face', so the clause literally says 'set their face toward the palace.' Note the classical SOV tendencies and poetic compactness.
segments.s166-g19: هم آنگه is a temporal/discourse phrase ('then' / 'at that moment' / 'also then') setting time. به نزد سیاوش is a directional/locative phrase meaning 'to the presence of Siavash' (نزد + personal name = 'before Siavash' / 'to Siavash'). چو is a simile marker 'like/as', and باد is the image 'wind'—together 'چو باد' = 'like the wind', modifying the coming action.
segments.s166-g20: سواری is the subject 'a rider/horseman'. بیامد is the simple past 'came'. ورا is the contracted prepositional pronoun 'to him' (ورا = به + او), marking the indirect object. مژده is the direct object 'good news' and داد is the past of دادن 'gave', so مژده داد = 'gave the good news.' Word order follows classical Persian allowance for subject–verb–object with clitics.
segments.s166-g21: که introduces a subordinate/relative clause here ('that'). از marks origin/possession 'from/of'. دختر پهلوان سپاه is a genitive chain: daughter (دختر) of the champion (پهلوان) of the army (سپاه) — Persian links possessors right-to-left, so سپاه modifies پهلوان and that phrase modifies دختر.
segments.s166-g22: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain / one' introducing the noun. کودک آمد is a compact narrative phrase 'a child came' (in poetic Persian آمد can be used for 'was born' or 'appeared'). به مانند شاه is a simile 'in the likeness of the king' (به + مانند + noun), so the whole clause says a child was born/appeared who resembled the king.
segments.s166-g23: ورا is the dative/clitic 'to him' (marking the person being named). نام کردند is a light-noun + verb construction: نام (name) + کردند (they made/did) = 'they named (him)'. فرخ فرود is the proper name given; in the narrative it functions as the received personal name.
segments.s166-g24: به تیره شب آمد is a temporal clause 'when/the dark night came' (به + adjective + noun + آمد = 'when darkness fell'). چو is used here as a temporal 'when', introducing the following clause. پیران شنود literally 'the elders heard' — شنود is a classical-form verb 'hear' used in narrative here (contextually past 'they heard'); word order places the verb after the noun phrase in a typically poetic arrangement.
segments.s166-g105: یکی is an indefinite subject 'one / someone' (here 'one man'). در کمان راند is a compact past action: در + کمان (into the bow) + راند (past of راندن 'to drive/fit/loose'), so 'he fitted/drove (an arrow) into the bow.' The coordinating و links the next clause; بفشارد is a poetic/historical-form verb (ب‑ prefix + past stem + د) here meaning 'pressed' (see explanation), and ران is the direct object 'thigh' (i.e. he pressed his thigh to his horse).
segments.s166-g106: نظاره is a noun 'gaze / sight' used adverbially ('with his eyes / watching'). به introduces the target of the looking: گردش here means 'movement / turning / motion' and takes the following noun as its genitive: سپاهی گران = 'heavy / weighty soldiery' (گران qualifying سپاهی). The whole phrase = 'watching the movement of the heavy troops.'
segments.s166-g107: بران is a poetic deictic 'thereon / upon that' (linking to the previous action). چار چوبین = 'four wooden (pieces/planks)' (چار = four, چوبین = wooden). و ز آهن سپر = 'and (through) an iron shield' where ز is the poetic preposition 'from / through / of' and آهن سپر = 'iron shield.' Together it lists the things the arrow passes through.
segments.s166-g108: گذر کرد is a compound verbal expression: گذر (passage) + کرد (do/make) functioning as 'passed / pierced.' The following noun phrase پیکان آن نامور is the actor/thing that performed the passing: 'that renowned arrow.' Word order here is typical poetic Persian (verb phrase followed by the noun phrase it qualifies or vice versa); the meaning is 'the renowned arrow passed through / pierced.'
segments.s166-g109: بزد is a simple past 'he struck / smote.' هم 'also / too' emphasizes that this action accompanies the previous. بر آن گونه = 'in that manner / in the same way' (بر + آن + گونه). دو چوبه تیر = 'two wooden posts / two planks (چوبه) [with] an arrow (تیر)' — syntactically the phrase says he struck two posts with an arrow in that way.
segments.s166-g110: برو آفرین کرد is an idiomatic/performance clause: آفرین is the exclamation 'bravo' and کرد (did) reports that someone uttered it; برو here functions as an interjection/emphatic element prefixed to the exclamation (common in epic diction) rather than the literal 'go.' برنا و پیر ('the young and the old') is the plural group that voiced the acclamation; word order places the verb phrase before the nominative group in this poetic line.
segments.s166-g111: ازان ده = 'of those ten' (از + آن + ده); یکی here is numerical 'one' (not 'someone') and بی‌گذاره نماند is a negative-past statement: بی‌گذاره 'unrelenting / relentless' as a predicate and نماند (past of ماندن) 'did not remain / was not left.' The whole clause means 'not one of those ten remained unrelenting' (i.e. all were affected / none escaped).
segments.s166-g112: برو here again is an emphatic/exclamatory particle in this register. هر کسی = 'everyone / each person' is the subject; نام یزدان بخواند is a present-tense/subjunctive-style form meaning 'called the name of God' (خواند in narrative often renders 'he cried/sang/called'). The clause means 'each man called on the name of God.'
segments.s166-g513: بدو is the dative clitic phrase 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb; گفت is the past tense 'said' (narrative verb). گرسیوز‌ای مهربان is a vocative: گرسیوز (name) plus the vocative particle ای and the epithet مهربان ('kind'), addressed to Garsivaz.
segments.s166-g514: تو is the subject 'you'; او را is the object marked by the definite/object marker را. بدان سان ('be-dan sān') = 'in that manner/so' (بدان = archaic contraction of به + این = 'with/according to this'). که دیدی is a relative clause 'which/that you saw'. مدان is an archaic negative imperative/ prohibitive meaning 'do not consider / do not suppose' (م‑ negative + دان 'know/consider'), so the clause means 'do not consider him otherwise than as you saw him.'
segments.s166-g515: و links this clause to the previous line. دیگر here means 'again/another (time)'. بجایی (بجأیی) functions like 'at another time/place' (timely/appropriate moment). که introduces the subordinate clause. گردان سپهر = 'the turning/revolving sky/heavens' where گردان is a participial adjective 'revolving' modifying سپهر.
segments.s166-g516: شود is the third-person singular present/ narrative 'becomes' or 'comes to be'. تند = 'fierce/violent' (quality the sky acquires). چین 'wrinkle(s)' and اندرآرد is a causative verb 'brings in/causes'; به چهر = 'upon the face' (به + چهر). The clause means 'when the heavens become fierce and cause wrinkles upon the face.'
segments.s166-g517: خردمند دانا are two epithets used as a compound subject 'the wise and learned' (collective). نداند is the older negative present form 'does not know / cannot know' (نداند = ن + دانَد). فسون = 'enchantment/charm' is the object of نداند.
segments.s166-g518: که introduces a relative clause modifying فسون ('the enchantment that ...'). از چنبر او = 'out of/from his ring/noose' (چنبر = hoop, noose, or ring-like snare). سر آرد برون is a small clause with سر 'head' as the theme and آرد 'brings' + برون 'out' = 'brings the head out' (i.e. forces/causes the head to come out of the ring); word order is archaic but literal: 'that from his noose his head brings forth.'
segments.s166-g519: بدین here is an archaic compound equivalent to به + این 'with this' (ب‑ as a prepositional prefix + this). دانش = 'knowledge'. و این دل هوشمند = 'and this intelligent/discerning heart' (دل = heart/mind, هوشمند = clever/knowing). The phrase is an instrumental/qualifying list introduced by 'بدین' ('by/with such ...').
segments.s166-g520: The second parallel phrase continues the instruments/epithets: بدین again 'with this'; سرو بالا is a figurative comparison 'tall cypress' (سرو = cypress, metaphor for tall/beautiful stature; بالا = high/tall). و رای بلند = 'and lofty counsel' (رای = opinion/plan/counsel, بلند = lofty/high). Grammatically these are coordinated noun phrases describing the same person/qualities.
segments.s166-g337: This is a negative copular clause. سیاوش is the subject; نه آنست is a fixed negated copula (نه + آنست, where ست is the archaic form of است). کش here functions as a verbal element meaning 'to draw/attract' and links to دیده شاه ('the king's eye') as its object/goal — literally 'that which draws the king's eye.' Word order is topical subject + negated predicate + relative/verbal element, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s166-g338: همی before the verb gives a habitual/continuous sense ('would/continually'). ز آسمان is a prepositional phrase 'from the sky/heavens.' برگذارد is the verbal predicate (here with the habitual nuance from همی) meaning roughly 'set down/take down' or 'pluck (down)'; کلاه is the direct object 'hat' (metaphorical for crown). Word order: adverb + prepositional phrase + verb + object, with a poetic inversion.
segments.s166-g339: فرنگیس را places the proper name with the object marker را up front for emphasis. هم here has the sense 'even.' ندانی is the present negative form 'you do not know' (second person singular); تو is an explicit subject pronoun adding emphasis; باز at the end is the adverb 'again' or 'still' modifying the following clause. Overall: 'You do not even know Farangis, and still...'.
segments.s166-g340: تو is the topical/emphatic subject. گویی here functions as 'you say/claim' (classical usage 'گویی' = 'you say / you seem to say'); since that exact sense was not listed in the provided options, it is marked below as not matching the given sense list. شدست is a contracted classical perfect ('شده است' = 'has become'); از جهان بی‌نیاز is the predicate phrase 'free of the world' or 'independent of worldly concerns.' Word order: subject + verb + prepositional phrase complement.
segments.s166-g341: سپاهت ('your army/host') is the topical object/subject of the clause; بدو is the prepositional object 'to him'; بازگردد is the verb 'return' in a jussive/habitual sense (poetically placed before the final particle); همه qualifies it as 'all'/'entire.' The clause reads as a wish or injunction: 'May your whole army return to him.'
segments.s166-g342: تو is emphatic 'you'; باشی is an optative/subjunctive form of 'to be' here best read as 'be/let (you) be' (imperative/jussive nuance). رمه‌گر is a compound meaning 'shepherd/herdsman.' نیاری is difficult in the listed sense set (the provided senses are semantic for 'یار' and do not fit); the phrase دمه 'moment/breath' is the time-unit object. The whole line contrasts the addressee's humble role with the inability to gain 'even a moment' — 'you, be a shepherd, you will not gain a moment.' Because نیاری does not match the given sense options, its sense below is marked -1.
segments.s166-g343: سپاهی 'a soldier/warrior' is the head noun; که introduces a relative clause 'who/that.' شاهی here means 'kingship' (not the coin or merely 'royal') and is the object seen; ببین د is the present/optative 'sees' or 'beholds'; چنوی (چنو/چنوی) means 'in such a way / thus / like that.' Structure: noun + relative + verb + manner phrase: 'a warrior who sees kingship thus.'
segments.s166-g344: بدان here is composed of the prefix ب (به 'to/for') attached to دان ('know/consider') producing an imperative/jussive sense 'consider/attend to that.' بخشش is 'grant/favor' (here a tangible 'gift' or 'grant'); رای is 'counsel/opinion' (advice); آن ماه‌روی is 'that moon‑faced (one)' — a poetic epithet meaning a beautiful (moon‑faced) person. The sequence is a list of things the warrior should take into account: 'consider that grant and counsel and that moon-faced [one].'
segments.s166-g481: مرا زین سخن ویژه اندوه تست is a clause in which مرا ('me') marks the experiencer/affected person (object/topic); زین سخن literally 'from this speech' gives the cause; ویژه اندوه 'a special sorrow' is a noun phrase predicative to the experiencer; تست here is an archaic/poetic 2nd-person agreement or copula linking the predicate to an implied 'thou' (so: '...is a special sorrow to me'). Word order places the experiencer before the cause and the predicate for emphasis.
segments.s166-g482: که بیدار دل بادی و تن درست is an explanatory clause introduced by که ('because/that') giving the reason. بیدار دل is an adjectival phrase 'alert/awake of heart'; بادی here is an epithet modifying the (implied) person and does not match the literal senses of 'windy' in the lexicon (poetic usage), while و links to تن درست 'and (a) sound/proper body' — درست is an adjective 'sound/proper' describing تن.
segments.s166-g483: تو تا آمدستی بدین بوم و بر is a temporal clause: تو 'you' + تا آمدستی ('when/since you came' — archaic 2nd-person past) followed by the destination phrase بدین بوم و بر ('to this abode/land and region'). The particle ب (prefix) plus دین (here part of the demonstrative 'بدین') and بوم form the locative; و بر expands the place to 'land and region.'
segments.s166-g484: کسی را نیامد بد از تو به سر is the main proposition with negation: کسی را 'anyone/no one' marked by را (object/topic); نیامد 'did not come' is the past negative verb; بد از تو به سر is an idiomatic sequence meaning 'bad (ill) from you upon the head' — i.e. 'no bad befell anyone because of you.' In Persian negative contexts کسی را ... نیامد functions to mean 'no one'.
segments.s166-g485: همه مردمی جستی و راستی: همه مردمی 'all the people' is the object/group; جستی is a 2nd-person past form of جستن here used in the sense 'you won/obtained/acquired (the people's support)' (poetic usage of 'جستن' for 'to gain'); و راستی places 'uprightness/truth' alongside the action as a coordinating noun indicating one of the qualities associated with the subject.
segments.s166-g486: جهانی به دانش بیاراستی is a result/extension: جهانی 'the world' (adjectival form used as the domain), به دانش 'with/by knowledge' marks the instrument, and بیاراستی is the verb in 2nd-person past meaning 'you adorned/arrayed' (poetic form of آراستن). The phrase thus says the speaker 'adorned the world with knowledge', i.e. improved or embellished it by learning.
segments.s166-g487: کنون خیره آهرمن دل گسل is a nominal phrase with stacked epithets: کنون 'now' sets time, خیره modifies آهرمن ('the astonished/astounded Ahriman'), and دل گسل is a compound epithet literally 'heart-breaker / heart-sunderer' applied to that subject. The order places the temporal adverb first, then the characterized subject with two linked epithets.
segments.s166-g488: ورا از تو کردست آزرده‌دل contains the verbal statement: ورا ('to him/onto him') is the affected person placed before the verb for emphasis; از تو 'because of you' gives the cause; کردست is an archaic perfect/preterite 'has done / has made' (3rd-person singular with -ست ending); آزرده‌دل is the resulting predicate/adjective 'sore‑hearted / grieved'.
segments.s166-g465: شنیدی is 2nd/3rd-person past/perfect (literally “you heard” or “have you heard”), used here to introduce a reported clause. که is the subordinating particle “that/that which” introducing the content of what was heard. با + proper name (با ایرج) marks accompaniment or the addressee (‘with Irāj’). کم precedes the noun سخن and functions adverbially/adjectivally as “few/scant,” so کم سخن = “few words” or “briefly.” Word order is typical poetic Persian: prepositional phrase + modifier + noun, with the subject of the subordinate clause left implicit (understood from context).
segments.s166-g466: به آغاز کینه is a locative/temporal phrase “at the beginning (of) hatred” (به + noun + genitive). چه is exclamatory/interrogative here (“what/how”) and introduces the following verb phrase. افگند is a past transitive verb “he cast/laid/caused to fall,” with its object بن (“root/foundation”) attached; classical/poetic Persian commonly omits an explicit subject, so the verb stands in 3rd singular. The object بن is the concrete noun “root/seed,” giving the metaphor ‘to plant the root (of hatred).’ The phrase shows poetic inversion (چه + verb + object).
segments.s166-g467: وزان is a connective adverb meaning “and from that” or “from that time/place onward.” جایگه is “place/position”; together وزان جایگه تا به افراسیاب sets a spatial/temporal extent: “and from that place up to Afrasiab.” تا + به + name marks the limit (“up to Afrasiab”). Word order places the extent phrase before the following predicate for poetic effect.
segments.s166-g468: شدست is an archaic/poetic perfect/past construction meaning “it became” or “it was made.” It governs the predicate nominal آتش ایران و توران چو آب: “the fire of Iran and Turan became like water.” آتش ایران و توران is a genitive phrase “the fire (i.e. war/strife) of Iran and Turan”; چو آب is a simile “like/as water.” The verb + predicate-nominal order reflects classical Persian narrative style.
segments.s166-g469: به یک جای is a prepositional phrase “into/at one place”; هرگز + negative verb (نیامیختند) gives “never.” نیامیختند is past negative plural of آمدن/آمیکتن in the sense “they did not mix/come together,” here meaning “they did not unite.” The verb is plural and the subject is understood from the narrative (third-person plural).
segments.s166-g470: ز is the poetic preposition “from.” پند و خرد (“advice and wisdom”) is a coordinated NP; هر دو literally “each two” functions idiomatically as “both.” بگریختند is the past plural “they fled/ran away.” The whole segment means “they fled from both counsel and wisdom,” with the verb agreeing in number and the preposition expressing source/avoidance.
segments.s166-g471: سپهدار ترکان is a nominal phrase “the commander (spahdār) of the Turks,” with the title preceding the noun it governs. ازان (“from that/of that”) is a demonstrative/relational adverb referring back to the previous matter. بترست is a past-singular verb (third person) meaning “feared” (intransitive here): subject سپهدار ترکان + ازان + بترست = “the Turkish commander feared that/it.”
segments.s166-g472: کنون is the temporal adverb “now.” گاو پیسه is a compact noun phrase where پیسه (small coin/pittance) modifies/qualifies the value implied for گاو (“a cow now worth a pittance”), a poetic way to state decline in value; Persian often omits an explicit copula here. به چرم اندرست uses به + noun + اندرست (archaic copular/locative “is within/among”) to indicate where/ how the pittance exists (“in leather” — i.e., in a leather pouch). Together the line says that now a cow is reckoned a mere small coin kept in leather, a terse idiom of impoverishment.
segments.s166-g241: دل شاه (literally 'heart [of] the king') is a genitive noun phrase meaning 'the king's heart.' زان here is a prepositional/adverbial 'from that / because of that' modifying کار ('that affair'). شد is the past form of شدن meaning 'became' and introduces the predicate دردمند 'sorrowful / pained' as the resulting state.
segments.s166-g242: پر از غم شد is an adjectival phrase 'became full of grief' (پر + از + noun) with شد as the copular verb. از روزگار گزند is a causal/agent-like phrase 'from/of the times/fate the harm' explaining the source of sorrow; the word order is idiomatic (reason after the predicate).
segments.s166-g243: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' with بدو a dative clitic/pronoun 'to him' placed before the verb (classical order). بر من here means 'in/on me' or 'within me'; ترا مهر خون is an older word order putting the object-pronoun ترا ('you') before مهر ('love/affection') and خون ('blood')—together: 'the love for you in [my] blood.' Note: the lexical entry given for گفت does not include the verb 'said', so its provided senses do not match this use.
segments.s166-g244: بجنبید here means 'was stirred / was roused' (a past/inchoative sense). و شد links the next clause 'and became'; مر ترا رهنمون is inverted word order meaning 'you became my guide' (مر = to/for me, ترا = you, رهنمون = guide) with شد as the copula 'became.'
segments.s166-g245: سه روز اندرین کار is a temporal + locative phrase 'for three days in this affair' (numeral + noun + demonstrative-locative). رای آوریم is first-person-plural hortative/future 'we shall bring/produce counsel' (آوریم here = 'bring forth; produce' used for 'decide/consider').
segments.s166-g246: سخنهای بهتر بجای آوریم continues the same verb ('we will bring') and states the object: 'we will bring better words instead / in their place.' بجای indicates substitution ('in place of / instead'), so the phrase means 'let us say/offer better words instead.'
segments.s166-g247: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; این رای گردد is a subordinate clause 'this counsel/plan is made/comes about' (گردد = becomes/is done). خرد را درست is the beginning of the main clause: خرد را is the indirect object 'to the wise' (literally 'to wisdom' used for 'the wise'), درست is an adverb/adjective 'correctly / plainly', and the verb بگویم (in the next segment) supplies 'I will tell.'
segments.s166-g248: بگویم introduces a content clause with که 'that' — here functioning as 'I will say / I will tell that ...' دران 'in that' (in it), چه 'what', بایدت is a modal construction 'it is necessary for you / you ought' with the clitic ت marking 'you', and جست is the classical verb 'to seek / to look for' placed at the end: 'I will tell (you/the wise) what you must seek in it.' Note: the listed senses for بگویم are not verb senses, so the correct verbal sense is not available in the provided list.
segments.s166-g201: This is a compact, somewhat inverted clause: 'یکی مرد را' ('a certain man' with the object marker را) is placed before the subject 'شاه' and verb. In classical/epic Persian the prefixal verb form 'بخواند' here functions as a 3rd-person past (he summoned/called). 'ز ایران' is a prepositional phrase 'from/of Iran' modifying the king (poetic order puts modifiers after the subject).
segments.s166-g202: The relative clause introduced by 'که' modifies that summoned man: 'که از ننگ ما را به خوی در نشاند'. 'از' = 'from/of', marking source ('from our shame'), and 'به' = 'to/into' introduces the goal. 'نشاند' is an archaic perfect/past 3rd‑person sense 'he set/sat (someone)'; the object 'ما را' ('us') is marked by را and receives the action. 'خوی' here means 'kinship' and the word order (goal after preposition) is typical of older Persian.
segments.s166-g203: 'دو شیر' is the numeral + noun 'two lions'. The adjective/epithet 'ژیان' follows the noun in this poetic line and gives a sense of vitality/might. 'چون' introduces the comparison 'like/as', followed by the two names 'دمور و گروی' (here acting as proper names or epithets and not taking their ordinary lexical senses).
segments.s166-g204: 'که' begins a relative clause modifying the two lions. 'بودند' is the past 3rd‑person plural 'they were'. The predicate 'گردان پرخاشجوی' is a noun phrase 'an aggressive troop/band' (گردان = troop, پرخاشجوی = quarrelsome/aggressive); classical Persian allows this predicate to follow the copula as here.
segments.s166-g205: 'چنین' (= thus) introduces the result. The adjectives 'زار و بیکار و خوار' ('wretched, idle, despised') are connected to the plural verb 'گشتند' ('they became') — in older Persian adjectives frequently follow the verb or are listed before it; here they form the predicate describing the subject (the two lions). 'گشتند' is past 3rd‑person plural.
segments.s166-g206: This is an instrumental/agent phrase introduced by 'به' (here meaning roughly 'by'/'with'). 'چنگال ناپاک' is a genitive/attributive sequence 'unclean claw(s)' and 'تن یک سوار' ('the body of a single rider') names the possessor/agent: literally 'by the unclean claws of the body of one rider.' Word order stacks the modifiers in typical poetic style.
segments.s166-g207: 'سرانجام' is an adverb 'finally/at last'. 'ازین' = 'از' + poetic 'ین' (from this) — 'بگذراند' is the classical 3rd‑person past 'he let pass' or 'he let go (the matter)', and 'سخن' is its object 'the speech/matter'. The verb-prefix form with 'ب' is a frequent feature of epic/older Persian.
segments.s166-g208: This is a negative evaluative sentence using the correlative 'نه ... نه ...' ('neither ... nor ...'). 'نه سر بینم' = 'I see no head' (where 'بینم' is 1st‑person present 'I see/ perceive'). 'این کار او را' ('to this deed of his') is the indirect object/goal inserted in the middle, and the final 'نه بن' completes the pair: 'nor (do I see) a root/foundation.' The structure expresses the speaker's dismissal of the matter as having neither head nor base.
segments.s166-g393: بدو is the clitic/adverbial 'to him' (to/unto), گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (past 3rd sg.) introducing reported speech, رو is an imperative 'go' (classical/poetic form of رفتن), با marks accompaniment 'with/to' before the addressee, سیاوش is the proper name 'Siavash', and بگوی is a poetic imperative/subjunctive form of گفتن ('say' / 'tell') forming the command 'go and tell Siavash'. The sequence is a compact imperative: 'He said to him: go, tell Siavash...'.
segments.s166-g394: که‌ای here introduces the vocative/exclamation 'که ای' = 'O' addressing the addressee; پاک زاده is a compound vocative 'pure/noble-born' (پاک adjective + زاده 'born/child of'), کی is the interrogative 'who' (here 'whom'), نام is 'name', and جوی is here the verbal/noun sense 'search/seek' (poetic: 'you seek'). Together this is the quoted question: 'O noble-born, whose name do you seek?'.
segments.s166-g395: به is the preposition used here as an oath-marker 'by', جان و سر are two nouns joined by و ('life and head') both governed by به, and شاه توران سپاه is a poetic inverted noun group meaning 'the army of the king of Turan' (in prose: سپاهِ شاهِ توران). The word order is elliptical/poetic but the structure is 'by the life and head of the Turanian king's host'.
segments.s166-g396: به again functions as 'by', فر is the special royal concept 'farr' or 'royal glory', و links the two oath-objects, به repeats 'by', دیهیم is an adjective/noun 'rural, rustic' used here as an attribute or quality, and کاووس شاه (name + title) specifies the king invoked. The phrase enumerates further things sworn by: 'and by the farr and the rustic quality of King Kavus.'
segments.s166-g397: که introduces the content clause 'that...', از بهر من is the idiom 'for my sake' (از بهر = for/because of), برنخیزی is a negative second-person form 'you do not / should not rise/get up' (classical poetic formation: بر‑ + نخیزی), ز is the poetic preposition 'from', and گاه means 'place' or 'time' (here 'place/seat' or 'from the place'). The clause means 'that you shall not rise from your place for my sake.'
segments.s166-g398: نه is the negative particle 'do not / nor', پیش من آیی literally 'you come before me' (پیش = before/in the presence of; آیی is the present/subjunctive of آمدن 'you come'), and پذیره به راه is an instruction 'receive (him) on the road'—پذیره here denotes 'reception/acceptance' used in an imperative/instructive sense, with به راه 'on the road'. The three parts form a sequence of prohibitions and an instruction: 'do not come before me; receive him on the road.'
segments.s166-g399: که again introduces the clause 'that...', تو is the subject 'you', زان فزونی literally 'from/for that superiority' (زان = from/that; فزونی = excess/superiority), and به فرهنگ و بخت marks the spheres in which the superiority holds ('in/with learning (فرهنگ) and fortune/fate (بخت)'). Grammatically this says 'that you, by that superiority, are outstanding in learning and fortune.'
segments.s166-g400: This segment continues the enumeration: به before each noun functions like 'in/by', فر is 'farr' (royal glory), و links to نژاد 'lineage', and then به تاج و به تخت continues the list 'and in crown and in throne'. The repeated به gives a rhythmic catalogue of honors/attributes in which the addressee excels.
segments.s166-g233: This is a conditional protasis introduced by اگر (if). تور را uses the object marker را after the proper name Tur, marking the target/direction of the feeling. دل نگشتی دژم is an archaic/poetic sequence: نگشتی is the past of گشتن (here 'turn/become') and دژم is an adjective 'sullen/grim' — literally 'the heart turned sullen (toward Tur)'. Word order puts the object (تور را) and the thing that changed (دل) before the verb phrase.
segments.s166-g234: This is the apodosis (result clause). ز گیتی (from/of the world) + به ایرج (toward Iraj) set the circumstantial frame. نکردی is the 2nd-person past negative of کردن ('you did not do'), and ستم is the noun 'injustice/oppression' — together 'you did not do injustice to Iraj in the world.' The prepositions are poetic (ز = از).
segments.s166-g235: This is a verbless descriptive clause: دو کشور (two realms) followed by apposition one/other: یکی آتش و دیگر آب — 'one (is) fire and the other (is) water.' Persian often omits the copula 'is' in such nominal descriptions.
segments.s166-g236: بدل ... گرفته شتاب: بدل here means 'in exchange' or 'as a reciprocal change'; گرفته is the perfect/participial form of گرفتن ('taken'); شتاب ('haste') is adverbial/nomic, giving the manner. The construction means they have quickly exchanged/mutually seized one another (i.e. 'have quickly exchanged places/effects'). The particle ز marks 'from/of the other.'
segments.s166-g237: تو خواهی ... آوری uses خواهی as a future/modal auxiliary ('you will') with the main verb آوری ('bring'). کشان is a present‑participle/adverbial ('dragging, pulling') modifying the action, and خیره (here 'astonished') modifies جفت ('pair'). The verb-final word order (جفت آوری) is normal in poetic Persian: subject + auxiliary + adverbial participle + object + main verb.
segments.s166-g238: همی before the verb marks continuous/habitual action ('will always / continually'). باد را is the definite object 'the wind'; در نهفت is a locative phrase in which نهفت ('hidden/concealed') is postpositive (typical in poetry). آوری repeats the verb 'bring/carry' — together: 'you will always bring the wind concealed inside.'
segments.s166-g239: Another conditional protasis: اگر introduces the clause. کردمی is an archaic/poetic past perfect 'I had done' (first person). بر تو = 'against you', این بد نهان = 'this evil (done) secretly' where نهان ('hidden/secretly') post-modifies the action. Overall: 'If I had secretly done this evil against you...'.
segments.s166-g240: مرا is the patient/object 'me' (in the manuscript the morpheme appears split as 'م' + 'را'); زشت نامی = 'an ugly/bad name/reputation' (زشت modifying نامی). بدی here functions as an archaic/poetic 2nd‑person resultive/conditional ('you would give/make [me]...'), and در جهان = 'in the world.' So the clause states the expected consequence within the conditional: 'you would have given me an ugly reputation in the world.'
segments.s166-g497: سپهبد is the subject. جزین (literally “this/except this”) here qualifies what was given; کرد is the past tense verb “made/gave.” ما را is the marked object (را marks ما as the direct object). امید is the noun (what was caused) — the whole phrase means “the spahbed made/gave us this hope.”
segments.s166-g498: که introduces a subordinate clause (the content of the hope: “that …”). آرد is a present/future‑flavored verb “brings/will bring.” شب is the grammatical subject of آرد, while بر من (بر = “toward/on”; من = “me”) marks the person affected. به‌روز سپید is the object/goal: “a bright day” (به‌روز = day/daylight, سپید = bright/white).
segments.s166-g499: گر is the conditional “if.” آزار is the noun “harm/ill‑will”; بودیش (بود + ish) expresses that such a feeling existed for him (“it was to him/he had it”). در دل = “in the heart,” and ز من = “because of/from me” (i.e. directed at me). Together: “If he had ill‑will in his heart toward me…”
segments.s166-g500: سرم is the possessed noun “my head” used idiomatically (object of the action). برنیفراختی is a negative past second‑person form (“you did not cast away/you did not raise/you did not expel”); here it means you did not expel or put me to shame. ز انجمن = “from the assembly/council” (ز = from, انجمن = assembly), completing the action’s source/relationship.
segments.s166-g501: ندادی is the past negative second person “you did not give.” به من marks the indirect object “to me.” The following nouns (کشور و تاج و گاه) are the first part of an enumerated list of what was not given: country/realm (کشور), crown/sovereignty (تاج), and گاه (here an archaic noun meaning “place/seat/position” — listed with the others).
segments.s166-g502: A continuation of the enumerated list: بر و بوم is a paired phrase (بر here functioning as part of the pair, with بوم “homeland/native soil”), followed by فرزند (child/offspring), گنج (treasure), and سپاه (army). The conjunction و links the list items; these are direct objects of ندادی understood from the previous segment.
segments.s166-g503: کنون is the temporal adverb “now.” با تو آیم uses the present/subjunctive form آیم (“I come”/“I will come/go”) with با تو “with you”; in narrative this present/subjunctive carries a future/intended meaning (“I will go with you”). به درگاه او marks the destination/place: “to his court/presence.”
segments.s166-g504: درخشان کنم is a light‑verb construction where درخشان (shining/bright) is the result and کنم (I make/do) is the verb: “I will make [it] shining.” The object is تیره‌گون ماه او — “his dark‑hued moon” (ماه here as a poetic image); word order places the result phrase before the verb, a common poetic inversion, and then the object that the speaker will cause to shine.
segments.s166-g289: 'اگر' introduces a conditional ('if'); 'گاه' here = 'at any time / ever' (temporal adverb modifying the condition); 'جویدگر' is an agent‑noun (from جویدن/جویدن in classical usage) meaning 'one who seeks' — 'a seeker'; 'انگشتری' is the object 'a ring'. Together they form 'if ever a seeker of a ring ...'.
segments.s166-g290: 'ازین' is از + این (here 'from this'); 'بوم و بر' is an idiomatic pairing meaning 'land and domain/home' (object phrase placed before the verb for emphasis); 'بگسلد' is a classical third‑person present/subjunctive/potential form 'he/she/it should break/rend' used after the conditional; 'داوری' is the thing being broken: 'judgment/justice'. Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect (locative/object before verb).
segments.s166-g291: 'بدو' = 'to him' (prepositional/indirect object); 'گفت' is the verb 'said' (past 3rd sg.) but the provided sense list did not include 'said' exactly; 'گرسیوز' is the speaker's name; 'ای شهریار' is a vocative address 'O king'. Word order is inverted: 'to him said Garsivaz — O king'.
segments.s166-g292: 'مگیر' is the negative imperative 'do not take/undertake/accept' addressed to the addressee; 'اینچنین' ('such') modifies 'کار' ('deed/affair'); 'پرمایه خوار' are adjectival modifiers: 'پرمایه' ('substantial/costly') and 'خوار' ('despised/disgraceful'), together describing the kind of deed to be avoided. The imperative governs the whole noun phrase that follows.
segments.s166-g293: 'از' = 'from'; 'ایدرگر' is a fused/archaic conditional locution ('if from here' / 'if so from here') used in classical Persian; 'او' is the third‑person subject 'he'; 'سوی ایران' = 'toward Iran' (سوی + proper name); 'شود' is the classical subjunctive/optative auxiliary meaning something like 'should go / will be' — here marking the potential action in the 'if' clause. Word order is archaic/poetic but transparent: 'If from here he toward Iran goes...'.
segments.s166-g294: 'بر و بوم' (inverted order here as 'بر و بوم') forms the idiom 'land and home/realm'; 'ما' is the possessor 'our'; 'پاک' is used adverbially/intensifyingly here 'completely/utterly'; 'ویران شود' = 'will/become ruined' with شود marking the resulting state. The phrase means 'our land and home will be utterly devastated.'
segments.s166-g295: 'هر آنگه که' = 'whenever / every time that'; 'بیگانه شد' = 'became a stranger / turned alien' (شد = became); 'خویش تو' = 'one of your own / your kin' — here the possessive is split into 'خوی' + enclitic 'ش' + 'تو' and functions to mean 'someone who was your own/near one becomes alien.' The clause sets the condition under which the following result obtains.
segments.s166-g296: 'بدانست' is a classical form meaning 'become/come to know / perceive' (here used as the resultant verb: 'then you will know' or 'you will learn'); 'راز' is the direct object 'secret'; 'کم و بیش' is a fixed adverbial phrase 'more or less / to a lesser or greater extent' modifying how fully the secret will be known; final 'تو' is the emphatic pronoun 'you' (the person who will learn). The clause therefore reads 'you will (more or less) know the secret.'
